Question 2 of 230

Data ID: 829510

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:50 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

In order to understand the chemical evolution of life, scientists have studied the production of amino acids under conditions that simulate those of primitive Earth.

Malic acid is thought to have formed via hydrolysis of a cyanohydrin intermediate (Scheme I).

Scheme I

Subsequent dehydration of malic acid results in the formation of fumaric acid (mp 286°C) and its cis isomer, maleic acid (mp 130°C).

Ammonia was thought to be a constituent of the early atmosphere, and it was proposed that it reacted with fumaric, maleic, and malic acids to afford a prebiological synthesis of aspartic acid, an amino acid (Scheme II).

Scheme II

In an attempt to verify this hypothesis, chemists tried to reproduce the synthesis of aspartic acid under simulated primitive Earth conditions.

An open flask containing malic acid was treated with a continuous ammonia feed, and the mixture was heated at 150°C for 1.5 h. The experiment was repeated with a mixture of fumaric and maleic acids. The results were analyzed by paper chromatography; the Rf values of the reaction mixture were compared to the Rf value of a standard sample of aspartic acid. (Note: Rf = distance moved by compound/distance moved by solvent front.)

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What assumption is being made if scientists conclude that aspartic acid was formed by the prebiological synthesis in the passage?

  • A.
    Aspartic acid is unstable at temperatures below 150°C.
  • B.
    All of the malic acid underwent the dehydration reaction to form fumaric/maleic acid.
  • C.
    Compound A and cyanide were available on primitive Earth.
  • D.
    The reaction between ammonia and fumaric acid was catalyzed by the presence of water.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Because the prebiotic experiment was performed at 150°C in order to form aspartic acid, it is contradictory that the researchers would expect this temperature to degrade at lower temperatures. In addition, aspartic acid is found in living organisms at 37°C.
  2. There is no requirement given that all of the malic acid dehydrate to fumaric/maleic acid, only that some does.
  3. Without Compound A and cyanide, no aspartic acid can form.
  4. There is no assumption in the passage that water acts as a catalyst in the reaction between ammonia and fumaric acid.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Separations and purifications.

Question 3 of 230

Data ID: 829530

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:51 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

In order to understand the chemical evolution of life, scientists have studied the production of amino acids under conditions that simulate those of primitive Earth.

Malic acid is thought to have formed via hydrolysis of a cyanohydrin intermediate (Scheme I).

Scheme I

Subsequent dehydration of malic acid results in the formation of fumaric acid (mp 286°C) and its cis isomer, maleic acid (mp 130°C).

Ammonia was thought to be a constituent of the early atmosphere, and it was proposed that it reacted with fumaric, maleic, and malic acids to afford a prebiological synthesis of aspartic acid, an amino acid (Scheme II).

Scheme II

In an attempt to verify this hypothesis, chemists tried to reproduce the synthesis of aspartic acid under simulated primitive Earth conditions.

An open flask containing malic acid was treated with a continuous ammonia feed, and the mixture was heated at 150°C for 1.5 h. The experiment was repeated with a mixture of fumaric and maleic acids. The results were analyzed by paper chromatography; the Rf values of the reaction mixture were compared to the Rf value of a standard sample of aspartic acid. (Note: Rf = distance moved by compound/distance moved by solvent front.)

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

According to the developed chromatography plate shown below, what is the approximate Rf value of aspartic acid?

  • A.
    0.20
  • B.
    0.50
  • C.
    5
  • D.
    10

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. The Rf of aspartic acid = (distance traveled by aspatic acid)/(distance to the solvent front) ​​
  2. This value is the Rf of the middle spot of the reaction mixture, not aspatic acid.
  3. This value is the distance traveled by the middle spot of the reaction mixture. It is not an Rf value.
  4. This value is the distance to the solvent front.

Question 4 of 230

Data ID: 829540

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:52 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

In order to understand the chemical evolution of life, scientists have studied the production of amino acids under conditions that simulate those of primitive Earth.

Malic acid is thought to have formed via hydrolysis of a cyanohydrin intermediate (Scheme I).

Scheme I

Subsequent dehydration of malic acid results in the formation of fumaric acid (mp 286°C) and its cis isomer, maleic acid (mp 130°C).

Ammonia was thought to be a constituent of the early atmosphere, and it was proposed that it reacted with fumaric, maleic, and malic acids to afford a prebiological synthesis of aspartic acid, an amino acid (Scheme II).

Scheme II

In an attempt to verify this hypothesis, chemists tried to reproduce the synthesis of aspartic acid under simulated primitive Earth conditions.

An open flask containing malic acid was treated with a continuous ammonia feed, and the mixture was heated at 150°C for 1.5 h. The experiment was repeated with a mixture of fumaric and maleic acids. The results were analyzed by paper chromatography; the Rf values of the reaction mixture were compared to the Rf value of a standard sample of aspartic acid. (Note: Rf = distance moved by compound/distance moved by solvent front.)

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following statements does NOT correctly describe the dehydration of malic acid to fumaric acid and maleic acid?

  • A.
    The reaction occurs most readily with tertiary alcohols.
  • B.
    The reaction involves the loss of a water molecule.
  • C.
    The reaction has a carbocation intermediate.
  • D.
    The reaction is stereospecific.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Tertiary alcohols readily dehydrate.
  2. Dehydration is loss of a water molecule by definition.
  3. During dehydration, a carbocation forms.
  4. Because of the carbocation intermediate in dehydration, the subsequent loss of a proton will lead to a mixture of cis- and trans-alkenes. Thus, dehydration is NOT stereospecific.

Question 5 of 230

Data ID: 829542

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:52 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

In order to understand the chemical evolution of life, scientists have studied the production of amino acids under conditions that simulate those of primitive Earth.

Malic acid is thought to have formed via hydrolysis of a cyanohydrin intermediate (Scheme I).

Scheme I

Subsequent dehydration of malic acid results in the formation of fumaric acid (mp 286°C) and its cis isomer, maleic acid (mp 130°C).

Ammonia was thought to be a constituent of the early atmosphere, and it was proposed that it reacted with fumaric, maleic, and malic acids to afford a prebiological synthesis of aspartic acid, an amino acid (Scheme II).

Scheme II

In an attempt to verify this hypothesis, chemists tried to reproduce the synthesis of aspartic acid under simulated primitive Earth conditions.

An open flask containing malic acid was treated with a continuous ammonia feed, and the mixture was heated at 150°C for 1.5 h. The experiment was repeated with a mixture of fumaric and maleic acids. The results were analyzed by paper chromatography; the Rf values of the reaction mixture were compared to the Rf value of a standard sample of aspartic acid. (Note: Rf = distance moved by compound/distance moved by solvent front.)

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What type of functional group is formed when aspartic acid reacts with another amino acid to form a peptide bond?

  • A.
    An amine group
  • B.
    An aldehyde group
  • C.
    An amide group
  • D.
    A carboxyl group

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. An amine is one of the functional groups that reacts to form a peptide bond, not the peptide bond itself.
  2. An aldehyde is RCHO, whereas the product is an amide, RCONHR'.
  3. The functional group that represents a peptide bond is called an amide group.
  4. A carboxyl group reacts with an amine to form a peptide bond.

Question 6 of 230

Data ID: 829570

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:53 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 6-9)

The nucleophilic alkyl substitution reactions are among the most thoroughly studied transformations in organic chemistry. A substitution by an SN2 reaction mechanism is shown in Figure 1 and a substitution by an SN1 mechanism is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1 SN2 Reaction mechanism

 

Figure 2 SN1 Reaction mechanism

 

The SN2 mechanism involves a direct displacement of a leaving group (L) by a nucleophile (N). The reaction results in an inversion of configuration at the carbon bearing the leaving group, because the incoming nucleophile attacks it from the side opposite the leaving group. The SN1 reaction mechanism involves an intermediate carbocation that is formed by loss of the leaving group. A nucleophile can react with the planar carbocation from either side, leading to the inversion or retention of the configuration at the site of the substitution.

The product distribution of two reactions is shown in figures 3 and 4.

Figure 3 SN2 Reaction of 1-pentanol

 

Figure 4 Reaction of 2-methyl-2-butanol

 

The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 3 increases as the concentration of alcohol, halide, or H+ increases. The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 4 increases as the concentration of alcohol or H+ increases. This reaction is independent of the halide concentration.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

If 2-pentanol replaces 1-pentanol in the reaction shown in Figure 3, the rate of substitution is less because:

  • A.
    the C–O bond in 2-pentanol is stronger than the C–O bond in 1-pentanol.
  • B.
    there is a competing elimination reaction that slows the rate of substitution.
  • C.
    there is more steric hindrance at the oxygen atom in 2-pentanol than in 1-pentanol, making protonation less likely.
  • D.
    there is more steric hindrance at the 2-position of 2-pentanol than at the 1-position of 1-pentanol.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This statement is false. For both heterolytic and homolytic bond breaking, the more substituted C–O bond is weaker. Relative C–O bond strength is not the relevant parameter for this question.
  2. Competing elimination does not slow the rate of substitution. Their rates are independent of each other.
  3. Protonation at OH is a fast, pre-equilibrium step, thus it has no effect on the reaction rate.
  4. At a secondary carbon, there is more crowding from the adjacent carbon chains, which makes nucleophilic substitution slower.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 7 of 230

Data ID: 829650

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:54 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 6-9)

The nucleophilic alkyl substitution reactions are among the most thoroughly studied transformations in organic chemistry. A substitution by an SN2 reaction mechanism is shown in Figure 1 and a substitution by an SN1 mechanism is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1 SN2 Reaction mechanism

 

Figure 2 SN1 Reaction mechanism

 

The SN2 mechanism involves a direct displacement of a leaving group (L) by a nucleophile (N). The reaction results in an inversion of configuration at the carbon bearing the leaving group, because the incoming nucleophile attacks it from the side opposite the leaving group. The SN1 reaction mechanism involves an intermediate carbocation that is formed by loss of the leaving group. A nucleophile can react with the planar carbocation from either side, leading to the inversion or retention of the configuration at the site of the substitution.

The product distribution of two reactions is shown in figures 3 and 4.

Figure 3 SN2 Reaction of 1-pentanol

 

Figure 4 Reaction of 2-methyl-2-butanol

 

The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 3 increases as the concentration of alcohol, halide, or H+ increases. The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 4 increases as the concentration of alcohol or H+ increases. This reaction is independent of the halide concentration.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

If Reaction 2 (Figure 4) is repeated with HCl and the compound shown below, which of the following compounds is NOT a direct product (without rearrangement)?

  • A.
  • B.
  • C.
  • D.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. The only way that this product can form is by rearrangement, a 1,2-hydride shift following the initial formation of a tertiary carbocation.
  2. This product forms directly from deprotonation after the formation of the tertiary carbocation.
  3. This product forms directly via chloride nucleophile covalently binding to the tertiary carbocation intermediate from below the plane of the carbocation.
  4. This product forms directly via chloride nucleophile covalently binding to the tertiary carbocation intermediate from above the plane of the carbocation.

Question 8 of 230

Data ID: 829656

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:54 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 6-9)

The nucleophilic alkyl substitution reactions are among the most thoroughly studied transformations in organic chemistry. A substitution by an SN2 reaction mechanism is shown in Figure 1 and a substitution by an SN1 mechanism is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1 SN2 Reaction mechanism

 

Figure 2 SN1 Reaction mechanism

 

The SN2 mechanism involves a direct displacement of a leaving group (L) by a nucleophile (N). The reaction results in an inversion of configuration at the carbon bearing the leaving group, because the incoming nucleophile attacks it from the side opposite the leaving group. The SN1 reaction mechanism involves an intermediate carbocation that is formed by loss of the leaving group. A nucleophile can react with the planar carbocation from either side, leading to the inversion or retention of the configuration at the site of the substitution.

The product distribution of two reactions is shown in figures 3 and 4.

Figure 3 SN2 Reaction of 1-pentanol

 

Figure 4 Reaction of 2-methyl-2-butanol

 

The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 3 increases as the concentration of alcohol, halide, or H+ increases. The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 4 increases as the concentration of alcohol or H+ increases. This reaction is independent of the halide concentration.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

If a solution containing the compounds shown in Figure 4, is injected into a gas-liquid chromatograph, the first peak observed in the gc trace is attributable to which compound?

  • A.
    2-Methyl-2-butanol
  • B.
    2-Methyl-2-butene
  • C.
    2-Chloro-2-methylbutane
  • D.
    2-Bromo-2-methylbutane

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. 2-Methyl-2-butanol is highly polar and makes hydrogen bonds, so it is the least volatile of the options.
  2. In gas-liquid chromatography, the first peak to emerge will be from the least polar, most volatile compound. 2-Methyl-2-butene is nonpolar and the most volatile because it only has London forces for intermolecular attraction.
  3. Unlike 2-methyl-2-butene, 2-chloro-2-methylbutene possesses dipole-dipole forces in addition to greater London forces, so it will not be the most volatile.
  4. Unlike 2-methyl-2-butene, 2-bromo-2-methylbutene possesses dipole-dipole forces in addition to greater London forces, so it will not be the most volatile.

Question 9 of 230

Data ID: 829667

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:55 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 6-9)

The nucleophilic alkyl substitution reactions are among the most thoroughly studied transformations in organic chemistry. A substitution by an SN2 reaction mechanism is shown in Figure 1 and a substitution by an SN1 mechanism is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1 SN2 Reaction mechanism

 

Figure 2 SN1 Reaction mechanism

 

The SN2 mechanism involves a direct displacement of a leaving group (L) by a nucleophile (N). The reaction results in an inversion of configuration at the carbon bearing the leaving group, because the incoming nucleophile attacks it from the side opposite the leaving group. The SN1 reaction mechanism involves an intermediate carbocation that is formed by loss of the leaving group. A nucleophile can react with the planar carbocation from either side, leading to the inversion or retention of the configuration at the site of the substitution.

The product distribution of two reactions is shown in figures 3 and 4.

Figure 3 SN2 Reaction of 1-pentanol

 

Figure 4 Reaction of 2-methyl-2-butanol

 

The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 3 increases as the concentration of alcohol, halide, or H+ increases. The rate of the reaction shown in Figure 4 increases as the concentration of alcohol or H+ increases. This reaction is independent of the halide concentration.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What is the product of the reaction of Compound 1 (shown below) with HBr by the pathway shown in Figure 3?

Compound 1

  • A.
    (R)-1-bromo-1-deuteriohexane
  • B.
    (S)-1-bromo-1-deuteriohexane
  • C.
    (S)-1-bromo-1-deuteriopentane
  • D.
    (R)-1-bromo-1-deuteriopentane

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. This product has both the wrong stereochemistry and the wrong number of carbon atoms.
  2. Although this product has correct stereochemistry, it has the wrong number of carbon atoms.
  3. Because this is an SN2 reaction, there will be inversion of configuration when bromide ion displaces water as a leaving group, changing the stereochemistry from R to S.
  4. Although this product has the correct number of carbons, the stereochemistry should be S, not R.

Question 10 of 230

Data ID: 829669

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:56 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Acetic acid and ethanol react to form an ester product as shown below.

In determining which reactant loses the –OH group, which of the following isotopic substitutions would be most useful?

  • A.
    Replace the acidic H of acetic acid with D.
  • B.
    Replace the alcoholic H of ethanol with D.
  • C.
    Replace the carbonyl oxygen of acetic acid with O-18.
  • D.
    Replace the hydroxyl oxygen of ethanol with O-18.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. In order to determine which reactant loses the –OH group, an oxygen atom on ethanol must be radiolabeled.
  2. In order to determine which reactant loses the –OH group, an oxygen atom on ethanol must be radiolabeled. Replacement the alcoholic H by D will not provide this information because of rapid H/D exchange between acetic acid and ethanol.
  3. Replacing the carbonyl oxygen in acetic acid with O-18 will yield a mixture of O-18-labeled water and ester, further confusing the situation. There may be a mechanism of O-18 exchange with ethanol via O-18 labeled water that would obscure which reactant actually loses the –OH during ester formation.
  4. Labeling the oxygen in ethanol with O-18 definitely determines which –OH is lost because 100% of the O-18 in radiolabeled ethanol will be retained in the ester, not the water. Thus, only acetic acid supplies –OH that is lost to form water.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 11 of 230

Data ID: 829675

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:56 PM

Question + Answer Choices

A person, whose eye has a lens-to-retina distance of 2.0 cm, can only clearly see objects that are closer than 1.0 m away. What is the strength S of the person’s eye lens? (Note: Use the thin lens formula ​​.)

  • A.
    –50 D
  • B.
    –10 D
  • C.
    51 D
  • D.
    55 D

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The negative sign of the lens strength is indicative of a diverging lens, whereas the eye lens is a converging lens.
  2. The negative sign of the lens strength is indicative of a diverging lens, whereas the eye lens is a converging lens.
  3. According to the thin lens formula, the strength of the eye lens is equal to the inverse of the focal length of the eye lens. Its numerical value is given by (1 m)−1 + (0.02 m)−1 = 1 D + 50 D = 51
  4. This answer implies the person can only clearly see objects that are closer than 20 cm away.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sound; Light, Electromagnetic Radiation; Geometrical Optics; Spherical mirrors; and Reflection and refraction.

Question 12 of 230

Data ID: 829679

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:57 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which statement correctly describes how enzymes affect chemical reactions? Stabilization of:

  • A.
    the substrate changes the free energy of the reaction.
  • B.
    the transition state changes the free energy of the reaction.
  • C.
    the substrate changes the activation energy of the reaction.
  • D.
    the transition state changes the activation energy of the reaction.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Although an enzyme binds to substrate, it does not affect the free energy of a reaction.
  2. Although an enzyme does bind the transition state, it does not affect the free energy of a reaction.
  3. An enzyme does not stabilize a substrate to change the activation energy of a reaction. Substrate stabilization would increase the activation energy and slow down the reaction.
  4. An enzyme stabilizes the transition state, thus lowering the activation energy of a reaction.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Enzymes; Kinetics; and Bioenergetics.

Question 13 of 230

Data ID: 829686

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:57 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following will decrease the percentage ionization of 1.0 M acetic acid, CH3CO2H(aq)?

  • A.
    Chlorinating the CH3 group
  • B.
    Diluting the solution
  • C.
    Adding concentrated HCl(aq)
  • D.
    Adding a drop of basic indicator

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Replacing H with Cl will stabilize the formation of negative charge on the nearby carboxylic acid group. This is because chlorine is an electronegative atom. This change will therefore increase the percentage ionization of the acid.
  2. Diluting the solution will increase the percentage ionization by Le Châtelier's Principle.
  3. HCl is a strong acid that will increase the amount of H+ in solution and thus decrease the percentage of CH3CO2H that ionizes.
  4. Adding a drop of basic indicator will slightly increase the amount of dissociated (ionized) acid due to the acid−base reaction that occurs between the two.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Acid-Base Equilibria; Solubility; and Titration.

Question 14 of 230

Data ID: 829942

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:58 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 14-17)

Researchers are designing a micro-robot equipped with an oxygen sensor for intraocular procedures. The micro-robot will be injected into the patient’s eye to determine if a blood clot is present. The micro-robot is powered by electromagnetic radiations that induce a current in its interior circuit. The current powers a motor that propels the micro-robot through the fluid of the vitreous humor. Inside the eye, the micro-robot experiences a viscous drag force F dependent on the vitreous humor viscosity η, and the micro-robot’s diameter d and velocity v:

F = –3πdηv

Equation 1

The micro-robot uses a luminescent sensor film to determine the oxygen concentration, and potential blood clots, in the retina. In this method, a blue LED is shined into the eye and strikes the surface of the sensor. The sensor coating absorbs the photons from the LED and a short time later emits photons that are subsequently detected by a photodiode (Figure 1). Experiments in which oxygen parameters were controlled showed that oxygen, if present in the retina, affects both the time it takes the sensor to emit the photons and the intensity of the emitted radiation.

Figure 1 Micro-robot equipped with oxygen sensor used to detect blood clots

 

If the results of the test indicate that the patient has a clot that is obstructing blood flow, a second micro-robot, coated with tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA), is steered to the thrombus site where it punctures and docks with a retinal vein. The enzyme t-PA is a serine protease that converts plasminogen into plasmin, which subsequently degrades the clot by fibrinolysis.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

When the current in the micro-robot’s circuit increases and the resistance of the circuit remains constant, the voltage of the circuit:

  • A.
    decreases.
  • B.
    increases.
  • C.
    is constant.
  • D.
    is zero.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. This option contradicts Ohm’s law which states that current is equal to voltage divided by resistance. If current increases and resistance is constant, then voltage cannot decrease.
  2. This option is correct because according to Ohm’s law, current is equal to voltage divided by resistance. If current increases and resistance is constant, then voltage increases as well.
  3. This option contradicts Ohm’s law which states that current is equal to voltage divided by resistance. If current increases and resistance is constant, then voltage cannot remain constant.
  4. This option contradicts Ohm’s law that states that current is equal to voltage divided by resistance. If current is not zero, then voltage cannot be zero, too.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Electrostatics; Circuit Elements; and Magnetism.

Question 15 of 230

Data ID: 829954

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:59 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 14-17)

Researchers are designing a micro-robot equipped with an oxygen sensor for intraocular procedures. The micro-robot will be injected into the patient’s eye to determine if a blood clot is present. The micro-robot is powered by electromagnetic radiations that induce a current in its interior circuit. The current powers a motor that propels the micro-robot through the fluid of the vitreous humor. Inside the eye, the micro-robot experiences a viscous drag force F dependent on the vitreous humor viscosity η, and the micro-robot’s diameter d and velocity v:

F = –3πdηv

Equation 1

The micro-robot uses a luminescent sensor film to determine the oxygen concentration, and potential blood clots, in the retina. In this method, a blue LED is shined into the eye and strikes the surface of the sensor. The sensor coating absorbs the photons from the LED and a short time later emits photons that are subsequently detected by a photodiode (Figure 1). Experiments in which oxygen parameters were controlled showed that oxygen, if present in the retina, affects both the time it takes the sensor to emit the photons and the intensity of the emitted radiation.

Figure 1 Micro-robot equipped with oxygen sensor used to detect blood clots

 

If the results of the test indicate that the patient has a clot that is obstructing blood flow, a second micro-robot, coated with tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA), is steered to the thrombus site where it punctures and docks with a retinal vein. The enzyme t-PA is a serine protease that converts plasminogen into plasmin, which subsequently degrades the clot by fibrinolysis.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Knowing that the speed of light in the vitreous humor is 2.1 × 108 m/s, what is the index of refraction of the vitreous humor? (Note: The speed of light in a vacuum is 3.0 × 108 m/s.)

  • A.
    0.7
  • B.
    1.4
  • C.
    2.1
  • D.
    3.0

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. This option implies light travels faster through the vitreous humor than through vacuum, which is a scientific impossibility.
  2. The index of refraction of a medium is equal to the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to the speed of light in the medium, thus in this case is equal to (3.0 × 108 m/s)/(2.1 × 108 m/s) = 1.4.
  3. This is 50% larger than the index of refraction of the vitreous humor, which is equal to (3.0 × 108 m/s)/(2.1 × 108 m/s) = 1.4.
  4. The index of refraction of the vitreous humor is equal to (3.0 × 108 m/s)/(2.1 × 108 m/s) = 1.4; however, this (3.0) number is 114% larger.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sound; Light, Electromagnetic Radiation; Geometrical Optics; Spherical mirrors; and Reflection and refraction.

x

C
D

Question 16 of 230

Data ID: 829962

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:39:59 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 14-17)

Researchers are designing a micro-robot equipped with an oxygen sensor for intraocular procedures. The micro-robot will be injected into the patient’s eye to determine if a blood clot is present. The micro-robot is powered by electromagnetic radiations that induce a current in its interior circuit. The current powers a motor that propels the micro-robot through the fluid of the vitreous humor. Inside the eye, the micro-robot experiences a viscous drag force F dependent on the vitreous humor viscosity η, and the micro-robot’s diameter d and velocity v:

F = –3πdηv

Equation 1

The micro-robot uses a luminescent sensor film to determine the oxygen concentration, and potential blood clots, in the retina. In this method, a blue LED is shined into the eye and strikes the surface of the sensor. The sensor coating absorbs the photons from the LED and a short time later emits photons that are subsequently detected by a photodiode (Figure 1). Experiments in which oxygen parameters were controlled showed that oxygen, if present in the retina, affects both the time it takes the sensor to emit the photons and the intensity of the emitted radiation.

Figure 1 Micro-robot equipped with oxygen sensor used to detect blood clots

 

If the results of the test indicate that the patient has a clot that is obstructing blood flow, a second micro-robot, coated with tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA), is steered to the thrombus site where it punctures and docks with a retinal vein. The enzyme t-PA is a serine protease that converts plasminogen into plasmin, which subsequently degrades the clot by fibrinolysis.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

The intensity of the radiation emitted by the oxygen sensor is directly proportional to the:

  • A.
    propagation speed of the radiation.
  • B.
    wavelength of the radiation.
  • C.
    polarization of photons emitted.
  • D.
    number of photons emitted.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The intensity of radiation is not related to its propagation speed, but to the number of photons emitted per unit time and their respective frequency.
  2. The intensity of radiation is not related to the wavelength of radiation, but to the number of photons emitted per unit time and their respective frequency.
  3. The intensity of radiation is not related to the polarization state, but to the number of photons emitted per unit time and their respective frequency.
  4. The energy of electromagnetic radiation is directly proportional to the number of photons, and the intensity of electromagnetic radiation is defined as energy emitted per unit time. Thus, intensity is directly proportional to the number of photons emitted.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sound; Light, Electromagnetic Radiation; Geometrical Optics; Spherical mirrors; and Reflection and refraction.

Question 17 of 230

Data ID: 829965

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:00 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 14-17)

Researchers are designing a micro-robot equipped with an oxygen sensor for intraocular procedures. The micro-robot will be injected into the patient’s eye to determine if a blood clot is present. The micro-robot is powered by electromagnetic radiations that induce a current in its interior circuit. The current powers a motor that propels the micro-robot through the fluid of the vitreous humor. Inside the eye, the micro-robot experiences a viscous drag force F dependent on the vitreous humor viscosity η, and the micro-robot’s diameter d and velocity v:

F = –3πdηv

Equation 1

The micro-robot uses a luminescent sensor film to determine the oxygen concentration, and potential blood clots, in the retina. In this method, a blue LED is shined into the eye and strikes the surface of the sensor. The sensor coating absorbs the photons from the LED and a short time later emits photons that are subsequently detected by a photodiode (Figure 1). Experiments in which oxygen parameters were controlled showed that oxygen, if present in the retina, affects both the time it takes the sensor to emit the photons and the intensity of the emitted radiation.

Figure 1 Micro-robot equipped with oxygen sensor used to detect blood clots

 

If the results of the test indicate that the patient has a clot that is obstructing blood flow, a second micro-robot, coated with tissue plasminogen activator (t-PA), is steered to the thrombus site where it punctures and docks with a retinal vein. The enzyme t-PA is a serine protease that converts plasminogen into plasmin, which subsequently degrades the clot by fibrinolysis.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What is the energy of the photons emitted by the LED at a frequency of 610 THz? (Note: h = 6.6 × 10–34 J·s)

  • A.
    9.2 × 10–12 J
  • B.
    1.6 × 10–16 J
  • C.
    1.1 × 10–18 J
  • D.
    4.0 × 10–19 J

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This is about seven orders of magnitude larger than the energy of a photon of frequency 610 THz which is 4 × 10−19 J.
  2. This is about three orders of magnitude larger than the energy of a photon of frequency 610 THz which is 4 × 10−19 J.
  3. This is about three times larger than the energy of a photon of frequency 610 THz which is 4 × 10−19 J.
  4. The energy of a photon of frequency 610 THz is equal to 6.6 × 10−34 J•s × 610 x 1012 Hz = 4 × 10−19 J.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sound; Light, Electromagnetic Radiation; Geometrical Optics; Spherical mirrors; and Reflection and refraction.

Question 18 of 230

Data ID: 829979

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:00 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 18-21)

Blood pressure can be measured in the brachial artery, which has a cross-sectional area of 25 mm2 in a healthy adult. The blood flow rate is related to the power the person generates to move their body, as shown Figure 1.

Figure 1 Relationship between the brachial artery blood flow rate of a healthy adult and the power generated for body motion

 

An athlete running on a horizontal treadmill maintained a brachial artery blood flow rate of 1.1 L/min for 30 s. The treadmill had an electric circuit used to charge a parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance 1 mF.

The maximum and minimum blood pressures of a healthy adult (the systolic Ps and the diastolic Pd pressures, respectively) are about Ps  = 120 mmHg and Pd = 75 mm Hg. The mean arterial pressure PMPA is given by

PMPA = Pd + (Ps – Pd)/3

Equation 1

and the pulse pressure Pp is defined as

Pp = Ps – Pd

Equation 2

 

Ca2+ ions are normally found in the blood at a concentration of 100 mg/L. The location of internal organs where changes in the calcium content occur can be found by placing positron-emitting isotopes such as 15O (with a half-life T1/2 = 2 min) into the patient’s bloodstream. When positrons annihilate with electrons, they produce pairs of gamma photons, each with an energy of 500 keV. The location where gamma photons originate gives the location of the organ of interest.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What is the relationship between Pp, PMPA, and Pd?

  • A.
    Pp = PMPA + 3Pd
  • B.
    Pp = 3PMPA + Pd
  • C.
    Pp = 3PMPA − 3Pd
  • D.
    Pp = PMPA − 3Pd

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. This option incorrectly implies that Equation 1 is PMPA = 3Pd.
  2. This option incorrectly implies that Pd = 0.
  3. Substituting Equation 2 in Equation 1 yields ​​ that can be arranged as Pp = 3PMPA − 3Pd.
  4. This option incorrectly implies that PMPA = 0.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Fluids and Gas Phase.

Result / Timing / Tags

Correct
A
B
C
✓
D

1 min 25 secs
Difficulty Level:
Easy

CC4B
FC4

Question 19 of 230

Data ID: 829994

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:01 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 18-21)

Blood pressure can be measured in the brachial artery, which has a cross-sectional area of 25 mm2 in a healthy adult. The blood flow rate is related to the power the person generates to move their body, as shown Figure 1.

Figure 1 Relationship between the brachial artery blood flow rate of a healthy adult and the power generated for body motion

 

An athlete running on a horizontal treadmill maintained a brachial artery blood flow rate of 1.1 L/min for 30 s. The treadmill had an electric circuit used to charge a parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance 1 mF.

The maximum and minimum blood pressures of a healthy adult (the systolic Ps and the diastolic Pd pressures, respectively) are about Ps  = 120 mmHg and Pd = 75 mm Hg. The mean arterial pressure PMPA is given by

PMPA = Pd + (Ps – Pd)/3

Equation 1

and the pulse pressure Pp is defined as

Pp = Ps – Pd

Equation 2

 

Ca2+ ions are normally found in the blood at a concentration of 100 mg/L. The location of internal organs where changes in the calcium content occur can be found by placing positron-emitting isotopes such as 15O (with a half-life T1/2 = 2 min) into the patient’s bloodstream. When positrons annihilate with electrons, they produce pairs of gamma photons, each with an energy of 500 keV. The location where gamma photons originate gives the location of the organ of interest.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What percentage of standard atmospheric pressure is the pulse pressure of a healthy adult?

  • A.
    10%
  • B.
    6%
  • C.
    2%
  • D.
    1%

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Atmospheric pressure is 760 mmHg. If pulse pressure was 10% of this value, it would be 76 mmHg.
  2. The pulse pressure in a healthy adult is (120 − 75) mmHg = 45 mmHg, and so the percentage is 45 mmHg/760 mmHg = 6%.
  3. A pulse pressure that is 2% of atmospheric pressure is 0.02 × 760 mmHg = 15.2 mmHg.
  4. A pulse pressure that is 1% of atmospheric pressure would be 0.01 × 760 mmHg = 7.6 mmHg.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Gas Phase.

Question 20 of 230

Data ID: 829997

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:02 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 18-21)

Blood pressure can be measured in the brachial artery, which has a cross-sectional area of 25 mm2 in a healthy adult. The blood flow rate is related to the power the person generates to move their body, as shown Figure 1.

Figure 1 Relationship between the brachial artery blood flow rate of a healthy adult and the power generated for body motion

 

An athlete running on a horizontal treadmill maintained a brachial artery blood flow rate of 1.1 L/min for 30 s. The treadmill had an electric circuit used to charge a parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance 1 mF.

The maximum and minimum blood pressures of a healthy adult (the systolic Ps and the diastolic Pd pressures, respectively) are about Ps  = 120 mmHg and Pd = 75 mm Hg. The mean arterial pressure PMPA is given by

PMPA = Pd + (Ps – Pd)/3

Equation 1

and the pulse pressure Pp is defined as

Pp = Ps – Pd

Equation 2

 

Ca2+ ions are normally found in the blood at a concentration of 100 mg/L. The location of internal organs where changes in the calcium content occur can be found by placing positron-emitting isotopes such as 15O (with a half-life T1/2 = 2 min) into the patient’s bloodstream. When positrons annihilate with electrons, they produce pairs of gamma photons, each with an energy of 500 keV. The location where gamma photons originate gives the location of the organ of interest.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What fraction of a 15O sample decays in 10 min?

  • A.
    1/8
  • B.
    9/16
  • C.
    3/4
  • D.
    31/32

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Based on the radioactive decay law N(t) = N (1/2)t/T where T = 2 min, this fraction of 15O decays in t = 2 min × ln(7/8) ÷ ln(1/2) = 22 seconds.
  2. Based on the radioactive decay law N(t) = N (1/2)t/T where T = 2 min, this fraction of 15O decays in t = 2 min × ln(7/16) ÷ ln(1/2) = 2.4 minutes.
  3. Based on the radioactive decay law N(t) = N (1/2)t/T where T = 2 min, this fraction of 15O decays in t = 2 min × ln(1/4) ÷ ln(1/2) = 4 minutes.
  4. This answer is correct because based on the radioactive decay law N(t) = N (1/2)t/T where T = 2 min, this fraction of 15O decays in t = 2 min × ln(1/32) ÷ ln(1/2) = 10 minutes.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Atomic Nucleus and Electronic Structure.

Question 21 of 230

Data ID: 829999

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:02 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 18-21)

Blood pressure can be measured in the brachial artery, which has a cross-sectional area of 25 mm2 in a healthy adult. The blood flow rate is related to the power the person generates to move their body, as shown Figure 1.

Figure 1 Relationship between the brachial artery blood flow rate of a healthy adult and the power generated for body motion

 

An athlete running on a horizontal treadmill maintained a brachial artery blood flow rate of 1.1 L/min for 30 s. The treadmill had an electric circuit used to charge a parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance 1 mF.

The maximum and minimum blood pressures of a healthy adult (the systolic Ps and the diastolic Pd pressures, respectively) are about Ps  = 120 mmHg and Pd = 75 mm Hg. The mean arterial pressure PMPA is given by

PMPA = Pd + (Ps – Pd)/3

Equation 1

and the pulse pressure Pp is defined as

Pp = Ps – Pd

Equation 2

 

Ca2+ ions are normally found in the blood at a concentration of 100 mg/L. The location of internal organs where changes in the calcium content occur can be found by placing positron-emitting isotopes such as 15O (with a half-life T1/2 = 2 min) into the patient’s bloodstream. When positrons annihilate with electrons, they produce pairs of gamma photons, each with an energy of 500 keV. The location where gamma photons originate gives the location of the organ of interest.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

What is the work generated by a healthy adult who circulates 9 L of blood through the brachial artery in 10 min?

  • A.
    2 kJ
  • B.
    12 kJ
  • C.
    20 kJ
  • D.
    120 kJ

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This option is 60 times smaller than the correct work, because a volume flow rate of 9 liters in 10 minutes means 900 mL/min, and according to the figure, this flow rate correlates to a power of 200 W. The correct work is then 200 W × 600 s = 120 kJ.
  2. This option is 10 times smaller than the correct work, because a volume flow rate of 9 liters in 10 minutes means 900 mL/min and according to the figure, this flow rate correlates to a power of 200 W. The correct work is then 200 W × 600 s = 120 kJ.
  3. This option is one order of magnitude smaller than the correct work, because a volume flow rate of 9 liters in 10 minutes means 900 mL/min, and according to the figure, this flow rate correlates to a power of 200 W. The correct work is then 200 W × 600 s = 120 kJ.
  4. A volume flow rate of 9 liters in 10 minutes means 900 mL/min. According to the figure, this flow rate correlates to a power of 200 W. The work is then 200 W × 600 s = 120 kJ.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Vectors; Newton's First Law; Torque; and Work and energy.

Question 22 of 230

Data ID: 830000

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:03 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 22-25)

The natural development and repair of bone tissue provides a good example for the design of mineralized tissue fabrication schemes. Besides cells, bone tissue consists of both inorganic and organic components. The inorganic component is a carbonated, calcium-deficient, nano-crystalline hydroxyapatite [HA = Ca5(PO4)3(OH)]. The organic component is a matrix composed of type I collagen (95%) and non-collageneous proteins.

Researchers in tissue engineering have developed a peptide amphiphile (Compound 1, Figure 1) that can be used to make a nanostructured fibrous scaffold (composite) similar to an extracellular matrix found in bone.

Compound 1

Figure 1 Structure of peptide amphiphile, Compound 1, used to build scaffolds for synthetic bone formation

 

Compound 1 contained five regions: (1) a hydrophobic region, (2) residues that could be cross-linked for additional structural integrity, (3) spacer residues, (4) a residue that was phosphorylated for future attachment of nanocrystals of HA, and (5) residues with affinity for cells.

Molecules of Compound 1 spontaneously self-assemble into cylindrical micelles in water at pH 4 but freely redissolve at pH 8 (Equation 1). Oxidation of self-assembled, micellular Compound 1 with iodine resulted in a solid material (Compound 2) that was stable to alkaline solutions (Equation 2). Reaction of Compound 2 with dithiol (Compound 3) resulted in reformation of Compound 1 (Equation 3).

Equation 1

Equation 2

Equation 3

The researchers wanted to mimic the alignment that occurs between crystals of HA and collagen fibrils in bone tissue. They created a peptide amphiphile that assembles into cylindrical micelles as shown in Figure 2. Subsequent capture of nanocrystals of HA followed by mineralization was used to form synthetic bone.

Figure 2 Space-filling molecular model illustrating cylindrical micelle formation by the peptide amphiphile, Compound 1

 

 

Adapted from J. D. Hartgerink, E. Beniash, and S. I. Stupp, Science. ©2001 American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Question + Answer Choices

Compared to micellular Compound 1, Compound 2 is structurally more rigid as a result of what type of interaction?

  • A.
    Intermolecular hydrogen bonding
  • B.
    Intermolecular covalent bonding
  • C.
    Intramolecular hydrogen bonding
  • D.
    Intramolecular covalent bonding

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Both compounds 1 and 2 form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Only Compound 2 has disulfide bridges, which is a type of intermolecular covalent bonding.
  2. Only Compound 2 has disulfide bridges, which is a type of intermolecular covalent bonding.
  3. Although intramolecular hydrogen bonding would lead to structural rigidity, it is found in both compounds 1 and 2.
  4. Intramolecular cross-linking could be found in a fraction of Compound 2 molecules, but these cross-links would not enhance the rigidity of a micelle as much as intermolecular cross-links would.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Nucleotides and Nucleic Acids, Lipids; Amino Acids, Peptides, Proteins; The Three-Dimensional Protein Structure, Nonenzymatic Protein Function; and Phenols, Polycyclic and Heterocyclic Aromatic Compounds.

Question 23 of 230

Data ID: 830001

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:03 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 22-25)

The natural development and repair of bone tissue provides a good example for the design of mineralized tissue fabrication schemes. Besides cells, bone tissue consists of both inorganic and organic components. The inorganic component is a carbonated, calcium-deficient, nano-crystalline hydroxyapatite [HA = Ca5(PO4)3(OH)]. The organic component is a matrix composed of type I collagen (95%) and non-collageneous proteins.

Researchers in tissue engineering have developed a peptide amphiphile (Compound 1, Figure 1) that can be used to make a nanostructured fibrous scaffold (composite) similar to an extracellular matrix found in bone.

Compound 1

Figure 1 Structure of peptide amphiphile, Compound 1, used to build scaffolds for synthetic bone formation

 

Compound 1 contained five regions: (1) a hydrophobic region, (2) residues that could be cross-linked for additional structural integrity, (3) spacer residues, (4) a residue that was phosphorylated for future attachment of nanocrystals of HA, and (5) residues with affinity for cells.

Molecules of Compound 1 spontaneously self-assemble into cylindrical micelles in water at pH 4 but freely redissolve at pH 8 (Equation 1). Oxidation of self-assembled, micellular Compound 1 with iodine resulted in a solid material (Compound 2) that was stable to alkaline solutions (Equation 2). Reaction of Compound 2 with dithiol (Compound 3) resulted in reformation of Compound 1 (Equation 3).

Equation 1

Equation 2

Equation 3

The researchers wanted to mimic the alignment that occurs between crystals of HA and collagen fibrils in bone tissue. They created a peptide amphiphile that assembles into cylindrical micelles as shown in Figure 2. Subsequent capture of nanocrystals of HA followed by mineralization was used to form synthetic bone.

Figure 2 Space-filling molecular model illustrating cylindrical micelle formation by the peptide amphiphile, Compound 1

 

 

Adapted from J. D. Hartgerink, E. Beniash, and S. I. Stupp, Science. ©2001 American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Question + Answer Choices

Which amino acid residues were incorporated into Compound 1 to promote the adhesion of cells on the scaffold surfaces?

  • A.
    Arg and Gly
  • B.
    Cys and Gly
  • C.
    Cys and Asp
  • D.
    Asp and Arg

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Arg has a side chain that can promote strong adhesion via electrostatic interaction, but Gly does not.
  2. Cys is part of the cross-linking region of Compound 1, not the cell adhesion region, and Gly has no side chain that can form strong attractive forces of any type.
  3. Although the side of Asp is in the cell adhesion region of Compound 1 and can form electrostatic interactions, Cys is part of the cross-linking region of Compound 1, not the cell adhesion region.
  4. Asp and Arg are part of the cell adhesion region of Compound 1. Both have side chains that can participate in electrostatic interactions.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 24 of 230

Data ID: 830002

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:04 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 22-25)

The natural development and repair of bone tissue provides a good example for the design of mineralized tissue fabrication schemes. Besides cells, bone tissue consists of both inorganic and organic components. The inorganic component is a carbonated, calcium-deficient, nano-crystalline hydroxyapatite [HA = Ca5(PO4)3(OH)]. The organic component is a matrix composed of type I collagen (95%) and non-collageneous proteins.

Researchers in tissue engineering have developed a peptide amphiphile (Compound 1, Figure 1) that can be used to make a nanostructured fibrous scaffold (composite) similar to an extracellular matrix found in bone.

Compound 1

Figure 1 Structure of peptide amphiphile, Compound 1, used to build scaffolds for synthetic bone formation

 

Compound 1 contained five regions: (1) a hydrophobic region, (2) residues that could be cross-linked for additional structural integrity, (3) spacer residues, (4) a residue that was phosphorylated for future attachment of nanocrystals of HA, and (5) residues with affinity for cells.

Molecules of Compound 1 spontaneously self-assemble into cylindrical micelles in water at pH 4 but freely redissolve at pH 8 (Equation 1). Oxidation of self-assembled, micellular Compound 1 with iodine resulted in a solid material (Compound 2) that was stable to alkaline solutions (Equation 2). Reaction of Compound 2 with dithiol (Compound 3) resulted in reformation of Compound 1 (Equation 3).

Equation 1

Equation 2

Equation 3

The researchers wanted to mimic the alignment that occurs between crystals of HA and collagen fibrils in bone tissue. They created a peptide amphiphile that assembles into cylindrical micelles as shown in Figure 2. Subsequent capture of nanocrystals of HA followed by mineralization was used to form synthetic bone.

Figure 2 Space-filling molecular model illustrating cylindrical micelle formation by the peptide amphiphile, Compound 1

 

 

Adapted from J. D. Hartgerink, E. Beniash, and S. I. Stupp, Science. ©2001 American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Question + Answer Choices

Which amino acid was incorporated into Compound 1 as a future site of covalent attachment to HA prior to mineralization?

  • A.
    Ser
  • B.
    Ala
  • C.
    Tyr
  • D.
    Thr

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Ser is the residue that was phosphorylated at its primary alcohol side chain for future attachment of nanocrystals of HA
  2. Ala has a methyl side chain, not a primary alcohol.
  3. The side chain of Tyr is a phenol, not a primary alcohol.
  4. The side chain of Thr is a secondary alcohol, not a primary alcohol.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 25 of 230

Data ID: 830003

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:05 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 22-25)

The natural development and repair of bone tissue provides a good example for the design of mineralized tissue fabrication schemes. Besides cells, bone tissue consists of both inorganic and organic components. The inorganic component is a carbonated, calcium-deficient, nano-crystalline hydroxyapatite [HA = Ca5(PO4)3(OH)]. The organic component is a matrix composed of type I collagen (95%) and non-collageneous proteins.

Researchers in tissue engineering have developed a peptide amphiphile (Compound 1, Figure 1) that can be used to make a nanostructured fibrous scaffold (composite) similar to an extracellular matrix found in bone.

Compound 1

Figure 1 Structure of peptide amphiphile, Compound 1, used to build scaffolds for synthetic bone formation

 

Compound 1 contained five regions: (1) a hydrophobic region, (2) residues that could be cross-linked for additional structural integrity, (3) spacer residues, (4) a residue that was phosphorylated for future attachment of nanocrystals of HA, and (5) residues with affinity for cells.

Molecules of Compound 1 spontaneously self-assemble into cylindrical micelles in water at pH 4 but freely redissolve at pH 8 (Equation 1). Oxidation of self-assembled, micellular Compound 1 with iodine resulted in a solid material (Compound 2) that was stable to alkaline solutions (Equation 2). Reaction of Compound 2 with dithiol (Compound 3) resulted in reformation of Compound 1 (Equation 3).

Equation 1

Equation 2

Equation 3

The researchers wanted to mimic the alignment that occurs between crystals of HA and collagen fibrils in bone tissue. They created a peptide amphiphile that assembles into cylindrical micelles as shown in Figure 2. Subsequent capture of nanocrystals of HA followed by mineralization was used to form synthetic bone.

Figure 2 Space-filling molecular model illustrating cylindrical micelle formation by the peptide amphiphile, Compound 1

 

 

Adapted from J. D. Hartgerink, E. Beniash, and S. I. Stupp, Science. ©2001 American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Question + Answer Choices

Compound 1 was designed to exhibit pH-dependent self-assembly. What feature(s) of the molecule is(are) responsible for the pH dependence of aggregation (Equation 1)?

  • A.
    Thiol side chains that can hydrogen bond
  • B.
    Long alkyl tail that exhibits predominantly London forces
  • C.
    Side chains whose net charge responds to pH
  • D.
    Covalent linkages that reversibly hydrolyze

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Charge development is the key to pH dependence of aggregation, not hydrogen bonding.
  2. Long alkyl chains are not affected by pH.
  3. In order for pH to affect self-assembly of Compound 1, there must be side chains whose net charge respond to pH. At pH 4, there is a smaller net negative charge, self-assembly; at pH 8, the net negative charge is much higher, causing repulsion of Compound 1 molecules and blocking self-assembly.
  4. There are no pH-dependent, reversibly hydrolyzable covalent bonds formed when Compound 1 self-assembles.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Nucleotides and Nucleic Acids, Lipids; Amino Acids, Peptides, Proteins; The Three-Dimensional Protein Structure, Nonenzymatic Protein Function; and Phenols, Polycyclic and Heterocyclic Aromatic Compounds.

Question 26 of 230

Data ID: 830005

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:05 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Why is the velocity of blood flow slower in capillaries than in arteries?

  • A.
    Capillary walls are more elastic than arterial walls.
  • B.
    Capillaries have less resistance to blood flow than arteries.
  • C.
    The total cross-sectional area of capillaries exceeds that of arteries.
  • D.
    Blood pressure is higher in the capillaries than in arteries.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The velocity of blood flow is primarily determined by the total cross-sectional area, not by elasticity of that level. The velocity of blood flow is inversely proportional to the total cross-sectional area.
  2. The velocity of blood flow is primarily determined by the total cross-sectional area, not by resistance to blood flow of that level.
  3. The high number of capillaries in the body means that the total cross-sectional area of these vessels is larger than any other vessel type in the circulatory system. This causes the velocity of the blood to decrease.
  4. There is an increased ratio of surface area to blood volume in capillaries compared to arteries. This means that the blood is in contact with more blood vessel surface area. This slows down the velocity of the blood within capillaries and thus drops the blood pressure.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Arterial and venous systems and Pressure and flow characteristics.

Question 27 of 230

Data ID: 830010

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:06 PM

Question + Answer Choices

The graph below shows the relationship between the predominant form of iron as a function of solution pH and applied potential. Based on the graph, which of the following statements is true?

  • A.
    At a potential of –0.4 V, as pH increases, Fe2+ is reduced and precipitates as Fe(OH)3.
  • B.
    At a potential of –0.44 V, the equilibrium between Fe and Fe2+ is independent of solution pH below pH 6.
  • C.
    At pH = 1, as the potential is changed from –0.2 to +0.8, Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+.
  • D.
    At pH = 8 and V = –0.1 V, Fe(OH)2 is the predominant form of iron.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The conversion of Fe2+ to Fe(OH)3(s) represents an oxidation. From the graph, at a potential of −0.4 V, no oxidation or reduction occurs. Fe(OH)2(s) precipitates.
  2. The Pourbaix diagram for iron is given, but only one statement correctly conveys information presented by the diagram. At an applied potential of –0.44V and pH below 6, there is an equilibrium between Fe(s) and Fe2+(aq), as shown by the line of demarcation. The fact that this line moves horizontally with increasing pH up until pH = 6 means that this equilibrium is unaffected by changing the pH.
  3. The opposite is true. At this pH as the potential is raised, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+.
  4. At pH = 8 and V = –0.1 V, Fe(OH)3(s) is the predominant form of iron.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Atomic Nucleus; Electronic Structure; The Periodic Table - Classification of Elements Into Groups by Electronic Structure, The Periodic Table - Variations of Chemical Properties with Group and Row; and Stoichiometry.

Question 28 of 230

Data ID: 830013

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:06 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following types of orbitals of the central atom are involved in bonding in octahedral compounds?

  • A.
    sp
  • B.
    sp3
  • C.
    p
  • D.
    d2 sp3

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. sp hybrid orbitals can be used to make linear AX2En compounds.
  2. Tetrahedral AX4 compounds and other compounds based on a tetrahedral arrangement of electron pairs utilize sp3 hybrid orbitals.
  3. A pure p type orbital will typically be involved in pi bonding, or will represent a lone pair of electrons.
  4. Octahedral compounds have six σ bonds and no stereochemically active lone pairs. According to valence bond theory, the central atom requires the hybridization of six atomic orbitals, d2sp3.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Covalent bonds.

Question 29 of 230

Data ID: 830016

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:07 PM

Question + Answer Choices

The term “ideal gas” refers to a gas for which certain assumptions have been made. Which of the following is such an assumption?

  • A.
    The law PV = nRT2 is strictly obeyed.
  • B.
    Intermolecular molecular forces are infinitely large.
  • C.
    Individual molecular volume and intermolecular forces are negligible.
  • D.
    One gram-mole occupies a volume of 22.4 L at 25°C and one atmosphere pressure.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The Ideal Gas Law is PV = nRT.
  2. Large intermolecular forces are associated with condensed phases (solids and liquids), not gases.
  3. A property of an "ideal" gas is that it is composed of particles that have negligible volume and do not exert intermolecular forces.
  4. The gram-mole is an old way to refer to the molar mass of a substance. The molar volume of an ideal gas at 25°C is 24.4 L, not 22.4 L.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Gas Phase.

Question 30 of 230

Data ID: 830060

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:08 PM

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 30-33)

Human rhodopsin is a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) found in rod cells of the retina. Rhodopsin is composed of opsin, a family of seven helix transmembrane proteins, and a covalently attached coenzyme, retinal. Retinal is derived from all-trans-retinol, also known as vitamin A (Figure 1). When rhodopsin is exposed to light, the 11-cis-retinal (Figure 1) converts to all-trans-retinal. Subsequent structural changes in rhodopsin allow it to interact with and activate the intracellular G protein transducin. The signal is turned off via phosphorylation of rhodopsin by rhodopsin kinase and subsequent binding of the protein arrestin to rhodopsin. Rhodopsin has a maximum absorption at 500 nm. Additional opsin based receptors are expressed in specialized cone cells and have maximum absorptions at 420 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm when reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal.

Figure 1 Structural representations of all-trans-retinol and 11-cis-retinal

 

Previous studies indicated that various intracellular loops of rhodopsin are important for interacting with arrestin. In order to determine which amino acids within the loops have the greatest impact on binding to arrestin, variants of rhodopsin containing one or more amino acid changes were prepared, and their binding to arrestin measured. Researchers focused on two loops, Loop I (Figure 2) and Loop II (Figure 3). Wild-type or variant rhodopsin was reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase in the presence of ATP before examining arrestin binding. In the absence of phosphorylation, arrestin binding to wild-type rhodopsin decreased by 90%.

Figure 2 Arrestin binding to Loop I variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

Figure 3 Arrestin binding to Loop II variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

 

Adapted from Raman, D.; Osawa, S.; and Weiss, E. ©1999 by American Chemical Society

Question + Answer Choices

The production of a variety of opsins functions to:

  • A.
    increase sensitivity to low light.
  • B.
    enable the detection of different colors.
  • C.
    ensure fast recovery of 11-cis-retinal after exposure.
  • D.
    increase refractive index of the eye lens.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. There is no information in the passage indicating that some opsins are more sensitive to light than others.
  2. Because the wavelengths of maximum light absorption are significantly different for the specialized opsins in the cone cells, these opsins enable the detection of different colors of light.
  3. There is no information in the passage indicating that some opsins have faster recovery of 11-cis-retinal after light exposure than others.
  4. There is no information in the passage indicating that opsins have any effect on the refractive index of the eye lens.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Molecular Structure and Absorption Spectra.

Question 31 of 230

Data ID: 830062

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:08 PM

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 30-33)

Human rhodopsin is a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) found in rod cells of the retina. Rhodopsin is composed of opsin, a family of seven helix transmembrane proteins, and a covalently attached coenzyme, retinal. Retinal is derived from all-trans-retinol, also known as vitamin A (Figure 1). When rhodopsin is exposed to light, the 11-cis-retinal (Figure 1) converts to all-trans-retinal. Subsequent structural changes in rhodopsin allow it to interact with and activate the intracellular G protein transducin. The signal is turned off via phosphorylation of rhodopsin by rhodopsin kinase and subsequent binding of the protein arrestin to rhodopsin. Rhodopsin has a maximum absorption at 500 nm. Additional opsin based receptors are expressed in specialized cone cells and have maximum absorptions at 420 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm when reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal.

Figure 1 Structural representations of all-trans-retinol and 11-cis-retinal

 

Previous studies indicated that various intracellular loops of rhodopsin are important for interacting with arrestin. In order to determine which amino acids within the loops have the greatest impact on binding to arrestin, variants of rhodopsin containing one or more amino acid changes were prepared, and their binding to arrestin measured. Researchers focused on two loops, Loop I (Figure 2) and Loop II (Figure 3). Wild-type or variant rhodopsin was reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase in the presence of ATP before examining arrestin binding. In the absence of phosphorylation, arrestin binding to wild-type rhodopsin decreased by 90%.

Figure 2 Arrestin binding to Loop I variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

Figure 3 Arrestin binding to Loop II variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

 

Adapted from Raman, D.; Osawa, S.; and Weiss, E. ©1999 by American Chemical Society

Question + Answer Choices

The environment of the retinal binding site is most likely:

  • A.
    hydrophilic.
  • B.
    positively charged.
  • C.
    negatively charged.
  • D.
    hydrophobic.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Retinal consists almost entirely of C-H bonds and is non-polar. Hydrophilic molecules have polar bonds and are especially capable of hydrogen-bonding.
  2. A positively charged binding domain is well-suited for positioning substrates that have areas of negative charge. Retinal is non-polar.
  3. A negatively charged binding domain is well-suited for positioning substrates that feature positive charge. Retinal is non-polar.
  4. Retinal is composed of mainly carbon and hydrogen, making it largely hydrophobic.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Covalent bonds.

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 30-33)

Human rhodopsin is a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) found in rod cells of the retina. Rhodopsin is composed of opsin, a family of seven helix transmembrane proteins, and a covalently attached coenzyme, retinal. Retinal is derived from all-trans-retinol, also known as vitamin A (Figure 1). When rhodopsin is exposed to light, the 11-cis-retinal (Figure 1) converts to all-trans-retinal. Subsequent structural changes in rhodopsin allow it to interact with and activate the intracellular G protein transducin. The signal is turned off via phosphorylation of rhodopsin by rhodopsin kinase and subsequent binding of the protein arrestin to rhodopsin. Rhodopsin has a maximum absorption at 500 nm. Additional opsin based receptors are expressed in specialized cone cells and have maximum absorptions at 420 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm when reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal.

Figure 1 Structural representations of all-trans-retinol and 11-cis-retinal

 

Previous studies indicated that various intracellular loops of rhodopsin are important for interacting with arrestin. In order to determine which amino acids within the loops have the greatest impact on binding to arrestin, variants of rhodopsin containing one or more amino acid changes were prepared, and their binding to arrestin measured. Researchers focused on two loops, Loop I (Figure 2) and Loop II (Figure 3). Wild-type or variant rhodopsin was reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase in the presence of ATP before examining arrestin binding. In the absence of phosphorylation, arrestin binding to wild-type rhodopsin decreased by 90%.

Figure 2 Arrestin binding to Loop I variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

Figure 3 Arrestin binding to Loop II variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

 

Adapted from Raman, D.; Osawa, S.; and Weiss, E. ©1999 by American Chemical Society

Question + Answer Choices

The source of the phosphate groups that are added to rhodopsin is:

  • A.
    arrestin.
  • B.
    rhodopsin kinase.
  • C.
    ATP.
  • D.
    all-trans-retinol.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Arrestin binds to rhodopsin after rhodopsin has been phosphorylated. Arrestin is not the source of the phosphate groups.
  2. Rhodopsin kinase catalyzes the reaction between ATP and rhodopsin that causes rhodopsin to be phosphorylated, but ATP is the source of the phosphate.
  3. The substrate for rhodopsin kinase that the source of phosphate is ATP.
  4. Retinol contains no phosphate groups.

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Enzymes; Kinetics; and Bioenergetics.

Passage 6 (Questions 30-33)

Human rhodopsin is a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) found in rod cells of the retina. Rhodopsin is composed of opsin, a family of seven helix transmembrane proteins, and a covalently attached coenzyme, retinal. Retinal is derived from all-trans-retinol, also known as vitamin A (Figure 1). When rhodopsin is exposed to light, the 11-cis-retinal (Figure 1) converts to all-trans-retinal. Subsequent structural changes in rhodopsin allow it to interact with and activate the intracellular G protein transducin. The signal is turned off via phosphorylation of rhodopsin by rhodopsin kinase and subsequent binding of the protein arrestin to rhodopsin. Rhodopsin has a maximum absorption at 500 nm. Additional opsin based receptors are expressed in specialized cone cells and have maximum absorptions at 420 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm when reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal.

Figure 1 Structural representations of all-trans-retinol and 11-cis-retinal

 

Previous studies indicated that various intracellular loops of rhodopsin are important for interacting with arrestin. In order to determine which amino acids within the loops have the greatest impact on binding to arrestin, variants of rhodopsin containing one or more amino acid changes were prepared, and their binding to arrestin measured. Researchers focused on two loops, Loop I (Figure 2) and Loop II (Figure 3). Wild-type or variant rhodopsin was reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase in the presence of ATP before examining arrestin binding. In the absence of phosphorylation, arrestin binding to wild-type rhodopsin decreased by 90%.

Figure 2 Arrestin binding to Loop I variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

Figure 3 Arrestin binding to Loop II variants of rhodopsin: (A) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal and phosphorylated by rhodopsin kinase; (B) Reconstituted with 11-cis-retinal in the presence of rhodopsin kinase with ATP (+ATP) or without ATP (–ATP)

 

 

Adapted from Raman, D.; Osawa, S.; and Weiss, E. ©1999 by American Chemical Society

Question + Answer Choices

What are the hybridization states of the carbon atoms involved in the conversion of trans to cis retinal?

  • A.
    sp
  • B.
    sp2
  • C.
    sp3
  • D.
    sp3d

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. A carbon atom that is sp hybridized is part of an AX2 system. AX2 systems are linear according to VSEPR.
  2. The reacting carbon atoms are both central to AX3 systems with 3 bonded atoms and no lone pairs. The preferred geometry for such a system is trigonal planar and the hybridization scheme that facilitates this geometry is sp2.
  3. Tetrahedral AX4 compounds and other compounds based on a tetrahedral arrangement of electron pairs utilize sp3 hybrid orbitals. What is depicted is a geometric rearrangement at AX3 systems, which are trigonal planar.
  4. Transition metals are capable of using d orbitals in bonding. An sp3d system has 5 electron pair domains and is based on a trigonal bipyramidal or other 5-coordinate geometry.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 34 of 230

Data ID: 830071

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:10 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 34-37)

The crystal structure of the protein lysosomal lipase (LipA) was determined. Crystal structure B factors, which are an indication of structural rigidity, were used to identify regions of high flexibility. Sites within these regions were varied in an attempt to create versions of LipA with higher thermal stability. All variants were then screened for retention of enzymatic activity. The three most thermally stable variants that retained enzymatic activity are shown in Table 1.

Table 1 Variants of LipA
Variant Amino acid substitutions
IX K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
X R33Q, D34N, K35D, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
XI R33G, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M

All proteins were expressed in Escherichia coli and purified chromatographically. After protein purity was verified via SDS-PAGE, wild-type and variant LipA proteins were characterized. The enzymatic activity of the proteins was measured at 37°C and then again after the protein was briefly exposed to higher temperatures and cooled down to 37°C (Figure 1). The thermal unfolding of the proteins was followed by loss of secondary structure (Table 2). Structural integrity was verified using NMR spectroscopy.

Figure 1 Enzymatic activity retention as a function of exposure temperature for wild-type and variant LipA proteins (Note: The solution contained either a pH 7 buffer (A) or a pH 4 buffer (B). The x-axis represents the temperature that the protein was heated to prior to the activity assay.)

 

Table 2 Parameters for Thermal Unfolding as Monitored by Loss of Secondary Structure
Parameter Reference 2 M Urea 4 M Urea
Ti, (°C) Wild-type 51.8 39.6 26.3
Variant IX 45.6 32.0 24.9
Variant X 48.5 33.1 24.0
Variant XI 49.3 34.3 25.4
Slope, 
deg cm2/(mmol K)		 Wild-type 08.2 03.9 03.5
Variant IX 05.7 03.3 02.6
Variant X 04.6 04.6 02.1
Variant XI 06.0 03.9 03.0

Note: Ti is the temperature at the start of unfolding and the slope is measured for the unfolding transition. The experiments were performed in a pH 7 buffer (reference) and in the presence of 2 M and 4 M urea, which is a chemical denaturant.

Adapted from W. Augustyniak, A. Brzezinska, R. Pijning, H. Wienk, R. Boelens, et al., Protein Science. ©2012 The Protein Society.

Question + Answer Choices

Which reaction is catalyzed by LipA?

  • A.
    ATP hydrolysis
  • B.
    Peptide bond cleavage
  • C.
    Hydrolysis of triacylglycerides
  • D.
    Transfer of carboxyl groups

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. LipA is a lipase, which does not hydrolyze ATP.
  2. LipA is a lipase, not a protease.
  3. LipA is a lipase, which hydrolyzes triacylglycerides.
  4. LipA is a lipase, not a transferase.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Enzymes; Kinetics; and Bioenergetics.

Parameter Reference 2 M Urea 4 M Urea
Ti, (°C) Wild-type 51.8 39.6 26.3
Variant IX 45.6 32.0 24.9
Variant X 48.5 33.1 24.0
Variant XI 49.3 34.3 25.4
Slope, 
deg cm2/(mmol K)		 Wild-type 08.2 03.9 03.5
Variant IX 05.7 03.3 02.6
Variant X 04.6 04.6 02.1
Variant XI 06.0 03.9 03.0

Note: Ti is the temperature at the start of unfolding and the slope is measured for the unfolding transition. The experiments were performed in a pH 7 buffer (reference) and in the presence of 2 M and 4 M urea, which is a chemical denaturant.

Adapted from W. Augustyniak, A. Brzezinska, R. Pijning, H. Wienk, R. Boelens, et al., Protein Science. ©2012 The Protein Society.

Question + Answer Choices

Compared to the wild-type LipA, what is the change in net charge in variant XI at pH 7?

  • A.
    +4
  • B.
    +3
  • C.
    −3
  • D.
    −4

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This change in net charge has the wrong sign but the correct magnitude.
  2. Variant XI loses positive charge and gains negative charge.
  3. This is an insufficient change in net negative charge.
  4. Variant XI has a change of –1 charge with R33G (+1 to 0), –2 charge with K112D (+1 to –1), and –1 charge with M134D (0 to –1) for a net change of –4.
