Question 36 of 230

Data ID: 830074

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:11 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 34-37)

The crystal structure of the protein lysosomal lipase (LipA) was determined. Crystal structure B factors, which are an indication of structural rigidity, were used to identify regions of high flexibility. Sites within these regions were varied in an attempt to create versions of LipA with higher thermal stability. All variants were then screened for retention of enzymatic activity. The three most thermally stable variants that retained enzymatic activity are shown in Table 1.

Table 1 Variants of LipA
Variant Amino acid substitutions
IX K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
X R33Q, D34N, K35D, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
XI R33G, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M

All proteins were expressed in Escherichia coli and purified chromatographically. After protein purity was verified via SDS-PAGE, wild-type and variant LipA proteins were characterized. The enzymatic activity of the proteins was measured at 37°C and then again after the protein was briefly exposed to higher temperatures and cooled down to 37°C (Figure 1). The thermal unfolding of the proteins was followed by loss of secondary structure (Table 2). Structural integrity was verified using NMR spectroscopy.

Figure 1 Enzymatic activity retention as a function of exposure temperature for wild-type and variant LipA proteins (Note: The solution contained either a pH 7 buffer (A) or a pH 4 buffer (B). The x-axis represents the temperature that the protein was heated to prior to the activity assay.)

 

Table 2 Parameters for Thermal Unfolding as Monitored by Loss of Secondary Structure
Parameter Reference 2 M Urea 4 M Urea
Ti, (°C) Wild-type 51.8 39.6 26.3
Variant IX 45.6 32.0 24.9
Variant X 48.5 33.1 24.0
Variant XI 49.3 34.3 25.4
Slope, 
deg cm2/(mmol K)		 Wild-type 08.2 03.9 03.5
Variant IX 05.7 03.3 02.6
Variant X 04.6 04.6 02.1
Variant XI 06.0 03.9 03.0

Note: Ti is the temperature at the start of unfolding and the slope is measured for the unfolding transition. The experiments were performed in a pH 7 buffer (reference) and in the presence of 2 M and 4 M urea, which is a chemical denaturant.

Adapted from W. Augustyniak, A. Brzezinska, R. Pijning, H. Wienk, R. Boelens, et al., Protein Science. ©2012 The Protein Society.

Question + Answer Choices

The data in Figure 1 show that:

  • A.
    the unfolding of LipA proteins is a non-reversible process.
  • B.
    exposure to high temperature eliminates any variant protein activity.
  • C.
    variation in the flexible regions of LipA decreases the temperature sensitivity of activity.
  • D.
    wild-type LipA loses all activity when first heated to 50°C.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. At pH 4, the enzymatic activity of LipA proteins does not change even after heating and cooling.
  2. In Figure 1, the highest temperatures used still show enzymatic activity for variants.
  3. Figure 1 shows that temperature has less effect on the enzymatic activity of the variants than wild-type LipA.
  4. At pH 7, wild-type LipA loses some but not all activity when first heated to 50°C.

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:12 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 34-37)

The crystal structure of the protein lysosomal lipase (LipA) was determined. Crystal structure B factors, which are an indication of structural rigidity, were used to identify regions of high flexibility. Sites within these regions were varied in an attempt to create versions of LipA with higher thermal stability. All variants were then screened for retention of enzymatic activity. The three most thermally stable variants that retained enzymatic activity are shown in Table 1.

Table 1 Variants of LipA
Variant Amino acid substitutions
IX K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
X R33Q, D34N, K35D, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M
XI R33G, K112D, M134D, Y139C, I157M

All proteins were expressed in Escherichia coli and purified chromatographically. After protein purity was verified via SDS-PAGE, wild-type and variant LipA proteins were characterized. The enzymatic activity of the proteins was measured at 37°C and then again after the protein was briefly exposed to higher temperatures and cooled down to 37°C (Figure 1). The thermal unfolding of the proteins was followed by loss of secondary structure (Table 2). Structural integrity was verified using NMR spectroscopy.

Figure 1 Enzymatic activity retention as a function of exposure temperature for wild-type and variant LipA proteins (Note: The solution contained either a pH 7 buffer (A) or a pH 4 buffer (B). The x-axis represents the temperature that the protein was heated to prior to the activity assay.)

 

Table 2 Parameters for Thermal Unfolding as Monitored by Loss of Secondary Structure
Parameter Reference 2 M Urea 4 M Urea
Ti, (°C) Wild-type 51.8 39.6 26.3
Variant IX 45.6 32.0 24.9
Variant X 48.5 33.1 24.0
Variant XI 49.3 34.3 25.4
Slope, 
deg cm2/(mmol K)		 Wild-type 08.2 03.9 03.5
Variant IX 05.7 03.3 02.6
Variant X 04.6 04.6 02.1
Variant XI 06.0 03.9 03.0

Note: Ti is the temperature at the start of unfolding and the slope is measured for the unfolding transition. The experiments were performed in a pH 7 buffer (reference) and in the presence of 2 M and 4 M urea, which is a chemical denaturant.

Adapted from W. Augustyniak, A. Brzezinska, R. Pijning, H. Wienk, R. Boelens, et al., Protein Science. ©2012 The Protein Society.

Question + Answer Choices

By what factor is the proton concentration increased in the experiments shown in Figure 1B from those shown in Figure 1A?

  • A.
    2
  • B.
    10
  • C.
    200
  • D.
    1000

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Doubling the concentration of H3O+ involves decreasing the pH by only 0.3 units.
  2. If the solution ph was lower by 1 pH unit, this would correspond to an increase in [H3O+] by a factor of 10.
  3. An increase of [H3O+] by a factor of 200 corresponds to a decrease in 2.3 pH units.
  4. The pH scale is logarithmic (pH = –log([H+]). A difference of 3 pH units corresponds to a 103 = 1000-fold difference in proton concentrations.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Acid-Base Equilibria; Solubility; and Titration.

Question 38 of 230

Data ID: 830084

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:13 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Which structure represents a phosphorylated subunit of glycogen as described in the passage?

  • A.
  • B.
  • C.
  • D.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This glucose molecule is phosphorylated at C1 instead of at C2 and C3.
  2. This glucose molecule is phosphorylated at C1 and C6 instead of at C2 and C3.
  3. This glucose molecule is phosphorylated at C2 and C6. The phosphorylation at C6 should be at C3.
  4. According to the passage, glycogen has phosphate monoesters at C2 and C3 in one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules. This glucose molecule is phosphorylated at C2 and C3.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Nucleotides and Nucleic Acids, Lipids; Amino Acids, Peptides, Proteins; The Three-Dimensional Protein Structure, Nonenzymatic Protein Function; and Phenols, Polycyclic and Heterocyclic Aromatic Compounds.

Question 39 of 230

Data ID: 830086

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:13 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Which type of bond is formed by glycogen synthase upon release of UDP?

  • A.
    α-1,4-Glycosidic bond
  • B.
    α-1,6-Glycosidic bond
  • C.
    β-1,4-Glycosidic bond
  • D.
    β-1,6-Glycosidic bond

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. In glycogen, each glucose subunit is connected to an adjacent glucose subunit by an α-1,4-glycosidic bond.
  2. Glycogen contains glucose subunits connected by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds, not α-1,6-glycosidic bonds.
  3. Glycogen contains glucose subunits connected by 1,4-glycosidic bonds that are α, not β.
  4. Glycogen contains glucose subunits connected by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds, not β-1,6-glycosidic bonds.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 40 of 230

Data ID: 830088

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:14 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

In addition to glucose, what other monosaccharide is part of the UDP-glucose structure?

  • A.
    Xylose
  • B.
    Fructose
  • C.
    Ribose
  • D.
    Arabinose

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Xylose is pentofuranose in which the C2 and C3 hydroxyl groups are trans, and the C3 hydroxyl group and the C4 hydroxymethyl group are cis. Although the other monosaccharide in UDP glucose is a pentofuranose, the C2 and C3 hydroxyl groups are cis, and the C3 hydroxyl group and the C4 hydroxymethyl group are trans.
  2. Fructose is a hexose, not a pentose.
  3. The other monosaccharide in UDP-glucose is ribose, a pentofuranose in which the C2 and C3 hydroxyl groups are cis, and the C3 hydroxyl group and the C4 hydroxymethyl group are trans.
  4. Although arabinose can be a pentofuranose, the C2 and C3 hydroxyl groups would be trans, not cis.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 41 of 230

Data ID: 830092

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:14 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Addition of laforin to the reaction mixtures used to generate the data in Figure 2 will result in:

  • A.
    a decrease in the intensity of the band representing WT without glucosidase when visualized with 14C decay.
  • B.
    an increase in the intensity of the band representing WT without glucosidase when visualized with 14C decay.
  • C.
    a decrease in the intensity of the band representing WT without glucosidase when visualized with 32P decay.
  • D.
    an increase in the intensity of the band representing WT without glucosidase when visualized with 32P decay.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. A decrease in the intensity of this band will not be observed because laforin will not cleave off the 14C-glucose residues from glycogen.
  2. An increase in the intensity of this band will not be observed because laforin will not cleave off the 14C-glucose residues from glycogen.
  3. Laforin will cleave off phosphate groups from glycogen, thus removing radioactive phosphate from the WT band and decreasing the intensity of the band.
  4. Laforin is a phosphatase, not a kinase. It will remove radioactive phosphate from glucose subunits in glycogen, not add them.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Enzymes; Kinetics; and Bioenergetics.

Question 42 of 230

Data ID: 830094

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:15 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

The radioactive decay described in the passage results in the formation of which two atoms?

  • A.
    N and S
  • B.
    B and Si
  • C.
    B and S
  • D.
    N and Si

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. The β− radioactive decay of 14C occurs according to the reaction 14C →14N + e− + antineutrino, and that of 32P according to 32P → 32S + e− + antineutrino, so the resulting atoms are N and S.
  2. The resulting atoms are neither B nor Si because the β− radioactive decay of 14C occurs according to the reaction 14C →14N + e− + antineutrino, and that of 32P according to 32P → 32S + e− + antineutrino.
  3. B is not formed because the β− radioactive decay of 14C occurs according to the reaction 14C→14N + e− + antineutrino, and that of 32P according to 32P → 32S + e− + antineutrino.
  4. Si is not formed because the β– radioactive decay of 14C occurs according to the reaction 14C → 14N + e− + antineutrino, and that of 32P according to 32P → 32S + e− + antineutrino.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Atomic Nucleus and Electronic Structure.

Question 43 of 230

Data ID: 830096

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:16 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 38-43)

Glycogen synthase catalyzes the lengthening of glycogen polymers through the addition of glucose. The reaction consists of transferring a glucosyl moiety from uridine diphosphate glucose (UDP-glucose, Figure 1) to the nonreducing end of the glycogen molecule along with the release of UDP. UDP-glucose is formed from the enzymatically-catalyzed reaction between glucose 1-phosphate and UTP. It was discovered that approximately one out of every 600–1500 glucose molecules in muscle glycogen contains phosphate groups covalently linked to the C2 and C3 positions as monoesters. It is hypothesized that glycogen synthase is responsible for the phosphorylation.

Figure 1 The structure of UDP-glucose

 

Lafora disease is a fatal progressive epilepsy that is characterized by the formation of deposits of sparsely branched, insoluble glycogen-like polymers called Lafora bodies (LBs). LBs contain a much larger amount of covalently-linked phosphate compared to normal glycogen. The abundance of phosphate has been linked to a mutation in the gene for laforin, a phosphatase specific to carbohydrates.

In order to test if glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen phosphorylation, both UDP-[U-14C]glucose and [β-32P]UDP-glucose were incubated with muscle extracts from wild-type (WT) mice and mice lacking muscle glycogen synthase (MGSKO). The reaction products were separated by SDS-PAGE and visualized by autoradiography that detects the products of β− decay (Figure 2). The data were normalized to account for the different decay rates. The samples were also treated with glucosidase, an enzyme that breaks down glycogen, and assayed in the same manner.

Figure 2 SDS-PAGE analysis from muscle extracts visualized with either 14C or 32P radioactive decay (Note: The + and – lanes represent the presence or absence of glucosidase treatment, respectively.)

 

 

Adapted from Tagliabracci, V. et al. Cell Metabolism. © 2011 Elsevier Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Compared to normal glycogen, the amount of what type of bond is decreased in Lafora bodies?

  • A.
    Phosphomonoester
  • B.
    Phosphodiester
  • C.
    α-1,4-Glycosidic bond
  • D.
    α-1,6-Glycosidic bond

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. According to the passage, Lafora bodies should have more phosphomonoester bonds than normal glycogen.
  2. There is no evidence from the passage that either Lafora bodies or normal glycogen have phosphodiester bonds.
  3. The passage did not indicate that the polymerization of glucose subunits was less in Lafora bodies than normal glycogen.
  4. The passage stated that Lafora bodies have sparse branching compared to normal glycogen. In glycogen, branching is due to the formation of α−1, 6-glycosidic bonds. Thus, these bonds are decreased in Lafora bodies.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Nucleotides and Nucleic Acids, Lipids; Amino Acids, Peptides, Proteins; The Three-Dimensional Protein Structure, Nonenzymatic Protein Function; and Phenols, Polycyclic and Heterocyclic Aromatic Compounds.

Question 44 of 230

Data ID: 830101

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:16 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Each of the following equations shows the dissociation of an acid in water. Which of the reactions occurs to the LEAST extent?

  • A.
    HCl + H2O → H3O+ + Cl−
  • B.
    HPO42− + H2O → H3O+ + PO43−
  • C.
    H2SO4 + H2O → H3O+ + HSO4−
  • D.
    H3PO4 + H2O → H3O+ + H2PO4−

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Hydrochloric acid is a strong acid. This reaction will occur to completion.
  2. HPO42– has a high negative charge and so dissociation of it will occur to the least extent.
  3. Sulfuric acid is a strong acid. This means that the dissociation shown will go to completion as water is a stronger base than the hydrogen sulfate anion.
  4. Phosphoric acid is a much stronger Brønsted acid than the hydrogen phosphate anion. This is readily seen due to the increased charge on the anion which will make it much harder to lose another proton.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Acid-Base Equilibria; Solubility; and Titration.

Question 45 of 230

Data ID: 830126

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:17 PM

Question + Answer Choices

In the above figure, an object O is at a distance of three focal lengths from the center of a convex lens. What is the ratio of the height of the image to the height of the object?

  • A.
    1/3
  • B.
    1/2
  • C.
    2/3
  • D.
    3/2

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The ratio of the image height to the object height is equal to the ratio of the lens-image distance to the object-lens distance. According to the thin lens equation, the distance between the lens and the image is ​​. The ratio sought is then equal to ​​, and not ​​.
  2. This is correct because the ratio of the image height to the object height is equal to the ratio of the lens-image distance to the object-lens distance. According to the thin lens equation, the distance between the lens and the image is ​​. The ratio sought is then equal to ​​.
  3. The ratio of the image height to the object height is equal to the ratio of the lens-image distance to the object-lens distance. According to the thin lens equation, the distance between the lens and the image is ​​. The ratio sought is then equal to ​​, and not ​​.
  4. The ratio of the image height to the object height is equal to the ratio of the lens-image distance to the object-lens distance. According to the thin lens equation, the distance between the lens and the image is ​​. The ratio sought is then equal to ​​, and not ​​.

Question 46 of 230

Data ID: 830127

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:17 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Gamma decay occurs when a nucleus emits:

  • A.
    a photon.
  • B.
    a positron.
  • C.
    a neutron.
  • D.
    an electron.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. By definition, gamma decay means the emission of photons by the nucleus.
  2. The emission of positrons by the nucleus is called beta-plus decay.
  3. The emission of neutrons by the nucleus occurs only during stimulated nuclear fission.
  4. The emission of an electron by the nucleus is called beta-minus decay.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Atomic Nucleus and Electronic Structure.

Question 47 of 230

Data ID: 830128

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:18 PM

Question + Answer Choices

When a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution is added to pulverized blackboard chalk, the following reaction takes place.

CaCO3(s) + H2SO4(aq) CaSO4(s) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)

At 25°C, the reaction is spontaneous and has:

  • A.
    negative ΔG° and positive ΔS°.
  • B.
    negative ΔG° and negative ΔS°.
  • C.
    positive ΔG° and negative ΔS°.
  • D.
    positive ΔG° and positive ΔS°.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Spontaneous reactions must have a negative ΔG°, and reactions that create gaseous products from solids and liquids must have a positive ΔS°.
  2. The reaction does not have negative ΔS because it produces a gas.
  3. The reaction must have negative ΔG because it is spontaneous. It is also apparent that the reaction has positive ΔS, because the reaction produces a gas.
  4. Spontaneous reactions have negative ΔG.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Thermodynamics; Thermochemistry; and Equilibrium.

Correct
A
✓

C

Question 48 of 230

Data ID: 830129

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:19 PM

Passage

Passage 9 (Questions 48-51)

A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed. For example, V2O5 is used as a catalyst to oxidize SO2 to SO3 in the first step of the production of sulfuric acid (reactions 1 and 2).

Reaction 1

Reaction 2

Furthermore, H2O2 can be decomposed with the aid of a catalyst, as shown in reactions 3 and 4.

Reaction 3

Reaction 4

Catalysts are widely utilized in automobile catalytic converters, where the exhaust gases are passed through beads of a solid catalyst. The catalyst aids in the conversions of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons to carbon dioxide and water, and nitric oxide to nitrogen gas. Unfortunately, catalytic converters also can catalyze the oxidation of SO2 to SO3, which subsequently reacts with moisture to form unwanted sulfuric acid.

Catalysts are classified as either homogeneous or heterogeneous. A homogeneous catalyst exists in the same phase as the reactant molecules, whereas a heterogeneous catalyst exists in a different phase than the reactants.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following is the overall reaction for the decomposition of H2O2 that is shown in reactions 3 and 4?

  • A.
    2H2O2(aq) + IO−(aq) → 2H2O(l) + I−(aq) + O2(g)
  • B.
    2H2O2(aq) + 2I−(aq) → 2H2O(l) + I2(g) + O2(g)
  • C.
    H2O2(aq) → H2O(l) + O2(g)
  • D.
    2H2O2(aq) → 2H2O(l) + O2(g)

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Both of the anions shown in this equation are intermediates of the net of reactions 3 and 4. This reaction is also not balanced for O atoms.
  2. Iodide is an intermediate in reactions 3 and 4. Furthermore, iodine does not appear in either reaction. The reaction shown in this response is not balanced for charge.
  3. This reaction is not balanced for O atoms.
  4. This response depicts the net sum of reactions 3 and 4: 2H2O2(aq) → 2H2O(l) + O2(g)

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Atomic Nucleus; Electronic Structure; The Periodic Table - Classification of Elements Into Groups by Electronic Structure, The Periodic Table - Variations of Chemical Properties with Group and Row; and Stoichiometry.

A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed. For example, V2O5 is used as a catalyst to oxidize SO2 to SO3 in the first step of the production of sulfuric acid (reactions 1 and 2).

Reaction 1

Reaction 2

Furthermore, H2O2 can be decomposed with the aid of a catalyst, as shown in reactions 3 and 4.

Reaction 3

Reaction 4

Catalysts are widely utilized in automobile catalytic converters, where the exhaust gases are passed through beads of a solid catalyst. The catalyst aids in the conversions of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons to carbon dioxide and water, and nitric oxide to nitrogen gas. Unfortunately, catalytic converters also can catalyze the oxidation of SO2 to SO3, which subsequently reacts with moisture to form unwanted sulfuric acid.

Catalysts are classified as either homogeneous or heterogeneous. A homogeneous catalyst exists in the same phase as the reactant molecules, whereas a heterogeneous catalyst exists in a different phase than the reactants.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following diagrams does NOT illustrate a function of an automobile catalytic converter?

  • A.
  • B.
  • C.
  • D.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Catalytic converters were stated to aid in the conversion of hydrocarbons to carbon dioxide and water.
  2. Catalytic converters were stated to aid in the conversion of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide.
  3. This response depicts a chemical transformation (SO3 → SO2 + H2O) that is NOT catalyzed by an automobile's catalytic converter.
  4. Catalytic converters were stated to aid in the conversion of nitric oxide to nitrogen gas.

Question 50 of 230

Data ID: 830131

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:20 PM

Passage

Passage 9 (Questions 48-51)

A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed. For example, V2O5 is used as a catalyst to oxidize SO2 to SO3 in the first step of the production of sulfuric acid (reactions 1 and 2).

Reaction 1

Reaction 2

Furthermore, H2O2 can be decomposed with the aid of a catalyst, as shown in reactions 3 and 4.

Reaction 3

Reaction 4

Catalysts are widely utilized in automobile catalytic converters, where the exhaust gases are passed through beads of a solid catalyst. The catalyst aids in the conversions of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons to carbon dioxide and water, and nitric oxide to nitrogen gas. Unfortunately, catalytic converters also can catalyze the oxidation of SO2 to SO3, which subsequently reacts with moisture to form unwanted sulfuric acid.

Catalysts are classified as either homogeneous or heterogeneous. A homogeneous catalyst exists in the same phase as the reactant molecules, whereas a heterogeneous catalyst exists in a different phase than the reactants.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following most likely will occur if a homogeneous catalyst CANNOT be separated from the products at the end of a reaction?

  • A.
    The catalyst will become heterogeneous.
  • B.
    The products will be contaminated.
  • C.
    The reaction will not occur.
  • D.
    The reaction rate will speed up.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Of the many separation techniques available, only fractional distillation might cause the catalyst to become heterogeneous and that would facilitate separation by filtration.
  2. If a suitable separation technique is not found, the product will be contaminated by the catalyst.
  3. Separation of products from unreacted starting materials or catalysts does not affect the outcome of a reaction, which has already taken place.
  4. The reaction rate is unaffected. The separation process will occur after the reaction is completed.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Thermodynamics; Thermochemistry; and Equilibrium.

Question 51 of 230

Data ID: 830132

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:21 PM

Passage

Passage 9 (Questions 48-51)

A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction without being consumed. For example, V2O5 is used as a catalyst to oxidize SO2 to SO3 in the first step of the production of sulfuric acid (reactions 1 and 2).

Reaction 1

Reaction 2

Furthermore, H2O2 can be decomposed with the aid of a catalyst, as shown in reactions 3 and 4.

Reaction 3

Reaction 4

Catalysts are widely utilized in automobile catalytic converters, where the exhaust gases are passed through beads of a solid catalyst. The catalyst aids in the conversions of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide, hydrocarbons to carbon dioxide and water, and nitric oxide to nitrogen gas. Unfortunately, catalytic converters also can catalyze the oxidation of SO2 to SO3, which subsequently reacts with moisture to form unwanted sulfuric acid.

Catalysts are classified as either homogeneous or heterogeneous. A homogeneous catalyst exists in the same phase as the reactant molecules, whereas a heterogeneous catalyst exists in a different phase than the reactants.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

How will the rate of a catalyzed reaction be affected if the solid catalyst is finely ground before it is added to the reaction mixture?

  • A.
    The rate will be faster because a greater mass of catalyst will be present.
  • B.
    The rate will be faster because a greater surface area of catalyst will be exposed.
  • C.
    The rate will be slower because the fine catalyst particles will interfere with product formation.
  • D.
    The rate will remain the same because the mass of catalyst will be the same.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Grinding a solid does not change its mass.
  2. Grinding a heterogeneous catalyst increases the amount of catalyst available to the reaction and therefore increases its rate.
  3. Product formation occurs on a scale so small that the presence of more suspended solid cannot interfere with the course of reaction.
  4. While it is true that the mass is the same, the rate will increase due to the greater amount of catalyst that is interacting with the liquid solution.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Thermodynamics; Thermochemistry; and Equilibrium.

Question 52 of 230

Data ID: 831050

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:21 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A group of students investigated the reactions of several metals with solutions containing metallic ions.

Experiment 1

The students placed strips of a metal, X(s), into solutions containing different metallic ions, Ym+(aq), as shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Metal strip in solution

 

After several hours, they observed whether or not Y(s) formed on the surface of the metal strip (see Table 1).

Table 1 Results of Metal Strip Experiment
Identity of metal ions Composition of metal strip
Al(s) Cu(s) Zn(s) Ag(s) Au(s) Pb(s)
Al3+(aq) * no change no change no change no change no change
Cu2+(aq) new solid * new solid no change no change new solid
Zn2+(aq) new solid no change * no change no change no change
Ag+(aq) new solid new solid new solid * no change new solid
Au3+(aq) * * * new solid * *
Pb2+(aq) new solid no change new solid no change no change *
* = not studied

Experiment 2

The students placed a solid strip of each metal into an aqueous solution containing ions of the same metal. Complete circuits were established (Figure 2).

Figure 2 Complete circuit

 

Finally, the students checked a general chemistry text to determine the standard reduction potentials for the following reactions.

Pb2+(aq) + 2e– → Pb(s)     E°red = –0.127 V

Reaction 1

Cu2+(aq) + 2e– → Cu(s)     E°red = +0.339 V

Reaction 2

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

A student can most effectively increase the current passing through the circuit in Figure 2 by doing which of the following?

  • A.
    Using electrical wire with a smaller diameter
  • B.
    Increasing the temperature of the electrical wire
  • C.

    Decreasing the concentrations of Xn+(aq) and Ym+(aq)

  • D.
    Replacing the lightbulb with one that has a resistance of 0.2 Ω

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Using an electrical wire with a smaller diameter will increase the resistance of the wire. According to Ohm's law, this will decrease the current in the circuit.
  2. Increasing the temperature of the electrical wire will increase the resistance of the wire. According to Ohm's law, this will decrease the current in the circuit.
  3. Decreasing the concentrations will decrease the amount of charges that flow through the circuit that effectively decreases the current.
  4. This option is correct because according to Ohm's law, current is equal to voltage divided by resistance. In order to increase the current when the voltage is fixed, the resistance must be decreased, such as by replacing the lightbulb with one that has a resistance of 0.2 Ω instead of 0.5 Ω.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Electrostatics; Circuit Elements; and Magnetism.

Question 53 of 230

Data ID: 831123

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:22 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A group of students investigated the reactions of several metals with solutions containing metallic ions.

Experiment 1

The students placed strips of a metal, X(s), into solutions containing different metallic ions, Ym+(aq), as shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Metal strip in solution

 

After several hours, they observed whether or not Y(s) formed on the surface of the metal strip (see Table 1).

Table 1 Results of Metal Strip Experiment
Identity of metal ions Composition of metal strip
Al(s) Cu(s) Zn(s) Ag(s) Au(s) Pb(s)
Al3+(aq) * no change no change no change no change no change
Cu2+(aq) new solid * new solid no change no change new solid
Zn2+(aq) new solid no change * no change no change no change
Ag+(aq) new solid new solid new solid * no change new solid
Au3+(aq) * * * new solid * *
Pb2+(aq) new solid no change new solid no change no change *
* = not studied

Experiment 2

The students placed a solid strip of each metal into an aqueous solution containing ions of the same metal. Complete circuits were established (Figure 2).

Figure 2 Complete circuit

 

Finally, the students checked a general chemistry text to determine the standard reduction potentials for the following reactions.

Pb2+(aq) + 2e– → Pb(s)     E°red = –0.127 V

Reaction 1

Cu2+(aq) + 2e– → Cu(s)     E°red = +0.339 V

Reaction 2

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

When a strip of Zn is placed in a beaker containing 0.1 M HCl, H2(g) evolves. If a strip of Al is placed in a beaker containing 0.1 M HCl, does H2(g) evolve?

  • A.
    Yes; Al is reduced and H+(aq) is oxidized.
  • B.
    Yes; Al is oxidized and H+(aq) is reduced.
  • C.
    No; Al is reduced and Cl-(aq) is oxidized
  • D.
    No; Al is oxidized and H2O(l) is produced.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Al is an electropositive metal. It will not form anions. Formation of H2 from H+ is a reduction.
  2. Since a new solid forms when Al(s) is mixed with Zn2+(aq), it is reasonable to assume that Al(s) is more susceptible to oxidation than Zn(s).
  3. Electropositive metals are good reducing agents. Only exceptionally good oxidizing agents can oxidize chloride.
  4. If Al is oxidized, something has to be reduced. Of the possible choices, reduction of H+ occurs at a potential of 0.0 V and is most likely.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Electrochemistry.

Question 54 of 230

Data ID: 831124

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:23 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A group of students investigated the reactions of several metals with solutions containing metallic ions.

Experiment 1

The students placed strips of a metal, X(s), into solutions containing different metallic ions, Ym+(aq), as shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Metal strip in solution

 

After several hours, they observed whether or not Y(s) formed on the surface of the metal strip (see Table 1).

Table 1 Results of Metal Strip Experiment
Identity of metal ions Composition of metal strip
Al(s) Cu(s) Zn(s) Ag(s) Au(s) Pb(s)
Al3+(aq) * no change no change no change no change no change
Cu2+(aq) new solid * new solid no change no change new solid
Zn2+(aq) new solid no change * no change no change no change
Ag+(aq) new solid new solid new solid * no change new solid
Au3+(aq) * * * new solid * *
Pb2+(aq) new solid no change new solid no change no change *
* = not studied

Experiment 2

The students placed a solid strip of each metal into an aqueous solution containing ions of the same metal. Complete circuits were established (Figure 2).

Figure 2 Complete circuit

 

Finally, the students checked a general chemistry text to determine the standard reduction potentials for the following reactions.

Pb2+(aq) + 2e– → Pb(s)     E°red = –0.127 V

Reaction 1

Cu2+(aq) + 2e– → Cu(s)     E°red = +0.339 V

Reaction 2

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Assume that a circuit similar to that in Figure 2 is set up in which X = Al, Xn+ = Al3+, Y = Cu, and Ym+ = Cu2+. Which of the following reactions will occur?

  • A.
    2Al3+(aq) + 3Cu(s) → 2Al(s) + 3Cu2+(aq)
  • B.
    3Al3+(aq) + 2Cu(s) → 3Al(s) + 2Cu2+(aq)
  • C.
    2Al(s) + 3Cu2+(aq) → 2Al3+(aq) + 3Cu(s)
  • D.
    3Al(s) + 2Cu2+(aq) → 3Al3+(aq) + 2Cu(s)

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. While this reaction is balanced, it does not fit with the data in the table. Al3+(aq) does not react with Cu(s)
  2. This reaction is not balanced for charge. Furthermore, it does not fit with the data in the table. Al3+(aq) does not react with Cu(s).
  3. This response depicts the balanced chemical reaction that occurs when the four substances [Al(s), Al3+(aq), Cu(s), and Cu2+(aq)] are mixed.
  4. This reaction is not balanced for charge. There is a net +9 on the product side, but only net +4 on the reactant side.

Question 55 of 230

Data ID: 831125

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:23 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A group of students investigated the reactions of several metals with solutions containing metallic ions.

Experiment 1

The students placed strips of a metal, X(s), into solutions containing different metallic ions, Ym+(aq), as shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Metal strip in solution

 

After several hours, they observed whether or not Y(s) formed on the surface of the metal strip (see Table 1).

Table 1 Results of Metal Strip Experiment
Identity of metal ions Composition of metal strip
Al(s) Cu(s) Zn(s) Ag(s) Au(s) Pb(s)
Al3+(aq) * no change no change no change no change no change
Cu2+(aq) new solid * new solid no change no change new solid
Zn2+(aq) new solid no change * no change no change no change
Ag+(aq) new solid new solid new solid * no change new solid
Au3+(aq) * * * new solid * *
Pb2+(aq) new solid no change new solid no change no change *
* = not studied

Experiment 2

The students placed a solid strip of each metal into an aqueous solution containing ions of the same metal. Complete circuits were established (Figure 2).

Figure 2 Complete circuit

 

Finally, the students checked a general chemistry text to determine the standard reduction potentials for the following reactions.

Pb2+(aq) + 2e– → Pb(s)     E°red = –0.127 V

Reaction 1

Cu2+(aq) + 2e– → Cu(s)     E°red = +0.339 V

Reaction 2

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

A circuit similar to that in Figure 2 is set up in which X = Pb, Xn+ = Pb2+, Y = Cu, and Ym+ = Cu2+. Will an oxidation–reduction reaction occur spontaneously?

  • A.
    No; E°cell = –0.212.
  • B.
    No; E°cell = +0.212.
  • C.
    Yes; E°cell = –0.466.
  • D.
    Yes; E°cell = +0.466.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This is an incorrect calculation of E°cell. In order to use the potentials provided, one of the half-reactions must be reversed. This changes the sign of the reduction potential. The calculated value is the result of reversing both half-reactions and adding the resulting potentials.
  2. This is not the real value of E°cell. It results from adding both reduction potentials listed. One of the reductions needs to be reversed in order to have a complete oxidation−reduction reaction.
  3. This value of E°cell is obtained by combining the oxidation of Cu(s) to Cu2+(aq) with the reduction of Pb2+(aq) to Pb(s). The fact that the value is negative indicates that this reaction will not occur spontaneously.
  4. A spontaneous reaction occurs when E° is greater than zero, and this occurs if the oxidation of Pb(s) is combined with the reduction of Cu2+(aq), resulting in a net E° of +0.466 V.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Electrochemistry.

Experiment 2

The students placed a solid strip of each metal into an aqueous solution containing ions of the same metal. Complete circuits were established (Figure 2).

Figure 2 Complete circuit

 

Finally, the students checked a general chemistry text to determine the standard reduction potentials for the following reactions.

Pb2+(aq) + 2e– → Pb(s)     E°red = –0.127 V

Reaction 1

Cu2+(aq) + 2e– → Cu(s)     E°red = +0.339 V

Reaction 2

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

A circuit similar to that in Figure 2 is set up. It has a potential of 2.0 V. Assume that the resistance in the circuit is negligible compared to that of the lightbulb. Approximately how much current passes through the lightbulb?

  • A.
    0.5 A
  • B.
    1.0 A
  • C.
    2.0 A
  • D.
    4.0 A

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The current is equal to 2 V divided by 0.5 Ω equals 4.0 A, not 1.0 A. 
  2. The current is equal to 2 V divided by 0.5 Ω equals 4.0 A, not 1.0 A.
  3. The current is equal to 2 V divided by 0.5 Ω equals 4.0 A, not 2.0 A.
  4. The current is equal to 2 V divided by 0.5 Ω equals 4.0 A.

Question 57 of 230

Data ID: 831127

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:24 PM

Question + Answer Choices

The equilibrium

BaCrO4(s) Ba2+(aq) + CrO42–(aq)

exists in a saturated aqueous solution of BaCrO4. Dissolution of Na2CrO4 in a saturated aqueous BaCrO4 solution would:

  • A.
    have no effect on the position of this equilibrium.
  • B.
    shift this equilibrium left.
  • C.
    shift this equilibrium right.
  • D.
    shift this equilibrium first right and then left.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Adding the chromate anion to solution will drive the indicated equilibrium to the left by Le Châtelier's Principle.
  2. Dissolution of Na2CrO4 would introduce the common ion, CrO42–, which would reduce the solubility of BaCrO4 due to the common ion effect.
  3. The reverse is true. Adding a product anion stresses the existing equilibrium. Upon addition of chromate, the reaction quotient Q will exceed Ksp and the equilibrium shifts left until Q and Ksp are equal.
  4. This response does not follow the principles of chemical dynamics. While both reactions are constantly occurring (forward and reverse), the net reaction will only go to the left to compensate for the added anion.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Acid-Base Equilibria; Solubility; and Titration.

Result / Timing / Tags

Correct
A
B
✓
C
D

1 min 15 secs
Difficulty Level:

Question 58 of 230

Data ID: 831128

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:25 PM

Question + Answer Choices

What volume of a 0.120 M CaI2 solution would contain 0.078 mol of the solute?

  • A.
    35.0 mL
  • B.
    65.0 mL
  • C.
    350 mL
  • D.
    650 mL

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. A volume of 35 mL corresponds to only 0.035 L × 0.120 mol/L = 0.0042 mol of solute.
  2. A volume of 65 mL corresponds to only 0.065 L × 0.120 mol/L = 0.0078 mol of solute.
  3. A volume of 350 mL corresponds to only 0.35 L × 0.120 mol/L = 0.042 mol of solute.
  4. In order to obtain the volume of solution necessary to provide a given amount of solute in moles, one needs to take the number of moles and divide by the solution concentration in molarity: 0.078 mol × 1 L/0.120 mol = 0.65 L = 650 mL.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Acid-Base Equilibria; Solubility; and Titration.

Question 59 of 230

Data ID: 831129

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:26 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which measurement unit CANNOT be used to express power?

  • A.
    kg•m2 •s2
  • B.
    J•s-1
  • C.
    ft•lb •s-1
  • D.
    W

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. This answer is correct because the measurement unit of power is watt, defined as ​​.
  2. This unit is consistent with the definition of the watt, which is used to express power, because by definition ​​
  3. This unit is consistent with the definition of the watt, which is used to express power, because by definition ​​
  4. This unit is the watt, which is by definition is used to express power.

Question 60 of 230

Data ID: 831139

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:26 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

The practical ideal of democracy consists in the self-government of the masses in conformity with the decisions of popular assemblies. But while this system limits the extension of the principle of delegation, it fails to provide any guarantee against the formation of an oligarchical ruling clique. Undoubtedly it deprives the natural leaders of their quality as functionaries, for this quality is transferred to the people themselves. The crowd, however, is always subject to suggestion, being readily influenced by the eloquence of popular orators; moreover, direct government by the people, admitting of no serious discussions or thoughtful deliberations, greatly facilitates sudden insurrections of all kinds by individuals who are exceptionally bold, energetic, and adroit.

It is easier to dominate a large crowd than a small audience. The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional. Once the suggestions have taken effect, the crowd does not readily tolerate contradiction from a small minority, and still less from isolated individuals. A great multitude assembled within a small area is unquestionably more accessible to panic alarms, to unreflective enthusiasm, and the like than is a small meeting, whose members can quietly discuss matters among themselves.

It is a fact of everyday experience that enormous public meetings commonly carry resolutions by acclamation or by general assent, while these same assemblies, if divided into small sections, say of fifty persons each, would be much more guarded in their assent. Great party congresses, in which the elite of the membership are present, usually act in this way. Words and actions are far less deliberately weighed by the crowd than by the individuals of the little groups of which this crowd is composed. The fact is incontestable—a manifestation of the pathology of the crowd. The individual disappears in the multitude and therewith disappears also personality and sense of responsibility.

The most formidable argument against the sovereignty of the masses is, however, derived from the mechanical and technical impossibility of its realization. The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions. The impotence of direct democracy, like the power of indirect democracy, is a direct outcome of the influence of number.

It is obvious that a gigantic number of persons belonging to a unitary organization cannot do any practical work using a system of direct discussion. The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect to room and distance; from the topographical point of view, such an assembly would become altogether impossible if the members numbered ten thousand. Even if we imagined the means of communication to become much better than those that now exist, how would it be possible to assemble such a multitude in a given place, at a stated time, and with the frequency demanded by the exigencies of political life? In addition must be considered the physiological impossibility even for the most powerful orators of making themselves heard by a crowd of ten thousand persons.

There are, however, other reasons of a technical and administrative character that render impossible the direct self-government of large groups. If Peter wrongs Paul, it is out of the question that all the other citizens should hasten to the spot to undertake a personal examination of the matter in dispute and to take the part of Paul against Peter. By parity of reasoning, in the modern democratic state, it is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.

 

 

Adapted from R. Michels, Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchial Tendencies of Modern Democracy. In public domain. Published 1958 by The Free Press.

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following statements from the passage most strongly indicates that the passage was written prior to the twenty-first century?

  • A.
    “The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect of room and distance. . . .”
  • B.
    “The crowd . . . is . . . readily influenced by the eloquence of popular orators.”
  • C.
    “[T]hese same assemblies, if divided into small sections, . . . would be much more guarded in their assent.”
  • D.
    “The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions.”

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. This is a​ Social Science passage that falls under the content category "Government." This Comprehension question tests your understanding of passage statements as they appear in the content of the passage. The correct answer is A, because that option clearly reflects an earlier set of technological possibilities. In the twenty-first century, the Internet and other forms of electronic communication would certainly ameliorate “the gravest difficulties” in “holding . . . deliberative assemblies of a thousand members.” The sentence in paragraph five that follows the sentence in option A (“Even if we imagined the means of communication . . .”) clearly signals as well that the passage was written before the twenty-first century.
  2. There is no reason to think that eloquent orators do not retain the power to influence a crowd in the twenty-first century.
  3. There is no reason to think that the influence of large groups of people, as opposed to smaller groups, is less powerful in the twenty-first century than it was in previous eras.
  4. Nothing about this claim signals that it was made before the twenty-first century, or that it could not be made today.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Foundations of Comprehension.

Correct
A
✓
B
C
D

1 min 23 secs

The practical ideal of democracy consists in the self-government of the masses in conformity with the decisions of popular assemblies. But while this system limits the extension of the principle of delegation, it fails to provide any guarantee against the formation of an oligarchical ruling clique. Undoubtedly it deprives the natural leaders of their quality as functionaries, for this quality is transferred to the people themselves. The crowd, however, is always subject to suggestion, being readily influenced by the eloquence of popular orators; moreover, direct government by the people, admitting of no serious discussions or thoughtful deliberations, greatly facilitates sudden insurrections of all kinds by individuals who are exceptionally bold, energetic, and adroit.

It is easier to dominate a large crowd than a small audience. The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional. Once the suggestions have taken effect, the crowd does not readily tolerate contradiction from a small minority, and still less from isolated individuals. A great multitude assembled within a small area is unquestionably more accessible to panic alarms, to unreflective enthusiasm, and the like than is a small meeting, whose members can quietly discuss matters among themselves.

It is a fact of everyday experience that enormous public meetings commonly carry resolutions by acclamation or by general assent, while these same assemblies, if divided into small sections, say of fifty persons each, would be much more guarded in their assent. Great party congresses, in which the elite of the membership are present, usually act in this way. Words and actions are far less deliberately weighed by the crowd than by the individuals of the little groups of which this crowd is composed. The fact is incontestable—a manifestation of the pathology of the crowd. The individual disappears in the multitude and therewith disappears also personality and sense of responsibility.

The most formidable argument against the sovereignty of the masses is, however, derived from the mechanical and technical impossibility of its realization. The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions. The impotence of direct democracy, like the power of indirect democracy, is a direct outcome of the influence of number.

It is obvious that a gigantic number of persons belonging to a unitary organization cannot do any practical work using a system of direct discussion. The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect to room and distance; from the topographical point of view, such an assembly would become altogether impossible if the members numbered ten thousand. Even if we imagined the means of communication to become much better than those that now exist, how would it be possible to assemble such a multitude in a given place, at a stated time, and with the frequency demanded by the exigencies of political life? In addition must be considered the physiological impossibility even for the most powerful orators of making themselves heard by a crowd of ten thousand persons.

There are, however, other reasons of a technical and administrative character that render impossible the direct self-government of large groups. If Peter wrongs Paul, it is out of the question that all the other citizens should hasten to the spot to undertake a personal examination of the matter in dispute and to take the part of Paul against Peter. By parity of reasoning, in the modern democratic state, it is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.

 

 

Adapted from R. Michels, Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchial Tendencies of Modern Democracy. In public domain. Published 1958 by The Free Press.

Question + Answer Choices

Which of the following assertions in the passage does the author support with a practical example?

  • A.
    “[D]irect government by the people . . . greatly facilitates sudden insurrection of all kinds by individuals who are exceptionally bold, energetic, and adroit.”
  • B.
    “[A]nd therewith disappears also personality and sense of responsibility.”
  • C.
    “The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional.”
  • D.
    “[I]t is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.”

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The quotation in this option is not supported by any kind of practical, real-life example.
  2. The quotation in this option is not supported by any kind of practical, real-life example.
  3. The quotation in this option is not supported by any kind of practical, real-life example.
  4. This is a Reasoning Within the Text question because it asks you to assess the nature of the support that the author provides for various claims. In this case, the question asks which passage assertion is supported with a "practical example." The answer is D because the claim about the impossibility of a “collectivity” settling all of the “controversies that may arise” in a society, is supported by the example (in the final paragraph) of what happens when “Peter wrongs Paul.” That example demonstrates why it is absurd to imagine that the entire “collectivity” would be able to examine the circumstances of the controversy and adjudicate it.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Reasoning Within the Text.

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:40:27 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

The practical ideal of democracy consists in the self-government of the masses in conformity with the decisions of popular assemblies. But while this system limits the extension of the principle of delegation, it fails to provide any guarantee against the formation of an oligarchical ruling clique. Undoubtedly it deprives the natural leaders of their quality as functionaries, for this quality is transferred to the people themselves. The crowd, however, is always subject to suggestion, being readily influenced by the eloquence of popular orators; moreover, direct government by the people, admitting of no serious discussions or thoughtful deliberations, greatly facilitates sudden insurrections of all kinds by individuals who are exceptionally bold, energetic, and adroit.

It is easier to dominate a large crowd than a small audience. The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional. Once the suggestions have taken effect, the crowd does not readily tolerate contradiction from a small minority, and still less from isolated individuals. A great multitude assembled within a small area is unquestionably more accessible to panic alarms, to unreflective enthusiasm, and the like than is a small meeting, whose members can quietly discuss matters among themselves.

It is a fact of everyday experience that enormous public meetings commonly carry resolutions by acclamation or by general assent, while these same assemblies, if divided into small sections, say of fifty persons each, would be much more guarded in their assent. Great party congresses, in which the elite of the membership are present, usually act in this way. Words and actions are far less deliberately weighed by the crowd than by the individuals of the little groups of which this crowd is composed. The fact is incontestable—a manifestation of the pathology of the crowd. The individual disappears in the multitude and therewith disappears also personality and sense of responsibility.

The most formidable argument against the sovereignty of the masses is, however, derived from the mechanical and technical impossibility of its realization. The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions. The impotence of direct democracy, like the power of indirect democracy, is a direct outcome of the influence of number.

It is obvious that a gigantic number of persons belonging to a unitary organization cannot do any practical work using a system of direct discussion. The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect to room and distance; from the topographical point of view, such an assembly would become altogether impossible if the members numbered ten thousand. Even if we imagined the means of communication to become much better than those that now exist, how would it be possible to assemble such a multitude in a given place, at a stated time, and with the frequency demanded by the exigencies of political life? In addition must be considered the physiological impossibility even for the most powerful orators of making themselves heard by a crowd of ten thousand persons.

There are, however, other reasons of a technical and administrative character that render impossible the direct self-government of large groups. If Peter wrongs Paul, it is out of the question that all the other citizens should hasten to the spot to undertake a personal examination of the matter in dispute and to take the part of Paul against Peter. By parity of reasoning, in the modern democratic state, it is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.

 

 

Adapted from R. Michels, Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchial Tendencies of Modern Democracy. In public domain. Published 1958 by The Free Press.

Question + Answer Choices

Suppose the author were asked to advise a newly independent nation about the form of government it should establish. Based on the passage, the author would probably recommend:

  • A.
    a monarchy, in which decisions are made by one supreme leader.
  • B.
    a representative democracy, in which decisions are made by a limited number of elected representatives.
  • C.
    a direct democracy, in which decisions are made by all the adults in the country.
  • D.
    an oligarchy, in which decisions are made by a small group of capable individuals.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The author wishes to avoid “oligarchy” and the sway of a powerful “orator” (first paragraph), so it is clear that he prefers democratic values over the reign of a supreme leader.
  2. This is a Reasoning Beyond the Text question, because it asks you to apply reasoning from the passage to a scenario that is not addressed in the passage. The answer is B because the passage focuses on the problems inherent in a direct democracy, in which “the masses” would govern themselves entirely. The author worries about the susceptibility of crowds to passionate enthusiasms and the tendency of very large groups to avoid “serious discussion or thoughtful deliberations” (first paragraph). Based on the author’s preference for smaller assemblies rather than larger ones (paragraph 3) and his concern for the impracticality of huge assemblies—coupled with a dislike of “oligarchy” or domination in any form, the author would certainly recommend option B.
  3. Much of the passage is devoted to discussions of the intellectual and practical disadvantages of the political efforts of very large groups, so the author would not recommend a direct democracy.
  4. In the first paragraph, the author mentions the problem of failing to “guarantee against the formation of” an oligarchy, so this is not the form of government that he seeks.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Reasoning Beyond the Text.

It is easier to dominate a large crowd than a small audience. The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional. The most formidable argument against the sovereignty of the masses is, however, derived from the mechanical and technical impossibility of its realization. The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions. The impotence of direct democracy, like the power of indirect democracy, is a direct outcome of the influence of number.

It is obvious that a gigantic number of persons belonging to a unitary organization cannot do any practical work using a system of direct discussion. The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect to room and distance; from the topographical point of view, such an assembly would become altogether impossible if the members numbered ten thousand. Even if we imagined the means of communication to become much better than those that now exist, how would it be possible to assemble such a multitude in a given place, at a stated time, and with the frequency demanded by the exigencies of political life? In addition must be considered the physiological impossibility even for the most powerful orators of making themselves heard by a crowd of ten thousand persons.

There are, however, other reasons of a technical and administrative character that render impossible the direct self-government of large groups. If Peter wrongs Paul, it is out of the question that all the other citizens should hasten to the spot to undertake a personal examination of the matter in dispute and to take the part of Paul against Peter. By parity of reasoning, in the modern democratic state, it is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.

 

 

Adapted from R. Michels, Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchial Tendencies of Modern Democracy. In public domain. Published 1958 by The Free Press.

Question + Answer Choices

Consider that some groups are defined as “cults”; members of these extremist sects generally live communally and obey an authoritarian, charismatic leader. Assume that all cults have fewer than 100 members. This information would tend to weaken the passage’s assertions about the:

  • A.
    ability of great orators to sway large groups.
  • B.
    loss of personality and sense of responsibility.
  • C.
    difficulties involved in convening large assemblies.
  • D.
    reasonableness of small groups compared to large groups.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The information in the question only applies to the ability of orators to sway smaller groups; it has no impact on claims about the effect of orators on larger groups.
  2. The reference to “loss of personality and sense of responsibility” refers to the behavior of the individual in a large group, so the information about people’s behavior in a small group is irrelevant.
  3. The information in the question is irrelevant to passage claims about the difficulty involved in convening large assemblies.
  4. This is a Reasoning Beyond the Text question because it presents information that is beyond the scope of the passage and then asks you to consider  how this information would affect passage claims. The answer is D, because the question describes a small group in which people “obey an authoritarian, charismatic leader.” In the passage, however, the author claims that a “great multitude” is more likely to succumb to “unreflective enthusiasm . . . than is a small meeting” (paragraph 2). So if the cults that the question describes are small groups of fewer than 100 members, this would weaken passage claims about the more considered behavior of small groups when compared to larger ones.

Passage 1 (Questions 1-5)

The practical ideal of democracy consists in the self-government of the masses in conformity with the decisions of popular assemblies. But while this system limits the extension of the principle of delegation, it fails to provide any guarantee against the formation of an oligarchical ruling clique. Undoubtedly it deprives the natural leaders of their quality as functionaries, for this quality is transferred to the people themselves. The crowd, however, is always subject to suggestion, being readily influenced by the eloquence of popular orators; moreover, direct government by the people, admitting of no serious discussions or thoughtful deliberations, greatly facilitates sudden insurrections of all kinds by individuals who are exceptionally bold, energetic, and adroit.

It is easier to dominate a large crowd than a small audience. The adhesion of the crowd is tumultuous, summary, and unconditional. The most formidable argument against the sovereignty of the masses is, however, derived from the mechanical and technical impossibility of its realization. The sovereign masses are incapable of undertaking the most necessary resolutions. The impotence of direct democracy, like the power of indirect democracy, is a direct outcome of the influence of number.

It is obvious that a gigantic number of persons belonging to a unitary organization cannot do any practical work using a system of direct discussion. The regular holding of deliberative assemblies of a thousand members encounters the gravest difficulties in respect to room and distance; from the topographical point of view, such an assembly would become altogether impossible if the members numbered ten thousand. Even if we imagined the means of communication to become much better than those that now exist, how would it be possible to assemble such a multitude in a given place, at a stated time, and with the frequency demanded by the exigencies of political life? In addition must be considered the physiological impossibility even for the most powerful orators of making themselves heard by a crowd of ten thousand persons.

There are, however, other reasons of a technical and administrative character that render impossible the direct self-government of large groups. If Peter wrongs Paul, it is out of the question that all the other citizens should hasten to the spot to undertake a personal examination of the matter in dispute and to take the part of Paul against Peter. By parity of reasoning, in the modern democratic state, it is impossible for the collectivity to undertake the direct settlement of all the controversies that may arise.

 

 

Adapted from R. Michels, Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchial Tendencies of Modern Democracy. In public domain. Published 1958 by The Free Press.

Question + Answer Choices

When using the term “the sovereignty of the masses” (paragraph 4), the author seems to mean the ability of the masses to:

  • A.
    vote in elections that decide who their leaders will be.
  • B.
    make every decision concerning their governance.
  • C.
    discuss all the potential consequences of specific actions.
  • D.
    understand the difference between popular orators and natural leaders.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Option A refers to what the passage calls “indirect” democracy, and “the sovereignty of the masses” is another way of describing “direct” democracy.
  2. This is a Comprehension question, as it asks you to determine the meaning of a phrase as it is used in the context of the passage. The answer is B, because the author first refers to the “mechanical and technical impossibility of [the] realization” of the “sovereignty of the masses” (paragraph 4). He later states that some “technical and administrative” factors “render impossible the direct self-government of large groups” (final paragraph), and it is clear that this is another way of stating “the sovereignty of the masses.”
  3. The author claims that very large groups do a poor job of “serious discussions or thoughtful deliberations,” and there is no indication that “sovereignty of the masses” suggests that large groups are indeed able to engage in this kind of deliberation.
  4. The author suggests that large groups tend to be susceptible to popular orators, so it is unlikely that he implies that “the sovereignty of the masses” suggests that the masses can see popular orators for what they are.

Khan Academy Lessons
