Question 166 of 230

Data ID: 831592

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:31 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A 40-year-old patient developed a constant thirst and a frequent need to urinate. The attending physician suspected the patient had diabetes because in addition to these symptoms, the patient was overweight and had a family history of diabetes, which both are risk factors for the disease.

Diabetes mellitus is a condition in which the body cannot produce or is unable to effectively respond to insulin, a peptide hormone made in pancreatic β cells. Normally, insulin promotes facilitated diffusion of glucose into cells from the bloodstream by inducing the translocation of glucose transporter proteins to the plasma membrane. It also helps regulate the metabolism of fatty and amino acids.

In diabetes, the ability of many types of cells to take up glucose is compromised, leading to elevated blood glucose levels. Many tissues then metabolize fatty acids as an alternative energy source, which can lead to metabolic acidosis, a condition of low blood pH. Diabetes can also cause protein degradation, resulting in the release of excess amino acids that can be used as substrates for gluconeogenesis, which further increases blood glucose levels. There are two main types of diabetes. Many cases of Type 1 diabetes, in which the body is unable to produce insulin, are caused by an autoimmune response directed against pancreatic β cells. While the body often produces normal-to-elevated amounts of insulin in Type 2 diabetes, its ability to respond to insulin is compromised.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

During the production of insulin, the translated polypeptide is cleaved into the mature form and secreted from the cell. The cleavage most likely takes place in which of the following locations?

  • A.
    Nucleus
  • B.
    Ribosomes
  • C.
    Endomembrane system
  • D.
    Cytoplasm

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Insulin is not a transcription factor and its polypeptide is most likely not present in the nucleus.
  2. Insulin polypeptide is translated, not cleaved, in the ribosomes.
  3. The endomembrane system is the portion of the cells that is in charge of modifying proteins that will be secreted. Thus, it is most likely that insulin cleavage will occur in the endomembrane system.
  4. Insulin cleavage is most likely to occur in the endomembrane system, rather than the cytoplasm, because insulin is a secreted protein.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Plasma Membrane; Membrane-Bound Organelles and Defining Characteristics of Eukaryotic Cells, Tissues Formed From Eukaryotic Cells; and Cytoskeleton.

Question 167 of 230

Data ID: 831596

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:32 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A 40-year-old patient developed a constant thirst and a frequent need to urinate. The attending physician suspected the patient had diabetes because in addition to these symptoms, the patient was overweight and had a family history of diabetes, which both are risk factors for the disease.

Diabetes mellitus is a condition in which the body cannot produce or is unable to effectively respond to insulin, a peptide hormone made in pancreatic β cells. Normally, insulin promotes facilitated diffusion of glucose into cells from the bloodstream by inducing the translocation of glucose transporter proteins to the plasma membrane. It also helps regulate the metabolism of fatty and amino acids.

In diabetes, the ability of many types of cells to take up glucose is compromised, leading to elevated blood glucose levels. Many tissues then metabolize fatty acids as an alternative energy source, which can lead to metabolic acidosis, a condition of low blood pH. Diabetes can also cause protein degradation, resulting in the release of excess amino acids that can be used as substrates for gluconeogenesis, which further increases blood glucose levels. There are two main types of diabetes. Many cases of Type 1 diabetes, in which the body is unable to produce insulin, are caused by an autoimmune response directed against pancreatic β cells. While the body often produces normal-to-elevated amounts of insulin in Type 2 diabetes, its ability to respond to insulin is compromised.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Despite the effects of diabetes, the brains of diabetic patients still receive adequate nourishment. This is most likely because the brain uses:

  • A.
    less glucose than do other body tissues.
  • B.
    insulin-independent transporters for the uptake of glucose.
  • C.
    fatty acids for energy instead of glucose.
  • D.
    insulin-dependent transporters for the uptake of glucose.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The brain is the main consumer of glucose in the body.
  2. The brain cells contain glucose transporters that are insulin independent.
  3. Although under certain specific conditions the brain can use fuels other than glucose, under physiological conditions glucose is the main fuel for brain cells. Since the brains of diabetic patients receive an adequate amount of glucose, the brain must use an insulin-independent mechanism for glucose uptake
  4. If the brain cells had insulin-dependent transporters, they will not be functioning correctly in individuals affected by diabetes.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Nervous System: Structure and Function; Nerve Cell; and Endocrine System: Hormones and Their Sources, Endocrine System: Mechanisms of Hormone Action.

Question 168 of 230

Data ID: 831602

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:32 PM

Passage

Passage 10 (Questions 52-56)

A 40-year-old patient developed a constant thirst and a frequent need to urinate. The attending physician suspected the patient had diabetes because in addition to these symptoms, the patient was overweight and had a family history of diabetes, which both are risk factors for the disease.

Diabetes mellitus is a condition in which the body cannot produce or is unable to effectively respond to insulin, a peptide hormone made in pancreatic β cells. Normally, insulin promotes facilitated diffusion of glucose into cells from the bloodstream by inducing the translocation of glucose transporter proteins to the plasma membrane. It also helps regulate the metabolism of fatty and amino acids.

In diabetes, the ability of many types of cells to take up glucose is compromised, leading to elevated blood glucose levels. Many tissues then metabolize fatty acids as an alternative energy source, which can lead to metabolic acidosis, a condition of low blood pH. Diabetes can also cause protein degradation, resulting in the release of excess amino acids that can be used as substrates for gluconeogenesis, which further increases blood glucose levels. There are two main types of diabetes. Many cases of Type 1 diabetes, in which the body is unable to produce insulin, are caused by an autoimmune response directed against pancreatic β cells. While the body often produces normal-to-elevated amounts of insulin in Type 2 diabetes, its ability to respond to insulin is compromised.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Based on the passage, which of the following is LEAST likely to be a symptom of diabetes mellitus?

  • A.
    Loss of appetite
  • B.
    Sweet-tasting urine
  • C.
    Unexplained weight loss
  • D.
    Feelings of fatigue

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Based on the passage, diabetes affected individuals will use proteins and lipids as a source of glucose, thus increasing, rather than decreasing, their appetite.
  2. High levels of glucose are a symptom of diabetes. This is due to the fact that kidneys can reabsorb glucose up to 320 mg/min (tubular maximum). The levels of glucose in the filtrate that are in excess will pass into urine, leading to a sweet taste.
  3. Unexplained weight loss might occur as the body uses proteins and lipids as a source of energy.
  4. Feeling of fatigue might occur as the body uses amino acids and lipids as a source of energy.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Glycolysis, Gluconeogenesis, and the Pentose Phosphate Pathway; Citric Acid Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation; and Metabolism of Fatty Acids and Proteins.

Question 169 of 230

Data ID: 831609

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:33 PM

Question + Answer Choices

A certain bacterium was cultured for several generations in medium containing 15N, transferred to medium containing 14N, and allowed to complete two rounds of cell division. Given that the bacterium’s genome mass is 5.4 fg when grown in 14N media and 5.5 fg when grown in 15N medium, individual bacteria with which of the following genome masses would most likely be isolated from this culture?

  • A.
    5.4 fg only
  • B.
    5.4 fg and 5.45 fg
  • C.
    5.4 fg and 5.5 fg
  • D.
    5.45 fg only

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. This would be the genome mass if the bacterium was cultured only in 14N.
  2. DNA replication occurs in a semiconservative way and, after two rounds of bacterial growth, there will be bacteria containing only 14N and bacteria containing the same amount of 15N and 14N. Thus, the genome mass will be 5.4 fg for the bacteria that only contain 14N, and 5.45 fg for the bacteria that contain equal levels of 14N and 15N.
  3. This would be the genome mass if the bacteria were cultured only with 15N or 14N separately. The two rounds of division with 14N will generate bacteria that contain 14N and 15N, as well as bacteria that only contain 14N.
  4. Based on the semiconservative method of DNA replication, after two rounds of cell division some bacteria will only contain 14N (genome mass 5.4 fg) and some others will contain a mix of 14N and 15N (genome mass 5.45 fg), thus the genome mass of the entire culture cannot be only 5.45 fg.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Cell Theory; Classification and Structure of Prokaryotic Cells, Growth and Physiology of Prokaryotic Cells; and Virus Structure, Viral Life Cycle.

Question 170 of 230

Data ID: 831616

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:33 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Assume that K and M are two unlinked genes that affect hearing. The dominant K allele is necessary for hearing, and the dominant M allele causes deafness regardless of the other genes present. Given this, what fraction of the offspring of parents with the genotypes KkMm and Kkmm will most likely be deaf?

  • A.
    1/4
  • B.
    3/8
  • C.
    1/2
  • D.
    5/8

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This would be correct if both individuals were heterozygous for K and homozygous recessive for M.
  2. There are no possible genotype combination that will result in this ratio.
  3. This would be correct if one parent was Kkmm and the other kkmm, or if one parent was KkMm and the other was KKmm.
  4. Based on the possible allele combination, out of 16 possible combinations, there will be ten individuals that will be hearing impaired, carrying either one allele M or the two kk. Thus 10/16 = 5/8 individuals.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Evidence that DNA is Genetic Material; Mendelian Concepts, Analytic Methods; Meiosis and Other Factors Affecting Genetic Variability; and Evolution.

Question 171 of 230

Data ID: 831618

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:34 PM

Question + Answer Choices

An RNA molecule has 1500 bases. What is the maximum number of amino acids it can encode?

  • A.
    500
  • B.
    1000
  • C.
    1500
  • D.
    4500

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Each amino acid is coded by a triplet of nucleobases. Thus, ​​ amino acids.
  2. This is the number of amino acids coded by a sequence of 3000 bases, not 1500 bases.
  3. This is the number of amino acids coded by a sequence of 4500 bases, not 1500 bases.
  4. This is the number of amino acids coded by a sequence of 13500 bases, not 1500 bases.

Khan Academy Lessons

Question 172 of 230

Data ID: 831643

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:35 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-4)

Categorical bias refers to people’s tendency to judge the physical distance between objects from the same category as being smaller compared to the distance between objects from different categories. Seven-, nine-, and eleven-year-old children and a group of adults were recruited to participate in a study of categorical bias. In the training phase, each participant was presented with 20 objects in a box and was asked to study the location of these objects. The training objects were selected from four categories: vehicles, animals, food, and clothing. There were two different types of training conditions. The participants in the simultaneous condition were presented with all the training objects all at once and were asked to study their locations simultaneously. The participants in the serial condition were presented with the training objects one at a time.

Testing began immediately after the training. The objects were removed from the box, and the participants were asked to position the objects in the empty box exactly as they were arranged in the training phase.

The researchers were interested in whether the participants showed categorical bias, which was measured by central placement error. To assess this, the researchers first calculated the central point between objects of the same category based on their original locations during training. The central placement error was determined based on the errors participants made in placing objects from the same category nearer to the central point than their original locations. The results of the experiment are shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Average central placement error by age and type of presentation

 

 

Adapted from K. M. Recker and J. M. Plumert ©2008 Taylor & Francis Group.

Question + Answer Choices

Compared to the simultaneous condition, the serial condition of the experiment would be more likely to cause:

  • A.
    a primacy effect.
  • B.
    a state dependency effect.
  • C.
    a misinformation effect.
  • D.
    a dual-coding effect.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. In the serial condition, the objects are presented to the participants one at a time, which might result in better memory for events at the beginning of the series (a primacy effect).
  2. A state dependency effect refers to the benefit of a match between the conditions (in particular, the subject's mental and emotional state) under which information is encoded into memory and the conditions present when the subject attempts to retrieve that information.
  3. Misinformation effects refer to memory errors (usually errors of commission), in which some information introduced and encoded after the target information is retrieved along with some portions of the target information. In such cases, the subject usually has trouble identifying which retrieved information had been originally encoded and which information was introduced subsequently, a situation known as source confusion.
  4. Dual-coding refers to the retrieval advantage of verbal items that are imageable. These items are encoded with both a verbal code and a visual code, which benefits their retrievability.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 173 of 230

Data ID: 831651

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:35 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-4)

Categorical bias refers to people’s tendency to judge the physical distance between objects from the same category as being smaller compared to the distance between objects from different categories. Seven-, nine-, and eleven-year-old children and a group of adults were recruited to participate in a study of categorical bias. In the training phase, each participant was presented with 20 objects in a box and was asked to study the location of these objects. The training objects were selected from four categories: vehicles, animals, food, and clothing. There were two different types of training conditions. The participants in the simultaneous condition were presented with all the training objects all at once and were asked to study their locations simultaneously. The participants in the serial condition were presented with the training objects one at a time.

Testing began immediately after the training. The objects were removed from the box, and the participants were asked to position the objects in the empty box exactly as they were arranged in the training phase.

The researchers were interested in whether the participants showed categorical bias, which was measured by central placement error. To assess this, the researchers first calculated the central point between objects of the same category based on their original locations during training. The central placement error was determined based on the errors participants made in placing objects from the same category nearer to the central point than their original locations. The results of the experiment are shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Average central placement error by age and type of presentation

 

 

Adapted from K. M. Recker and J. M. Plumert ©2008 Taylor & Francis Group.

Question + Answer Choices

The researchers change the procedure such that instead of placing the objects in a box, the participants have to recall all the objects that they have seen during training. According to the spreading of activation theory, which type of memory error is most likely?

  • A.
    Making source monitoring errors regarding the location of the training objects
  • B.
    Poorer memory for the training objects seen at the later points in the experiment
  • C.
    Selective forgetting of the training objects that were placed in the center of the box
  • D.
    Recalling objects that were not presented but are from the same category as the training objects

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Source monitoring refers to a subject's ability to retrieve the details of the situation extant when memory items were encoded. More specifically, when some additional information is introduced after the original encoding, this post-event information can be mistakenly included in recall of the original event, leading to a misinformation effect.
  2. This option is incorrect for two reasons. First, poorer memory for items seen later is unlikely, because it would entail the absence of a recency effect (i.e., items at the end of a list are generally recalled better than items presented in the middle). Second, spreading activation would not predict such an outcome.
  3. There is no evidence to suggest the validity of such selective forgetting based on specific spatial locations.
  4. Spreading activation suggests that, when the representation of a concept is activated in memory, the activation spreads to concepts that are semantically or associatively related to it. Thus, people often retrieve unpresented members of a category when tested on their memory for a series of presented concepts from that category.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 174 of 230

Data ID: 831662

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:36 PM

Passage

Passage 1 (Questions 1-4)

Categorical bias refers to people’s tendency to judge the physical distance between objects from the same category as being smaller compared to the distance between objects from different categories. Seven-, nine-, and eleven-year-old children and a group of adults were recruited to participate in a study of categorical bias. In the training phase, each participant was presented with 20 objects in a box and was asked to study the location of these objects. The training objects were selected from four categories: vehicles, animals, food, and clothing. There were two different types of training conditions. The participants in the simultaneous condition were presented with all the training objects all at once and were asked to study their locations simultaneously. The participants in the serial condition were presented with the training objects one at a time.

Testing began immediately after the training. The objects were removed from the box, and the participants were asked to position the objects in the empty box exactly as they were arranged in the training phase.

The researchers were interested in whether the participants showed categorical bias, which was measured by central placement error. To assess this, the researchers first calculated the central point between objects of the same category based on their original locations during training. The central placement error was determined based on the errors participants made in placing objects from the same category nearer to the central point than their original locations. The results of the experiment are shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Average central placement error by age and type of presentation

 

 

Adapted from K. M. Recker and J. M. Plumert ©2008 Taylor & Francis Group.

Question + Answer Choices

The findings described in the passage suggest that memory for locations:

  • A.
    is comparable to adults by nine years of age.
  • B.
    is influenced by categorical knowledge.
  • C.
    becomes more accurate with age.
  • D.
    relies solely on recall of distances.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The nine-year-olds in fact performed differently from adults.
  2. The results showed that categorical bias was observed in adults.
  3. The fact that categorical bias was present in adults, hence spatial memory does not necessarily become more accurate with age.
  4. Because world knowledge about categories did not affect people's judgments of distance, the findings do not support the conclusion that spatial memory relies solely on the recall of distances.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question + Answer Choices

The children from the experiment in the passage participate in a separate study using Piaget's water conservation task. They are shown two identical beakers, containing equal amounts of water. The water from one of the containers is poured into a thinner and taller beaker. Which prediction is most likely to be confirmed?

  • A.
    All three age groups will state that the water in the taller beaker is greater in quantity.
  • B.
    The majority of the 11-year-olds will state that the amount of water in the taller beaker is the same as in the original beaker.
  • C.
    The majority of the 9- and 11-year-olds will state that the amount of water in the taller beaker is greater in quantity.
  • D.
    The majority of the 7-year-olds will state that the amount of water in the taller beaker is the same as in the original beaker.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Most of the 7-year-olds and many 9-year-olds will fail at tests of conservation.
  2. According to Piaget's theory of cognitive development, mastery of conservation is the criterion for entering the concrete operations stage, which starts at approximately 7 years and ends around 11 to 12 years. The vast majority of the 11-year-olds will have entered the concrete operational stage. Thus, they will be able to judge that pouring water into the taller beaker does not change its quantity.
  3. The concrete operations stage starts at approximately 7 years and ends around 11 to 12 years. Solving conservation problems is the criterion for entering this stage. Most of the 9-year-olds will have developed the ability to solve conservation problems.
  4. The concrete operations stage starts at approximately 7 years and ends around 11 to 12 years. Solving conservation problems is the criterion for entering this stage. Although some 7-year-old children already may have developed the ability to solve conservation problems, many will not.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 176 of 230

Data ID: 831681

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:37 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Maintenance Factor 1 is most closely related to which approach to psychological disorders?

  • A.
    Psychodynamic
  • B.
    Behaviorist
  • C.
    Trait
  • D.
    Humanistic

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Maintenance Factor 1 is centered on classical conditioning, which is completely unrelated to psychodynamic theory.
  2. Maintenance Factor 1 focuses on the role of classical conditioning, which is closely related to the behaviorist approach.
  3. Trait theory refers to an approach to personality. Maintenance Factor 1 is concerned with classical conditioning, which is completely unrelated to the trait approach.
  4. Maintenance Factor 1 is concerned with classical conditioning, which is completely unrelated to the humanistic approach.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 177 of 230

Data ID: 831686

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:38 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

According to Maintenance Factor 1, mild changes in bodily sensations act as:

  • A.
    unconditioned stimuli.
  • B.
    conditioned stimuli.
  • C.
    unconditioned responses.
  • D.
    conditioned responses.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Mild changes in bodily sensations become conditioned stimuli (CS), NOT unconditioned stimuli. These stimuli become associated with excessive panic. The excessive panic is the unconditioned stimulus (US).
  2. According to Maintenance Factor 1, mild changes in bodily sensations become cues that are associated with excessive panic. Those mild changes in bodily sensations become conditioned stimuli, which come to elicit the conditioned fear response.
  3. Mild changes in bodily sensations become conditioned stimuli, which become associated with excessive panic, which is the unconditioned stimulus. The excessive panic is NOT the unconditioned response.
  4. Mild changes in bodily sensations become conditioned stimuli, which elicit the conditioned fear response. But stimuli are not responses.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Habituation and Dishabituation, Associative Learning, Observational Learning and Theories of Attitude and Behavior Change.

Question 178 of 230

Data ID: 831701

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:38 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

For Patient 2, panic attacks act as:

  • A.
    discriminatory stimuli.
  • B.
    signaling stimuli.
  • C.
    positive punishers.
  • D.
    negative punishers.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Discriminatory stimuli allow an organism to tell whether an appetitive stimulus or an aversive stimulus is forthcoming in an operant conditioning situation.
  2. Signaling stimuli are neutral stimuli that may potentially become conditioned stimuli.
  3. Positive punishment refers to the decrease in the frequency of a response resulting from the introduction of an aversive stimulus following that response. The patient describes the panic attacks as highly aversive and mentions that he no longer goes to meetings for fear of a panic attack. Because the frequency of the patient's attendance at meetings has decreased as the result of the panic attacks, the panic attacks have acted as positive punishers.
  4. Negative punishment refers to the decrease in the frequency of a response resulting from the removal of an appetitive stimulus following that response. The patient describes the panic attacks as highly aversive. In addition, they are introduced, rather than removed. Thus, the panic attacks do not match either of the criteria for negative punishers.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Habituation and Dishabituation, Associative Learning, Observational Learning and Theories of Attitude and Behavior Change.

Question 179 of 230

Data ID: 831711

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:39 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Interoceptive awareness involves sensitivity to increases in the activity of the:

  • A.
    reticular activation system.
  • B.
    autonomic nervous system.
  • C.
    limbic system.
  • D.
    somatic nervous system.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The reticular formation (RF), deep in the brainstem, is concerned with functions involving arousal, particularly the sleep-wake cycle, and attention. Changes in the RF's activity level are not described as "interoceptive."
  2. Interoceptive awareness is described as increased sensitivity to internal bodily sensations, such as heartbeat and blood pressure. These functions are regulated by the autonomic nervous system.
  3. The limbic system consists of brain structures, such as the thalamus, hypothalamus, and hippocampus. Changes in the activity levels of these structures are generally not sensed and, therefore, we are not aware of such changes. In addition, such changes would not be considered "interoceptive."
  4. The somatic nervous system is the division of the peripheral nervous system that controls sensory and motor functions of effectors that enable the organism to deal with its external environment. It does not function in the ability to sense interoceptive changes.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 180 of 230

Data ID: 831715

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:40 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Which two processes best summarize maintenance factors 1 and 2, respectively?

  • A.
    Operant conditioning and top-down processes
  • B.
    Classical conditioning and affective processes
  • C.
    Operant conditioning and physiological processes
  • D.
    Classical conditioning and cognitive processes

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Maintenance Factor 1 focuses on classical (not operant) conditioning. Maintenance Factor 2 focuses on the cognitive appraisal of bodily sensations, not on any cognitive control of those sensations.
  2. Although it is true that Maintenance Factor 1 focuses on classical conditioning of a fear response to bodily sensations, Maintenance Factor 2 did not involve affective (i.e., emotional) processing.
  3. Maintenance Factor 1 focuses on classical conditioning, not operant conditioning. Maintenance Factor 2 focuses on cognitive appraisal of bodily sensations, not the physiological processes behind those sensations.
  4. Maintenance Factor 1 focuses on classical conditioning of a fear response to bodily sensations, whereas Maintenance Factor 2 focuses on cognitive appraisal (interpretation) of bodily sensations.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 181 of 230

Data ID: 831727

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:40 PM

Passage

Passage 2 (Questions 5-10)

Panic attacks co-occur with various other psychological disorders. Panic disorder (PD) is diagnosed when a patient is preoccupied with the possibility of panic attacks and experiences behavioral changes as a result of this preoccupation.

The following scenarios demonstrate two different patients’ responses to the question, “Do you worry about the panic attacks that you experience?”

Patient 1: No. They are somewhat unpleasant, but they are the least of my concerns. I am more worried about my daughter and our future. I don’t get any sleep most nights. I just lie awake feeling nervous about what would happen to my daughter if something happened to me and my husband.

Patient 2: I am terrified of having a panic attack at a meeting. I dread the thought of others noticing how nervous I am. I’m worried that others will think I am weird. I don’t go to meetings anymore.

Studies have identified temperament as a risk factor for PD. Specifically, patients who are diagnosed with PD tend to score high on neuroticism. Patients with PD also tend to have high interoceptive awareness (a heightened ability to detect bodily sensations of arousal, such as increases in heartbeat or blood pressure).

According to some researchers, interoceptive awareness contributes to maintenance factors for PD. Specifically, patients with high interoceptive awareness are likely to associate mild changes in bodily sensations (such as slightly heightened heartbeat) with the panic they experienced during panic attacks; this leads to conditioned fear in response to changes in bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 1). Another maintenance factor for PD is the misappraisal of bodily sensations (Maintenance Factor 2), such as interpreting bodily sensations as signs of imminent death or loss of control. Some researchers suggest that such misappraisals are largely responsible for the excessive panic and fear that PD patients experience during a panic attack.

 

 

Question + Answer Choices

Data on the role of Maintenance Factor 1 on PD is LEAST likely to come from which type of research?

  • A.
    Correlational studies
  • B.
    Case studies
  • C.
    Longitudinal studies
  • D.
    Experimental studies

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Most of the data on the course of a disorder like PD are going to come from correlational research.
  2. Case studies of individuals, and groups of individuals, with PD are entirely possible.
  3. The developmental course of a disorder like PD can appropriately be studied in longitudinal studies.
  4. It would be extremely difficult to systematically manipulate participants' physiological states and their sensitivity to changes in those states. In addition, random assignment to various conditions involving maintenance of PD would pose insurmountable ethical restrictions. For these reasons, it is very unlikely that experimental research could be designed to study the role of Maintenance Factor 1 on PD.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Habituation and Dishabituation, Associative Learning, Observational Learning and Theories of Attitude and Behavior Change.

Question 182 of 230

Data ID: 831736

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:41 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Harlequin ichthyosis, a rare genetic disorder, causes the skin to become thick and scaly. Flaking skin behind the eyelids of individuals with this condition is most likely to damage which structure of the eye?

  • A.
    Choroid
  • B.
    Cornea
  • C.
    Lens
  • D.
    Retina

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The choroid does not form the outermost layer of the eyeball, but instead is located deep to the sclera. Therefore, the choroid is unlikely to be significantly damaged by flaking skin off the back of the eyelids.
  2. The cornea forms the outermost layer of the front of the eye, in which it directly contacts the back of the eyelids. Due to this direct interaction, the cornea is highly susceptible to damage caused by flaking skin from the back of the eyelids.
  3. While the lens is positioned toward the front of the eye, it is located deep when compared to the cornea. Therefore, compared to the lens, the cornea will be more likely to be damaged by flaking skin from the back of the eyelids.
  4. The retina forms the innermost layer along the posterior portion of the eye. Therefore, the retina is unlikely to be significantly damaged by flaking skin off the back of the eyelids.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sensory Processing; Vision; Hearing; Somatosensation; and Taste and Smell.

Question 183 of 230

Data ID: 831741

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:41 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Sound-induced vibrations depolarize hair cells of the cochlea by opening ion channels that are gated in what way?

  • A.
    Chemically
  • B.
    Mechanically
  • C.
    Electrically
  • D.
    Synaptically

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Chemically gated ion channels require the binding of a molecule to the ion channel, causing it to open. This is observed during olfaction and gustation, but not during the activation of hair cells, which occurs upon the physical perturbation of the hair cell membrane.
  2. Sound wave-induced vibration of hair cells generates tension within the cell membrane that directly activate ion channels responsible for auditory signaling. This type of ion channel is a mechanically-gated channel, such that upon its activation, it generates a depolarizing current within the cell.
  3. Electrically gated ion channels activate upon a change in membrane potential. Activity of electrically gated ion channels is associated with action potential propagation, but not vibration of the hair cell membrane.
  4. While some ion channels are localized to the synapse, synaptic gating is not a mechanism by which ion channels can be activated.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Sensory Processing; Vision; Hearing; Somatosensation; and Taste and Smell.

Question 184 of 230

Data ID: 831744

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:42 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Max attends a party and does not make eye contact with, or approach, his acquaintance, Sam. According to the actor–observer bias, which graph best represents how Max and Sam view this behavior?

  • A.
  • B.
  • C.
  • D.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. According to the actor-observer bias, actors will attribute their own bad behavior to situational factors (not feeling well), whereas observers will attribute actors’ behavior to dispositional factors (social awkwardness).
  2. This figure shows the opposite pattern of attributions for the observer's and actor's behavior than the pattern described as the actor-observer bias.
  3. This figure shows no difference in the attributions of the observer's and actor's behavior. Both are shown as dispositional attributions.
  4. This figure inaccurately shows a situational attribution for both the observer's and the actor's behavior.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attributing Behavior to Persons or Situations; Prejudice and Bias; and Processes Related to Stereotypes.

Question 185 of 230

Data ID: 831751

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:43 PM

Question + Answer Choices

In a study, each trial involves administering a drop of lemon juice to the participant’s tongue and measuring the participant’s level of salivation. As more trials are conducted, the researcher finds that the magnitude of salivation declines. After a certain point, the researcher switches to administering lime juice. This researcher is most likely studying which process?

  • A.
    Sensory perception
  • B.
    Habituation and dishabituation
  • C.
    Stimulus generalization in classical conditioning
  • D.
    Conditioned responses in classical conditioning

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The study of sensory perception usually involves either a psychophysics paradigm (involving thresholds) or a signal detection theory paradigm (involving signal-to-noise ratio and response criterion), which were not described in the stem.
  2. The study involves reduced responding to a repeating stimulus, which is best described as habituation. The researcher then changes the stimulus, which will likely lead to dishabituation.
  3. No presentation of a conditioned stimulus (CS) was described in the stem.
  4. Since no CS was presented along with the Unconditioned CS (citrus juice), the stem does not describe a classical conditioning paradigm.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Habituation and Dishabituation, Associative Learning, Observational Learning and Theories of Attitude and Behavior Change.

Question 186 of 230

Data ID: 831822

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:43 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 15-18)

Research has indicated that stereotypes associated with a social group can affect the performance of its members. To counteract this effect, researchers have been trying to understand the mechanism behind it. In the United States, women are stereotyped as having lower math ability. Asian Americans, on the other hand, are stereotyped as having high math ability. Study 1 was designed to investigate the math performance of Asian American women, who have two conflicting stereotypes associated with their identity. The participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups. In the gender identity condition, the participants were asked to indicate their gender and answered personal questions related to their gender identity. In the ethnic identity condition, they indicated their ethnicity and answered personal questions related to their ethnic identity. In the control condition, the participants were not asked to indicate their gender or ethnicity, but answered general questions about their background. After answering the questions, all three groups worked on a math test. Findings from this study are represented in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Math performance as a function of experimental condition

 

Stereotypes associated with social groups are not consistent across cultures. In Canada, women are stereotyped to have lower math ability, but Asian Canadians are not stereotyped to have higher math ability. Study 2 attempted to test whether the difference in stereotypes would affect the math performance of Asian Canadian women. The same procedure that was used for Study 1 was used in Study 2, using Asian Canadian women as participants.

 

 

Adapted from M. Shih, T. L. Pittinsky, and N. Ambady, ©1999 American Psychological Society.

Question + Answer Choices

Which explanation of the results of Study 1 presents the most appropriate application of the optimal arousal theory? Reminding participants of:

  • A.
    their membership in a stereotyped group leads to optimal arousal, which results in varying levels of performance depending on ability.
  • B.
    their membership in a stereotyped group leads to fluctuating arousal and results in poor performance.
  • C.
    a negative stereotype associated with their identity increases arousal beyond what is optimal and leads to poor performance.
  • D.
    a positive stereotype associated with them eliminates arousal completely, which results in optimal performance.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The theory does not predict that arousal interacts with any factor other than the difficulty (or complexity) of the task. In particular, arousal and ability are not posited to interact.
  2. The theory does not say anything about fluctuating arousal levels.
  3. Optimal arousal theory states that optimal performance requires optimal arousal and that arousal levels that are too high or too low will impede performance. This option implies that the negative stereotype will cause poor performance by increasing arousal levels beyond the optimal level.
  4. The theory does not state that the suggestion of a positive stereotype will eliminate, or even reduce, arousal levels.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 187 of 230

Data ID: 831828

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:44 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 15-18)

Research has indicated that stereotypes associated with a social group can affect the performance of its members. To counteract this effect, researchers have been trying to understand the mechanism behind it. In the United States, women are stereotyped as having lower math ability. Asian Americans, on the other hand, are stereotyped as having high math ability. Study 1 was designed to investigate the math performance of Asian American women, who have two conflicting stereotypes associated with their identity. The participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups. In the gender identity condition, the participants were asked to indicate their gender and answered personal questions related to their gender identity. In the ethnic identity condition, they indicated their ethnicity and answered personal questions related to their ethnic identity. In the control condition, the participants were not asked to indicate their gender or ethnicity, but answered general questions about their background. After answering the questions, all three groups worked on a math test. Findings from this study are represented in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Math performance as a function of experimental condition

 

Stereotypes associated with social groups are not consistent across cultures. In Canada, women are stereotyped to have lower math ability, but Asian Canadians are not stereotyped to have higher math ability. Study 2 attempted to test whether the difference in stereotypes would affect the math performance of Asian Canadian women. The same procedure that was used for Study 1 was used in Study 2, using Asian Canadian women as participants.

 

 

Adapted from M. Shih, T. L. Pittinsky, and N. Ambady, ©1999 American Psychological Society.

Question + Answer Choices

If the participants in Study 1 differed in the degree to which their ethnic identity was a central part of their self-concept, which outcome would be most likely? Participants whose ethnic identity was a central part of their self-concepts would:

  • A.
    be more affected by the gender stereotypes regarding their math performance.
  • B.
    not be affected by the negative stereotype regarding women’s math ability.
  • C.
    be more affected by the positive stereotype regarding Asian Americans’ math ability.
  • D.
    perform the same regardless of which condition they were placed in.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. There is no reason that someone whose ethic identity is central to their self-concept would be more affected by gender stereotypes or affected by the conditions of the experiment.
  2. There is no reason to suppose that someone whose ethic identity is central to their self-concept would be unaffected by gender stereotypes.
  3. People whose ethnic identity is a central part of their self-concept would be more prone to the effects of stereotype threat regarding their ethnic identity.
  4. There is no reason that someone whose ethic identity is central to their self-concept would not be affected by the conditions of the experiment.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Self-Concept, Self-Identity, and Social Identity, Formation of Identity.

Question 188 of 230

Data ID: 831833

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:45 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 15-18)

Research has indicated that stereotypes associated with a social group can affect the performance of its members. To counteract this effect, researchers have been trying to understand the mechanism behind it. In the United States, women are stereotyped as having lower math ability. Asian Americans, on the other hand, are stereotyped as having high math ability. Study 1 was designed to investigate the math performance of Asian American women, who have two conflicting stereotypes associated with their identity. The participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups. In the gender identity condition, the participants were asked to indicate their gender and answered personal questions related to their gender identity. In the ethnic identity condition, they indicated their ethnicity and answered personal questions related to their ethnic identity. In the control condition, the participants were not asked to indicate their gender or ethnicity, but answered general questions about their background. After answering the questions, all three groups worked on a math test. Findings from this study are represented in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Math performance as a function of experimental condition

 

Stereotypes associated with social groups are not consistent across cultures. In Canada, women are stereotyped to have lower math ability, but Asian Canadians are not stereotyped to have higher math ability. Study 2 attempted to test whether the difference in stereotypes would affect the math performance of Asian Canadian women. The same procedure that was used for Study 1 was used in Study 2, using Asian Canadian women as participants.

 

 

Adapted from M. Shih, T. L. Pittinsky, and N. Ambady, ©1999 American Psychological Society.

Question + Answer Choices

Which measure would be most useful if the researchers were interested in the degree of sympathetic arousal experienced in the different conditions of Study 1?

  • A.
    A measure of electrical conductivity of the skin
  • B.
    A CT scan of the hindbrain
  • C.
    A PET scan of the parietal cortex
  • D.
    A measure of melatonin levels

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Increases in the electrical conductivity of the skin is a physiological indication of increased sympathetic arousal, which is associated with anxiety.
  2. Brain imaging is very unlikely to provide an indication of the level of sympathetic arousal and the hindbrain is not involved in anxiety.
  3. Brain imaging of the cerebral lobes will not provide an indication of sympathetic arousal.
  4. Melatonin is a hormone (involved in the sleep-wake cycle) secreted by the pineal gland. Levels of this hormone will not provide any indication of the level of sympathetic arousal.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 189 of 230

Data ID: 831838

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:45 PM

Passage

Passage 3 (Questions 15-18)

Research has indicated that stereotypes associated with a social group can affect the performance of its members. To counteract this effect, researchers have been trying to understand the mechanism behind it. In the United States, women are stereotyped as having lower math ability. Asian Americans, on the other hand, are stereotyped as having high math ability. Study 1 was designed to investigate the math performance of Asian American women, who have two conflicting stereotypes associated with their identity. The participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups. In the gender identity condition, the participants were asked to indicate their gender and answered personal questions related to their gender identity. In the ethnic identity condition, they indicated their ethnicity and answered personal questions related to their ethnic identity. In the control condition, the participants were not asked to indicate their gender or ethnicity, but answered general questions about their background. After answering the questions, all three groups worked on a math test. Findings from this study are represented in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Math performance as a function of experimental condition

 

Stereotypes associated with social groups are not consistent across cultures. In Canada, women are stereotyped to have lower math ability, but Asian Canadians are not stereotyped to have higher math ability. Study 2 attempted to test whether the difference in stereotypes would affect the math performance of Asian Canadian women. The same procedure that was used for Study 1 was used in Study 2, using Asian Canadian women as participants.

 

 

Adapted from M. Shih, T. L. Pittinsky, and N. Ambady, ©1999 American Psychological Society.

Question + Answer Choices

Which conclusion is supported by Figure 1?

  • A.
    Priming stereotypes always has a negative impact on performance.
  • B.
    Priming a negative stereotype interferes with performance.
  • C.
    Priming a positive stereotype does not affect performance.
  • D.
    Priming stereotypes does not affect performance as much as one’s self-concept.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Priming of positive stereotypes facilitates performance. The figure shows that participants performance improved after they were reminded of their ethnic identity.
  2. The figure indicates that participants performed worse after being reminded of their gender identity. This reminder may serve to prime the negative stereotype about performance in the math task.
  3. The figure indicates that participants' performance improved after they were reminded of their ethnic identity. This reminder may serve to prime the positive stereotype about performance in the math task.
  4. The figure indicates that subjects asked to describe their gender identity performed worse, and those who described their ethnic identity performed better, compared to the controls who did not describe either their gender or ethnic identity. Thus, being reminded of either a positive or negative stereotype about math performance affected subjects' performance.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attributing Behavior to Persons or Situations; Prejudice and Bias; and Processes Related to Stereotypes.

Question 190 of 230

Data ID: 831867

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:46 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 19-22)

Studies have shown that the availability of social support, before and after exposure to a stressor, is associated with fewer stress responses. Based on these findings, a research group hypothesized that when exposed to stressors, people will be more inclined to engage in prosocial behaviors (such as sharing and trusting) as a coping mechanism. To test this hypothesis, college-aged male participants were recruited. All the participants were given an anxiety assessment, and five participants who reported unusually high levels of anxiety were dropped. The remaining participants were randomly assigned to either the stress group or the control group. In the first phase of the experiment, the stress group was asked to study a set of slides and give a public presentation, a condition that is considered a social stressor. The control group was asked to study the same set of slides as the stress group but did not have to give a public presentation.

In the second phase of the experiment, the participants were told that they would be playing a “trust game” with another player. According to the trust game, each participant could choose between two monetary rewards: walking away with $30 or choosing to share $120 with another player. Sharing required trusting the other player because the other player would decide how the $120 would be divided. If the other player was fair, he could choose to divide the money into equal halves; in which case, the participant would get $60. If the other player wasn’t fair, he could keep the $120, and the participant would get $0. The results showed that the participants who had been in the stress condition were more likely to trust the other player than those who had been in the control condition. The researchers concluded that their findings supported the hypothesis that stress promotes prosocial behavior. However, the experiment has been criticized for not accounting for the possibility that stress might lead to an increase in risk-taking rather than an increase in prosocial behavior.

 

 

Adapted from B. von Dawans, U. Fischbacher, C. Kirschbaum, E. Fehr, & M. Heinrichs. ©2012 the Authors.

Question + Answer Choices

The experiment was replicated with the addition of another variable: when playing the trust game, the participants were told either that the other player was a student at their university or was a student at a rival university. Based on the findings in the passage and research on in-group and out-group dynamics, which outcome is most likely?

  • A.
  • B.
  • C.
  • D.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. This figure shows the reverse of what has been observed in research on group dynamics, namely that people are more likely to trust in-group members than out-group members. In addition, this figure shows no effect of stress level, whereas the study described in the passage suggested that stress caused participants to be more trusting of the other player.
  2. This figure shows no effect of stress level. The study described in the passage suggested that stress caused participants to be more trusting of the other player.
  3. Research on group dynamics has shown that people are more likely to trust in-group members than out-group members. The study described in the passage suggested that stress caused participants to be more trusting of the other player.
  4. This figure shows no difference between the levels of trust in members of an in-group and members of an out-group. Studies of group dynamics show that people are more likely to trust in-group members than out-group members.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Self-Concept, Self-Identity, and Social Identity, Formation of Identity.

Correct
A
B
C
✓
D

Question 191 of 230

Data ID: 831872

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:46 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 19-22)

Studies have shown that the availability of social support, before and after exposure to a stressor, is associated with fewer stress responses. Based on these findings, a research group hypothesized that when exposed to stressors, people will be more inclined to engage in prosocial behaviors (such as sharing and trusting) as a coping mechanism. To test this hypothesis, college-aged male participants were recruited. All the participants were given an anxiety assessment, and five participants who reported unusually high levels of anxiety were dropped. The remaining participants were randomly assigned to either the stress group or the control group. In the first phase of the experiment, the stress group was asked to study a set of slides and give a public presentation, a condition that is considered a social stressor. The control group was asked to study the same set of slides as the stress group but did not have to give a public presentation.

In the second phase of the experiment, the participants were told that they would be playing a “trust game” with another player. According to the trust game, each participant could choose between two monetary rewards: walking away with $30 or choosing to share $120 with another player. Sharing required trusting the other player because the other player would decide how the $120 would be divided. If the other player was fair, he could choose to divide the money into equal halves; in which case, the participant would get $60. If the other player wasn’t fair, he could keep the $120, and the participant would get $0. The results showed that the participants who had been in the stress condition were more likely to trust the other player than those who had been in the control condition. The researchers concluded that their findings supported the hypothesis that stress promotes prosocial behavior. However, the experiment has been criticized for not accounting for the possibility that stress might lead to an increase in risk-taking rather than an increase in prosocial behavior.

 

 

Adapted from B. von Dawans, U. Fischbacher, C. Kirschbaum, E. Fehr, & M. Heinrichs. ©2012 the Authors.

Question + Answer Choices

The researchers collected data on participants' heart rates and electrical skin conductance at different stages of the experiment. Which pattern would verify that the independent variable had the intended effect? Heart rate and skin conductance are higher:

  • A.
    for all the participants before they participate in the first phase of the experiment compared to after.
  • B.
    for the participants who chose to share $120 compared to those who chose to walk away with $30.
  • C.
    for the participants in the stress group than for the participants in the control group.
  • D.
    for all the participants before they participate in the second phase of the experiment compared to after.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The independent variable was whether participants were exposed to a stressor (i.e., being told that they would give a public presentation). Elevated heart rate and skin conductance are part of the stress response. Therefore, participants in the stress condition (but not those in the control condition) were expected to show higher levels of these dependent measures. Therefore, measuring heart rate and skin conductance before and after participation for all the participants may not verify the effect of independent variable.
  2. Whether participants in either condition would display greater levels of trust was the research question. The independent variable was whether participants were exposed to a stressor (i.e., being told that they would give a public presentation). Elevated heart rate and skin conductance are part of the stress response, which is associated with prosocial behavior.
  3. The independent variable was whether participants were exposed to a stressor (i.e., being told that they would give a public presentation). Elevated heart rate and skin conductance are part of the stress response. Therefore, participants in the stress condition, but not those in the control condition, were expected to show higher levels of these dependent measures.
  4. All participants (in both the stress and control conditions) participated in the second phase of the experiment. The independent variable was whether participants were exposed to a stressor (i.e., being told that they would give a public presentation). Elevated heart rate and skin conductance are part of the stress response. Therefore, participants in the stress condition, but not those in the control condition, are expected to show higher levels of these dependent measures.

Question 192 of 230

Data ID: 831877

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:47 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 19-22)

Studies have shown that the availability of social support, before and after exposure to a stressor, is associated with fewer stress responses. Based on these findings, a research group hypothesized that when exposed to stressors, people will be more inclined to engage in prosocial behaviors (such as sharing and trusting) as a coping mechanism. To test this hypothesis, college-aged male participants were recruited. All the participants were given an anxiety assessment, and five participants who reported unusually high levels of anxiety were dropped. The remaining participants were randomly assigned to either the stress group or the control group. In the first phase of the experiment, the stress group was asked to study a set of slides and give a public presentation, a condition that is considered a social stressor. The control group was asked to study the same set of slides as the stress group but did not have to give a public presentation.

In the second phase of the experiment, the participants were told that they would be playing a “trust game” with another player. According to the trust game, each participant could choose between two monetary rewards: walking away with $30 or choosing to share $120 with another player. Sharing required trusting the other player because the other player would decide how the $120 would be divided. If the other player was fair, he could choose to divide the money into equal halves; in which case, the participant would get $60. If the other player wasn’t fair, he could keep the $120, and the participant would get $0. The results showed that the participants who had been in the stress condition were more likely to trust the other player than those who had been in the control condition. The researchers concluded that their findings supported the hypothesis that stress promotes prosocial behavior. However, the experiment has been criticized for not accounting for the possibility that stress might lead to an increase in risk-taking rather than an increase in prosocial behavior.

 

 

Adapted from B. von Dawans, U. Fischbacher, C. Kirschbaum, E. Fehr, & M. Heinrichs. ©2012 the Authors.

Question + Answer Choices

Which statement provides the LEAST likely explanation for why the researchers dropped the highly anxious participants from their sample?

  • A.
    Ethical committees do not allow research with participants who may have psychological disorders.
  • B.
    If all the anxious participants get assigned to the stress condition, this may pose a confounding variable.
  • C.
    Highly anxious participants are not representative of the population and may reduce the generalizability of the findings.
  • D.
    If all the anxious participants get assigned to the control condition, this may lead to a false rejection of the experimental hypothesis.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Ethical standards of research with human participants allow research to be conducted with anxious participants.
  2. One of the reasons for excluding some volunteers from participation is to prevent the possibility that more of the outliers may be assigned to one of the conditions (i.e., either the stress or control group). Such confounding may bring about errors in inference.
  3. The research question was whether exposure to stressors causes people in general to engage in prosocial behaviors. Persons who are already at unusually high levels of stress may respond differently, or perhaps not at all (ceiling effect) to the stressor manipulation used in the study. Thus, their responses may limit the generalizability of the research findings.
  4. The main reason for screening the volunteers and excluding the extremely anxious ones from participation was to prevent the possibility that more of those outliers would be assigned to one of the conditions (i.e., either the stress or control group). Such confounding could cause errors in interpreting the results.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.
