Question 193 of 230

Data ID: 831882

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:48 PM

Passage

Passage 4 (Questions 19-22)

Studies have shown that the availability of social support, before and after exposure to a stressor, is associated with fewer stress responses. Based on these findings, a research group hypothesized that when exposed to stressors, people will be more inclined to engage in prosocial behaviors (such as sharing and trusting) as a coping mechanism. To test this hypothesis, college-aged male participants were recruited. All the participants were given an anxiety assessment, and five participants who reported unusually high levels of anxiety were dropped. The remaining participants were randomly assigned to either the stress group or the control group. In the first phase of the experiment, the stress group was asked to study a set of slides and give a public presentation, a condition that is considered a social stressor. The control group was asked to study the same set of slides as the stress group but did not have to give a public presentation.

In the second phase of the experiment, the participants were told that they would be playing a “trust game” with another player. According to the trust game, each participant could choose between two monetary rewards: walking away with $30 or choosing to share $120 with another player. Sharing required trusting the other player because the other player would decide how the $120 would be divided. If the other player was fair, he could choose to divide the money into equal halves; in which case, the participant would get $60. If the other player wasn’t fair, he could keep the $120, and the participant would get $0. The results showed that the participants who had been in the stress condition were more likely to trust the other player than those who had been in the control condition. The researchers concluded that their findings supported the hypothesis that stress promotes prosocial behavior. However, the experiment has been criticized for not accounting for the possibility that stress might lead to an increase in risk-taking rather than an increase in prosocial behavior.

 

 

Adapted from B. von Dawans, U. Fischbacher, C. Kirschbaum, E. Fehr, & M. Heinrichs. ©2012 the Authors.

Question + Answer Choices

A researcher replicates the experiment with the addition of a physical stressor to the first phase of the experiment. According to Selye’s general adaptation syndrome, this change is:

  • A.
    necessary, because humans respond differently to different types of stressors.
  • B.
    necessary, because physical stressors cause avoidance–avoidance conflict, whereas social stressors cause approach–approach conflict.
  • C.
    unnecessary, because the human stress response is not specific to the type of stressor.
  • D.
    unnecessary, because both physical stressors and social stressors cause avoidance–avoidance conflict.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. According to Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), an organism's stress response always follows a similar course, regardless of the exact nature of the stressor.
  2. Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) contains no provisions about the exact nature of the stressor. In particular, no mention of whether any specific stressor causes an approach-approach conflict or an avoidance-avoidance conflict.
  3. According to Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), an organism's stress response always follows a similar course, regardless of the exact nature of the stressor.
  4. Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) makes no mention of whether any specific stressor causes an avoidance-avoidance conflict. The nature of the stressor is irrelevant.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Emotion and Stress.

Question 194 of 230

Data ID: 831898

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:48 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 23-26)

Synchronized behaviors refer to coordinated actions that are matched in time. Study 1 demonstrated that these behaviors can affect affiliation ratings. The participants’ task was to tap their fingers to a metronome in two different testing conditions: with an experimenter who tapped her finger to the same metronome (synchronous condition) or with an experimenter who tapped her finger to another metronome (asynchronous condition). Afterwards, the participants were asked to rate the degree of affiliation they felt toward the experimenter. The results showed that the participants felt more affiliated with the experimenter under the synchronous condition.

Study 2 was designed to test the hypothesis that synchronous actions make participants more prone to comply when prompted to engage in aggression. Participants were assigned to either the synchronous or asynchronous condition in groups of three. Synchrony was manipulated similarly to Study 1, by having the participants in each group finger tap to the same metronome or to different metronomes. After the finger-tapping task, each group was told that a similar experiment was going to be conducted with either music or noise in the background and that their group would pick what the participants in the other group would be exposed to. They were asked to make a selection from a list composed of ten songs and an option labeled “noise blast” that was described as “90 seconds of static played at an extremely high volume. Can be aggravating.” In the persuasion trials, one of the participants was a research confederate posing as a participant. As the group was deciding, the confederate said, “Oooh, I think we should choose the noise blast.” In the no persuasion trials, there was no confederate in the group. The results supported the researchers’ hypothesis.

 

 

Adapted from S. S. Wiltermouth ©2011 Elsevier; M. J. Hove and J. L. Risen ©2009 Social Cognition.

Question + Answer Choices

Which conclusion is best supported by the outcome of Study 2?

  • A.
    Synchronous activity increases group polarization.
  • B.
    Synchronous activity increases conformity.
  • C.
    Asynchronous activity increases cognitive dissonance.
  • D.
    Asynchronous activity increases social loafing.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The results of Study 2 were unrelated to the phenomenon of group polarization.
  2. The results of Study 2 showed that synchronous activity made participants in the persuasion trials more likely to conform to the confederate's suggestion to expose the other group to the "noise blast."
  3. No results from Study 2 were reported that were related to cognitive dissonance.
  4. The passage did not mention any results from Study 2 that were related to the phenomenon of social loafing.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on How the Presence of Others Affects Individual Behavior, Group Decision-Making Processes, Socialization.

Question 195 of 230

Data ID: 831907

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:49 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 23-26)

Synchronized behaviors refer to coordinated actions that are matched in time. Study 1 demonstrated that these behaviors can affect affiliation ratings. The participants’ task was to tap their fingers to a metronome in two different testing conditions: with an experimenter who tapped her finger to the same metronome (synchronous condition) or with an experimenter who tapped her finger to another metronome (asynchronous condition). Afterwards, the participants were asked to rate the degree of affiliation they felt toward the experimenter. The results showed that the participants felt more affiliated with the experimenter under the synchronous condition.

Study 2 was designed to test the hypothesis that synchronous actions make participants more prone to comply when prompted to engage in aggression. Participants were assigned to either the synchronous or asynchronous condition in groups of three. Synchrony was manipulated similarly to Study 1, by having the participants in each group finger tap to the same metronome or to different metronomes. After the finger-tapping task, each group was told that a similar experiment was going to be conducted with either music or noise in the background and that their group would pick what the participants in the other group would be exposed to. They were asked to make a selection from a list composed of ten songs and an option labeled “noise blast” that was described as “90 seconds of static played at an extremely high volume. Can be aggravating.” In the persuasion trials, one of the participants was a research confederate posing as a participant. As the group was deciding, the confederate said, “Oooh, I think we should choose the noise blast.” In the no persuasion trials, there was no confederate in the group. The results supported the researchers’ hypothesis.

 

 

Adapted from S. S. Wiltermouth ©2011 Elsevier; M. J. Hove and J. L. Risen ©2009 Social Cognition.

Question + Answer Choices

Damage to which nervous system structure is most likely to cause problems in the participants’ ability to perform the synchronous behaviors in studies 1 and 2?

  • A.
    Hippocampus
  • B.
    Hypothalamus
  • C.
    Cochlea
  • D.
    Cerebellum

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The primary functions of the hippocampus involve memory.
  2. The hypothalamus is primarily concerned with functions like the processing of sympathetic nervous system inputs and control of the endocrine system (i.e., through its connection to the pituitary gland).
  3. The cochlea, a structure in the inner ear, is primarily concerned with the reception of auditory sensory inputs.
  4. The coordinated finger tapping in the synchronous behavior condition is a motor task. Therefore, it most likely requires the proper functioning of the cerebellum.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 23-26)

Synchronized behaviors refer to coordinated actions that are matched in time. Study 1 demonstrated that these behaviors can affect affiliation ratings. The participants’ task was to tap their fingers to a metronome in two different testing conditions: with an experimenter who tapped her finger to the same metronome (synchronous condition) or with an experimenter who tapped her finger to another metronome (asynchronous condition). Afterwards, the participants were asked to rate the degree of affiliation they felt toward the experimenter. The results showed that the participants felt more affiliated with the experimenter under the synchronous condition.

Study 2 was designed to test the hypothesis that synchronous actions make participants more prone to comply when prompted to engage in aggression. Participants were assigned to either the synchronous or asynchronous condition in groups of three. Synchrony was manipulated similarly to Study 1, by having the participants in each group finger tap to the same metronome or to different metronomes. After the finger-tapping task, each group was told that a similar experiment was going to be conducted with either music or noise in the background and that their group would pick what the participants in the other group would be exposed to. They were asked to make a selection from a list composed of ten songs and an option labeled “noise blast” that was described as “90 seconds of static played at an extremely high volume. Can be aggravating.” In the persuasion trials, one of the participants was a research confederate posing as a participant. As the group was deciding, the confederate said, “Oooh, I think we should choose the noise blast.” In the no persuasion trials, there was no confederate in the group. The results supported the researchers’ hypothesis.

 

 

Adapted from S. S. Wiltermouth ©2011 Elsevier; M. J. Hove and J. L. Risen ©2009 Social Cognition.

Question + Answer Choices

A researcher suggests that the effect of the testing condition on the affiliation ratings is caused by increased sympathetic nervous system activity due to performing a repetitive behavior. Is this explanation likely to be supported?

  • A.
    Yes, because studies show that increased sympathetic nervous system arousal can affect how people feel about others.
  • B.
    Yes, because the design used in Study 1 allows researchers to establish the effect of sympathetic nervous system arousal on affiliation ratings.
  • C.
    No, because performing repetitive behaviors is more likely to increase parasympathetic nervous system activity.
  • D.
    No, because both of the conditions involve repetitive behaviors, but participants' ratings differ under the two conditions.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Although it is entirely plausible that increased sympathetic activity affects people's feelings towards others, the repetitive activities were performed by participants in both the synchronous and asynchronous conditions.
  2. The study's design did not allow any inferences about the potential effects of increases in sympathetic arousal.
  3. The study's design did not allow any inferences about the potential effects of increases in parasympathetic arousal. Also, it is unlikely that the repetitive behaviors would affect responses of the parasympathetic nervous system.
  4. Participants in both the synchronous and asynchronous conditions engaged in repetitive activities. Therefore, if differences in the affiliation ratings had been caused by sympathetic nervous system arousal, only the synchronous condition would have been affected.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Correct
A
B
C
D

Question 197 of 230

Data ID: 831914

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:50 PM

Passage

Passage 5 (Questions 23-26)

Synchronized behaviors refer to coordinated actions that are matched in time. Study 1 demonstrated that these behaviors can affect affiliation ratings. The participants’ task was to tap their fingers to a metronome in two different testing conditions: with an experimenter who tapped her finger to the same metronome (synchronous condition) or with an experimenter who tapped her finger to another metronome (asynchronous condition). Afterwards, the participants were asked to rate the degree of affiliation they felt toward the experimenter. The results showed that the participants felt more affiliated with the experimenter under the synchronous condition.

Study 2 was designed to test the hypothesis that synchronous actions make participants more prone to comply when prompted to engage in aggression. Participants were assigned to either the synchronous or asynchronous condition in groups of three. Synchrony was manipulated similarly to Study 1, by having the participants in each group finger tap to the same metronome or to different metronomes. After the finger-tapping task, each group was told that a similar experiment was going to be conducted with either music or noise in the background and that their group would pick what the participants in the other group would be exposed to. They were asked to make a selection from a list composed of ten songs and an option labeled “noise blast” that was described as “90 seconds of static played at an extremely high volume. Can be aggravating.” In the persuasion trials, one of the participants was a research confederate posing as a participant. As the group was deciding, the confederate said, “Oooh, I think we should choose the noise blast.” In the no persuasion trials, there was no confederate in the group. The results supported the researchers’ hypothesis.

 

 

Adapted from S. S. Wiltermouth ©2011 Elsevier; M. J. Hove and J. L. Risen ©2009 Social Cognition.

Question + Answer Choices

Based on the findings in the passage, engaging in synchronous behaviors is most likely to lead to an increase in:

  • A.
    egocentricism.
  • B.
    observational learning.
  • C.
    obedience.
  • D.
    social facilitation.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. Neither study involved the measurement of any response that would indicate whether participants were behaving in an egocentric manner.
  2. There was no modeling of behavior in either study. Therefore, no inference about observational learning is possible.
  3. Based on the finding (Study 2) that engaging in repetitive synchronous behaviors can increase compliance, it can be inferred that it may also increase levels of obedience.
  4. There was no manipulation of group size in either study, which would be needed to make any inference about social facilitation.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on How the Presence of Others Affects Individual Behavior, Group Decision-Making Processes, Socialization.

Question 198 of 230

Data ID: 831931

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:51 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which type of design is LEAST appropriate for research on residential segregation?

  • A.
    Ethnographic methods
  • B.
    Experimental methods
  • C.
    Quantitative methods
  • D.
    Survey methods

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Ethnography typically involves the systematic observation of people in their cultural settings. Sometimes ethnographic methods also include in-depth interviews as well, to better understand participants' culture. The study of residential segregation can be appropriately conducted using ethnographic methods.
  2. Experimental methods are generally not appropriate for the study of residential segregation. Experiments require random assignment to different groups (usually an intervention group and a control group). Except under specific circumstances, it would be unethical and impractical to randomly assign participants to different groups in which residential segregation varies.
  3. Quantitative methods simply refer to methods that utilize numerical data. Residential segregation can be appropriately studied using quantitative methods.
  4. Survey methods involve the use of a questionnaire in which respondents provide answers to various questions. Residential segregation can be appropriately studied using surveys.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Spatial Inequality, Social Class, Health Disparities, Healthcare Disparities.

Question 199 of 230

Data ID: 831944

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:51 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Participants in a weight-loss program agree to have their body mass index (BMI) measured to track their progress in the program. Among a sample of 72 participants, the mean BMI is 30 and the median BMI is 25. Which statement provides an accurate description of the sample?

  • A.
    More participants had a BMI over 25 than a BMI under 25.
  • B.
    The majority of participants had a BMI between 25 and 30.
  • C.
    Half of the participants had a BMI over 25 and half had a BMI under 25.
  • D.
    More outliers among the participants had a BMI under 25 than a BMI over 30.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. The median splits the sample distribution of values in half. Thus, there would not be more participants over (or under) the median BMI of 25.
  2. With the information given in the question, it cannot be determined whether a majority of participants had a BMI between 25 and 30 (to make such a determination, one would need to have some information on the variance of the sample distribution).
  3. The median represents the middle of the distribution, splitting the sample distribution of values in half. Thus, a median of 25 can be accurately described as when half of the sample has a BMI over 25 and the other half of the sample has a BMI under 25.
  4. The number of outliers cannot be determined based on the information provided in the question alone. Given that the mean is greater than the median, however, it is more likely that there would be more outliers over the mean or median values, as opposed to under them.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Theoretical Approaches, Social Institutions and Culture.

A
x

C

Question 200 of 230

Data ID: 831951

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:52 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Often utilized when studying communicable diseases, which type of analysis maps the series of relationships among a set of individuals?

  • A.
    Social support analysis
  • B.
    Social network analysis
  • C.
    Social stratification analysis
  • D.
    Social reproduction analysis

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Social support typically refers to the tangible and intangible forms of assistance that individuals receive from their social ties. Social support describes the assistance that individuals receive, rather than the series of relationships among a set of individuals.
  2. Used in some epidemiological studies, social network analysis is the method described in the question. Network analysis involves mapping the social relationships that exist among a set of individuals.
  3. Social stratification refers to the hierarchy of social positions in a society (often according to either social class or social status). Stratification does not describe the relationship among individuals but rather the relationship among social positions in a society.
  4. Social reproduction refers to the perpetuation of inequality through social institutions. Because of the focus on institutions, social reproduction would not apply to relationships among a set of individuals.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Elements of Social Interaction; Self-presentation and Interacting with Others; and Discrimination.

Question 201 of 230

Data ID: 831957

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:53 PM

Question + Answer Choices

Which effect is LEAST likely to occur with the process of gentrification?

  • A.
    Development of affordable housing
  • B.
    Increased neighborhood stratification
  • C.
    Displacement of lower-income residents
  • D.
    Expanded tax base for local government

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Gentrification describes the process in which relatively affluent individuals move into a neighborhood that recently consisted of residents with moderate to low income. With the arrival of more affluent residents, housing demand increases and often leads to a decrease in affordable housing available to lower income residents. In contrast, the other options are more likely to occur with the process of gentrification.
  2. Given the increase in more affluent residents that occurs from gentrification, there would likely be an increase in neighborhood stratification (meaning a larger difference in the social class or status positions across the residents in the neighborhood).
  3. Due to the influx of more affluent residents, and the increased demand for housing that then raises prices, gentrification often leads to the displacement of lower-income residents who can no longer afford to live in the neighborhood.
  4. The increase in more affluent residents that occurs with gentrification is likely to increase the tax base for the local government (given the increase in real estate prices, which would yield increased tax revenue).

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Demographic Structure of Society, Demographic Shifts and Social Change.

Question 202 of 230

Data ID: 831973

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:53 PM

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 31-34)

A study was designed to examine the role of peer pressure on risky behaviors across different age groups. The participants were post-pubescent adolescents, young adults, and adults. The majority of the participants were White. The measures were a decision-making questionnaire (DMQ) and a computer game (CG). The DMQ presented the participants with hypothetical dilemmas that involved risky decisions and asked them what they would do if they were faced with these dilemmas. In the CG, the player watched an animated car move toward a stoplight that was yellow. The player was instructed to press a button to stop the car before the light turned red. The objective of the game was for the car to advance as much as possible without running a red light. For both the DMQ and the CG, higher scores indicated riskier behavior.

The participants completed the measures under one of two testing conditions: alone or in a group of three. For the DMQ, the participants in the group condition were encouraged to discuss the scenarios with each other. In the CG, the participants in the group condition took turns playing. The participants who were not playing at a given time were told to offer the player advice. The young adults and the adults came to the experiments in groups of three friends. The adolescents were assigned to groups of three by the experimenters. All of the groups were composed of same-sex participants.

The results showed a negative correlation between age and scores on the DMQ. The effect of testing condition on risky behavior scores from the CG is shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Risky behavior by age group and testing condition
(Note: An asterisk (*) indicates that the difference between the testing conditions was statistically significant.)

 

 

Adapted from M. Gardner and L. Steinberg. ©2005 the American Psychological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

On average, adults are found to be more risk averse than adolescents. Based on group polarization, how would testing condition affect adults’ scores on the DMQ? Adults would have:

  • A.
    lower scores on the DMQ in a group than alone.
  • B.
    higher scores on the DMQ in a group than alone.
  • C.
    lower scores on the DMQ in small groups but higher scores on the DMQ in large groups.
  • D.
    higher scores on the DMQ in small groups but lower scores on the DMQ in large groups.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. After interacting with one another, a group of risk averse individuals will most likely become more risk averse. The DMQ (Decision Making Questionnaire) scores will most likely be lower after a group discussion, indicating a higher level of risk aversion. This prediction is based on the phenomenon of group polarization, in which people's attitudes become more extreme after they discuss the attitude object with like-minded individuals. Thus, their scores will most likely indicate a greater risk aversion than participants who were tested alone.
  2. After interacting with one another, a group of risk averse individuals will most likely become more risk averse. Their DMQ (Decision Making Questionnaire) scores will most likely be lower than the DMQ scores of the participants who are tested individually.
  3. Group size is expected to influence the results. Thus, the effect of group polarization for adult participants will be greater (i.e., lower DMQ scores, indicating greater risk aversion) in the group condition.
  4. Group size is expected to influence the results. There will still be evidence of the group polarization effect (i.e., low DMQ scores, indicating greater risk aversion) in adults with any group size. However, participants were studied either alone or with two friends, and no social influence is expected in the alone condition.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on How the Presence of Others Affects Individual Behavior, Group Decision-Making Processes, Socialization.

✓

Question 203 of 230

Data ID: 831980

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:54 PM

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 31-34)

A study was designed to examine the role of peer pressure on risky behaviors across different age groups. The participants were post-pubescent adolescents, young adults, and adults. The majority of the participants were White. The measures were a decision-making questionnaire (DMQ) and a computer game (CG). The DMQ presented the participants with hypothetical dilemmas that involved risky decisions and asked them what they would do if they were faced with these dilemmas. In the CG, the player watched an animated car move toward a stoplight that was yellow. The player was instructed to press a button to stop the car before the light turned red. The objective of the game was for the car to advance as much as possible without running a red light. For both the DMQ and the CG, higher scores indicated riskier behavior.

The participants completed the measures under one of two testing conditions: alone or in a group of three. For the DMQ, the participants in the group condition were encouraged to discuss the scenarios with each other. In the CG, the participants in the group condition took turns playing. The participants who were not playing at a given time were told to offer the player advice. The young adults and the adults came to the experiments in groups of three friends. The adolescents were assigned to groups of three by the experimenters. All of the groups were composed of same-sex participants.

The results showed a negative correlation between age and scores on the DMQ. The effect of testing condition on risky behavior scores from the CG is shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Risky behavior by age group and testing condition
(Note: An asterisk (*) indicates that the difference between the testing conditions was statistically significant.)

 

 

Adapted from M. Gardner and L. Steinberg. ©2005 the American Psychological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

Which statement best applies the behaviorist theory to explain the association between age and DMQ scores? Compared to younger participants, older participants:

  • A.
    have had more experiences in which they have been punished for risky behaviors.
  • B.
    are less prone to the effects of peer pressure.
  • C.
    have a more mature prefrontal cortex that inhibits impulsive behavior.
  • D.
    have a more developed superego that inhibits the impulses of the id.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Older participants most likely have had greater levels of experience with their risky decisions leading to aversive consequences. Thus, their risky behavior has been punished more often than that of younger participants.
  2. Although the statement is accurate, peer pressure, a form of normative social influence, is not a concept used in behaviorist theory.
  3. Although the statement is accurate, behaviorist theory does not explain behavioral variations in terms of neural mechanisms.
  4. Superego and id are constructs used in psychoanalytic theory, which is antithetical to behaviorist theory.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Habituation and Dishabituation, Associative Learning, Observational Learning and Theories of Attitude and Behavior Change.

Question 204 of 230

Data ID: 831985

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:54 PM

Passage

Passage 6 (Questions 31-34)

A study was designed to examine the role of peer pressure on risky behaviors across different age groups. The participants were post-pubescent adolescents, young adults, and adults. The majority of the participants were White. The measures were a decision-making questionnaire (DMQ) and a computer game (CG). The DMQ presented the participants with hypothetical dilemmas that involved risky decisions and asked them what they would do if they were faced with these dilemmas. In the CG, the player watched an animated car move toward a stoplight that was yellow. The player was instructed to press a button to stop the car before the light turned red. The objective of the game was for the car to advance as much as possible without running a red light. For both the DMQ and the CG, higher scores indicated riskier behavior.

The participants completed the measures under one of two testing conditions: alone or in a group of three. For the DMQ, the participants in the group condition were encouraged to discuss the scenarios with each other. In the CG, the participants in the group condition took turns playing. The participants who were not playing at a given time were told to offer the player advice. The young adults and the adults came to the experiments in groups of three friends. The adolescents were assigned to groups of three by the experimenters. All of the groups were composed of same-sex participants.

The results showed a negative correlation between age and scores on the DMQ. The effect of testing condition on risky behavior scores from the CG is shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1 Risky behavior by age group and testing condition
(Note: An asterisk (*) indicates that the difference between the testing conditions was statistically significant.)

 

 

Adapted from M. Gardner and L. Steinberg. ©2005 the American Psychological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

Which finding would support the validity of the DMQ measure?

  • A.
    A positive correlation between the DMQ scores and the CG scores
  • B.
    A negative correlation between the DMQ scores and the CG scores
  • C.
    A positive correlation between participants' scores on two different administrations of the DMQ
  • D.
    A negative correlation between participants' scores on two different administrations of the DMQ

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Both the DMQ and the CG were designed to assess risky behavior. Lower scores indicated greater risk aversion on both measures. Therefore, a positive correlation between the DMQ scores and the CG scores can be interpreted as supporting their validity (i.e., that both measure what they are intended to measure).
  2. Both the DMQ and the CG were designed to assess the same tendency toward engaging in risky behavior. Thus, a negative correlation between these measures would be a contraindication of the validity of either or both of them.
  3. The consistency of scores on the DMQ would indicate reliability, but not the validity of the assessment.
  4. Although it would not really tell us anything about the validity of the assessment, such inconsistency of scores on the DMQ would indicate that this assessment lacked reliability, which is normally regarded as a necessary condition for validity.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on How the Presence of Others Affects Individual Behavior, Group Decision-Making Processes, Socialization.

Passage 6 (Questions 31-34)

A study was designed to examine the role of peer pressure on risky behaviors across different age groups. The participants were post-pubescent adolescents, young adults, and adults. The majority of the participants were White. The measures were a decision-making questionnaire (DMQ) and a computer game (CG). The DMQ presented the participants with hypothetical dilemmas that involved risky decisions and asked them what they would do if they were faced with these dilemmas. In the CG, the player watched an animated car move toward a stoplight that was yellow. The player was instructed to press a button to stop the car before the light turned red. The objective of the game was for the car to advance as much as possible without running a red light. For both the DMQ and the CG, higher scores indicated riskier behavior.

The participants completed the measures under one of two testing conditions: alone or in a group of three. For the DMQ, the participants in the group condition were encouraged to discuss the scenarios with each other. In the CG, the participants in the group condition took turns playing. The participants who were not playing at a given time were told to offer the player advice. The young adults and the adults came to the experiments in groups of three friends. Figure 1 Risky behavior by age group and testing condition
(Note: An asterisk (*) indicates that the difference between the testing conditions was statistically significant.)

 

 

Adapted from M. Gardner and L. Steinberg. ©2005 the American Psychological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

According to Erikson's theory of psychosocial development, which group of participants are most likely dealing with the challenges of generativity versus stagnation?

  1. Adolescents
  2. Young adults
  3. Adults
  • A.
    I only
  • B.
    III only
  • C.
    I and II only
  • D.
    II and III only

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Adolescents have not yet reached the age at which the major crisis is that of generativity versus stagnation.
  2. In Erikson's theory, the major crisis facing adults is that of generativity versus stagnation.
  3. Neither adolescents nor young adults have reached the stage at which the major life crisis is that of generativity versus stagnation.
  4. In Erikson's theory, the major crisis facing adults is that of generativity versus stagnation. Young adults have not yet reached the age at which this is the major life crisis.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Self-Concept, Self-Identity, and Social Identity.

Question 206 of 230

Data ID: 832000

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:56 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 35-38)

Studies have found that being bilingual is associated with better self-rated physical and mental health among immigrants in the United States. Research has also found that, on average, assimilation is associated with worse health. Thus, the processes behind both bilingualism and assimilation raise the question of how social integration influences immigrant health.

Using data from the National Latino and Asian American Analysis Project, researchers designed a study to investigate the relationship between language and self-rated health. The measures of language included English and native language proficiency, as well as frequency of English and native language use. The immigrant groups included in the sample were Asian and Latino immigrants.

Based on a national area probability sample, the researchers oversampled geographical areas that have a moderate-to-high density of Latinos and Asian Americans. Bilingual interviewers asked the respondents to rate their overall physical health status and overall mental health status on a 5-point scale (with 1 being poor health and 5 being excellent health). The researchers’ statistical models considered multiple variables that may affect the relationship between language and health. These control variables included socioeconomic status (SES), assimilation, family and social support, stress and discrimination, and healthcare access and health behaviors.

Results showed strong associations between bilingualism and both physical and mental self-rated health for Latino and Asian immigrants. These associations are partially explained by SES and family and social support, but the researchers did not find evidence that assimilation, stress and discrimination, or healthcare access and health behaviors affected the language–health relationship. Based on their findings, the researchers suggested that future studies should consider how the unique cultural, linguistic, and cognitive skills of bilingual immigrants might shape their physical and mental health statuses.

Adapted from A. Schachter, R. T. Kimbro, and B. K. Gorman, “Language Proficiency and Health Status: Are Bilingual Immigrants Healthier?” ©2012 American Sociological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

Which statement does NOT identify an aspect of the concept of assimilation?

  • A.
    Assimilation is the influence that cultural changes have on an individual’s health.
  • B.
    Assimilation is the process of cultural adaptation that results from geographic mobility.
  • C.
    Assimilation occurs when individuals adopt the cultural norms of a dominant culture.
  • D.
    Assimilation occurs when individuals relinquish the cultural norms of their childhood.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Assimilation refers to the process in which new members of a society adopt the norms and values of the dominant culture. Each of the other options provides an element of assimilation (cultural adaptation, adopting new norms, and relinquishing old norms). However, this option does NOT describe an aspect of assimilation itself, but instead speculates about a possible consequence of assimilation.
  2. Assimilation is often used in the context of migration (movement both within a nation and across nations), describing the process of cultural adaptation that occurs as a result of moving to a different location.
  3. A central tenet of assimilation is that individuals will adopt the norms and values of a new culture.
  4. Assimilation usually presumes that individuals will not retain the norms that they previously held when they were younger. The assimilation perspective has been criticized for this assumption of relinquishing norms.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Theoretical Approaches, Social Institutions and Culture.

Correct
A
✓
B
C
D

Question 207 of 230

Data ID: 832003

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:56 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 35-38)

Studies have found that being bilingual is associated with better self-rated physical and mental health among immigrants in the United States. Research has also found that, on average, assimilation is associated with worse health. Thus, the processes behind both bilingualism and assimilation raise the question of how social integration influences immigrant health.

Using data from the National Latino and Asian American Analysis Project, researchers designed a study to investigate the relationship between language and self-rated health. The measures of language included English and native language proficiency, as well as frequency of English and native language use. The immigrant groups included in the sample were Asian and Latino immigrants.

Based on a national area probability sample, the researchers oversampled geographical areas that have a moderate-to-high density of Latinos and Asian Americans. Bilingual interviewers asked the respondents to rate their overall physical health status and overall mental health status on a 5-point scale (with 1 being poor health and 5 being excellent health). The researchers’ statistical models considered multiple variables that may affect the relationship between language and health. These control variables included socioeconomic status (SES), assimilation, family and social support, stress and discrimination, and healthcare access and health behaviors.

Results showed strong associations between bilingualism and both physical and mental self-rated health for Latino and Asian immigrants. These associations are partially explained by SES and family and social support, but the researchers did not find evidence that assimilation, stress and discrimination, or healthcare access and health behaviors affected the language–health relationship. Based on their findings, the researchers suggested that future studies should consider how the unique cultural, linguistic, and cognitive skills of bilingual immigrants might shape their physical and mental health statuses.

Adapted from A. Schachter, R. T. Kimbro, and B. K. Gorman, “Language Proficiency and Health Status: Are Bilingual Immigrants Healthier?” ©2012 American Sociological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

A researcher suggests that the benefits of bilingualism are related to the idea that the structure of language affects the perceptions of its speakers. This researcher is referring to which concept?

  • A.
    Weber’s Law
  • B.
    The nativist hypothesis
  • C.
    Schacter–Singer theory
  • D.
    Linguistic relativity

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. Weber's Law is the name given to the robust psychophysical observation that the just noticeable difference (JND) between two physical stimuli is a constant proportion (i.e., Weber's constant) of the original (or standard) stimulus. It is unrelated to the relationship between language and cognition.
  2. The nativist hypothesis is concerned with the acquisition of a child's first language, and not with the relationship between language and cognition.
  3. The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory is concerned with explaining the relation between emotions and cognitive awareness of one's emotional state. It is unrelated to language and cognition.
  4. Linguistic relativity, which represents the weak version of the Whorf-Sapir hypothesis, is the only term related to the idea referenced in the question.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Result / Timing / Tags

Correct
A
B
C
D
✓

Question 208 of 230

Data ID: 832007

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:57 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 35-38)

Studies have found that being bilingual is associated with better self-rated physical and mental health among immigrants in the United States. Research has also found that, on average, assimilation is associated with worse health. Thus, the processes behind both bilingualism and assimilation raise the question of how social integration influences immigrant health.

Using data from the National Latino and Asian American Analysis Project, researchers designed a study to investigate the relationship between language and self-rated health. The measures of language included English and native language proficiency, as well as frequency of English and native language use. The immigrant groups included in the sample were Asian and Latino immigrants.

Based on a national area probability sample, the researchers oversampled geographical areas that have a moderate-to-high density of Latinos and Asian Americans. Bilingual interviewers asked the respondents to rate their overall physical health status and overall mental health status on a 5-point scale (with 1 being poor health and 5 being excellent health). The researchers’ statistical models considered multiple variables that may affect the relationship between language and health. These control variables included socioeconomic status (SES), assimilation, family and social support, stress and discrimination, and healthcare access and health behaviors.

Results showed strong associations between bilingualism and both physical and mental self-rated health for Latino and Asian immigrants. These associations are partially explained by SES and family and social support, but the researchers did not find evidence that assimilation, stress and discrimination, or healthcare access and health behaviors affected the language–health relationship. Based on their findings, the researchers suggested that future studies should consider how the unique cultural, linguistic, and cognitive skills of bilingual immigrants might shape their physical and mental health statuses.

Adapted from A. Schachter, R. T. Kimbro, and B. K. Gorman, “Language Proficiency and Health Status: Are Bilingual Immigrants Healthier?” ©2012 American Sociological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

The study found that Mexican immigrants reported worse physical and mental health statuses than the other Latino immigrant groups. Based on other results reported in the passage, which statement provides the best explanation of this finding?

  • A.
    Other Latino immigrants have higher average SES than Mexican immigrants.
  • B.
    Mexican immigrants have more extensive social support networks than other Latino immigrants.
  • C.
    Mexican immigrants report greater levels of assimilation than other Latino immigrants.
  • D.
    Other Latino immigrants report higher stress levels than Mexican immigrants.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. The results in the passage suggest that the relationship between language and health is partly explained by socioeconomic status (SES), along with family and social support. If the average SES of other Latino immigrant groups is higher than that of Mexican immigrants, then average differences in SES across racial/ethnic groups (or sub-groups) could help explain the observed differences in health. Thus, SES could be an explanatory factor for the reported health status differentials across Latino immigrant groups.
  2. Social support is generally associated with better health, and thus it would not be likely to explain the difference in health status referenced in the question.
  3. The passage reports that the researchers did not find evidence that assimilation affected the health-language relationship. It can be inferred from this result that assimilation alone, without any other factors, is unlikely to explain the reported difference in health status among Latino immigrant groups.
  4. Although high stress levels are generally associated with worse health status, this option does not follow the finding in the question. To provide a possible explanation, the groups would have to be reversed in this option (with Mexican immigrants reporting higher stress levels than the other two groups).

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Demographic Structure of Society, Demographic Shifts and Social Change.

Result / Timing / Tags

Correct
A
✓
B
C
D

FC9

Question 209 of 230

Data ID: 832020

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:57 PM

Passage

Passage 7 (Questions 35-38)

Studies have found that being bilingual is associated with better self-rated physical and mental health among immigrants in the United States. Research has also found that, on average, assimilation is associated with worse health. Thus, the processes behind both bilingualism and assimilation raise the question of how social integration influences immigrant health.

Using data from the National Latino and Asian American Analysis Project, researchers designed a study to investigate the relationship between language and self-rated health. The measures of language included English and native language proficiency, as well as frequency of English and native language use. The immigrant groups included in the sample were Asian and Latino immigrants.

Based on a national area probability sample, the researchers oversampled geographical areas that have a moderate-to-high density of Latinos and Asian Americans. Bilingual interviewers asked the respondents to rate their overall physical health status and overall mental health status on a 5-point scale (with 1 being poor health and 5 being excellent health). The researchers’ statistical models considered multiple variables that may affect the relationship between language and health. These control variables included socioeconomic status (SES), assimilation, family and social support, stress and discrimination, and healthcare access and health behaviors.

Results showed strong associations between bilingualism and both physical and mental self-rated health for Latino and Asian immigrants. These associations are partially explained by SES and family and social support, but the researchers did not find evidence that assimilation, stress and discrimination, or healthcare access and health behaviors affected the language–health relationship. Based on their findings, the researchers suggested that future studies should consider how the unique cultural, linguistic, and cognitive skills of bilingual immigrants might shape their physical and mental health statuses.

Adapted from A. Schachter, R. T. Kimbro, and B. K. Gorman, “Language Proficiency and Health Status: Are Bilingual Immigrants Healthier?” ©2012 American Sociological Association.

Question + Answer Choices

Sociologists describe the health benefits of social support as resulting from all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A.
    friends and family members that help patients adhere to medical treatment.
  • B.
    friends and family members that help patients to reduce harmful behaviors.
  • C.
    social relationships that help individuals to cope with stressful events.
  • D.
    social activities that help individuals to avoid loneliness and boredom.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. This option refers to help with treatment adherence from family and friends, which would reflect a health benefit of social support.
  2. Some social ties can help reduce harmful behaviors, which would reflect a health benefit of social support.
  3. Social relationships that help an individual cope with stress would indicate a benefit of social support.
  4. Social support refers to social network ties (friends, family, and other relationships) that provide an individual with various types of assistance, some of which can improve health or reduce harms. All of the response options except for this one address potential health benefits of social support. This option refers to social activities rather than social relationships (or social ties) and thus does not identify social support.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Elements of Social Interaction; Self-presentation and Interacting with Others; and Discrimination.

D

Question 210 of 230

Data ID: 832027

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:58 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

If adults performed the block tapping task as a measure of working memory capacity, they would most likely replicate sequences of what length correctly?

  • A.
    9 plus or minus 1
  • B.
    7 plus or minus 2
  • C.
    10 plus or minus 1
  • D.
    16 plus or minus 2

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. The capacity of STM was believed to be lower, approximately between 5 and 9 items (plus or minus 2).
  2. Based on early research into the capacity of the short-term memory store, the belief was that STM capacity lies between 5 and 9 items. George Miller (1956) named this idea the "Magical Number 7 + 2".
  3. The range in this option is too high. Based on early research into the capacity of the short-term memory store, the belief was that STM capacity lies between 5 and 9 items.
  4. Based on early research into the capacity of the short-term memory store, the belief was that STM capacity lies between 5 and 9 items. The range in this option is much too high.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Incorrect
A
B
C
x

Question 211 of 230

Data ID: 832032

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:59 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Based on the study, which assessment represents a subjective measure of motivation?

  • A.
    The number of grid sequences participants attempted to solve on the computer task
  • B.
    The participants’ decision to continue playing the game or stop to read a magazine
  • C.
    Participants’ answers on the exit questionnaire
  • D.
    Participants’ time spent not playing the game using the computer mouse

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is C.

  1. This was an objective measure, which more directly measured motivation.
  2. The duration of time playing the game was an objective measure, which more directly measured motivation.
  3. Answers participants gave on their exit questionnaires were the only subjective measures obtained in the study. They provided an indirect measure of each participant's motivation.
  4. This duration was another objective measure, which was a direct measure of motivation.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 212 of 230

Data ID: 832040

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:41:59 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Based on the study, which extrinsic factor most likely accounts for the motivational differences between groups?

  • A.
    Rewards and losses tied to memory performance for the gaming group
  • B.
    The presence of animated characters in the visual display for the gaming group
  • C.
    The opportunity to stop playing the game for the gaming and non-gaming groups
  • D.
    The opportunity to state they disliked the game for the gaming and non-gaming groups

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. In the gaming condition, each correct response strengthened the participant and weakened the opponent, and each incorrect response weakened the participant and strengthened the opponent.
  2. The presence or absence of the animated figures in the gaming condition was not made contingent on a participant's responses. Thus, it was not an extrinsic motivational factor.
  3. The decision to stop playing represents an intrinsic, rather than an extrinsic, factor.
  4. This opportunity represents an intrinsic factor, rather than an extrinsic factor, related to their motivation.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Biological Bases of Behavior; Human Development; Behavior and Genetics; Personality; Psychological Disorders; and Motivation, Attitudes.

Question 213 of 230

Data ID: 832046

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:42:00 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Suppose squares 1, 2, 3, and 4, moving from left to right at the top of the grid light up and some participants think “top row lights up first,” to remember the sequence. Which process is most likely being used to enhance working memory capacity?

  • A.
    Divided attention
  • B.
    Priming
  • C.
    Automatic processing
  • D.
    Chunking

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is D.

  1. The participants are engaging in selective attention, rather than divided attention.
  2. No evidence of priming has been presented.
  3. The selective attention described in the stem requires some amount of effort, which rules out the possibility that the sequence has been processed automatically.
  4. The participants are engaging in chunking, in that a sequence of lights is combined into a single unit, which is easier to recognize.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 214 of 230

Data ID: 832051

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:42:00 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Which type of memory would most likely be activated first during the computer task, before working memory is activated?

  • A.
    Implicit memory
  • B.
    Sensory memory
  • C.
    Short-term memory
  • D.
    Episodic memory

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Implicit memory is assessed using indirect measures, none of which involves working memory.
  2. In the Atkinson-Shiffrin multistore memory model, the initial registration of the information is briefly processed in a form of sensory memory, which is extremely brief in duration.
  3. Prior to the creation of Baddeley's working memory (WM) model, the Atkinson-Shiffrin multistore memory model included a short-term store, which came to be termed short-term memory (STM).
  4. Episodic memory refers to long-term memory representations of events experienced by the participant. In this study, participants were not required (and made no attempt) to retain any information about the game.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 215 of 230

Data ID: 832060

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:42:01 PM

Passage

Passage 8 (Questions 39-44)

Researchers examined whether adding gaming elements to a computerized working memory task for children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) was beneficial. Fifty-one children between 7 and 12 years of age with ADHD were randomly assigned to a computer task with either a non-gaming or gaming format.

In the non-gaming condition, squares on a four-by-four grid lit up in random order on a screen. Participants were asked to replicate the pattern by clicking their computer mouse on the squares in the same sequence. Trial difficulty was adjusted depending on participants’ performance.

In the gaming condition, animated figures were added. Points were earned by responding correctly, which strengthened participants’ efforts and weakened animated opponents. Points were lost by responding incorrectly, strengthening the opponents’ power. Trials of varying difficulty were presented randomly to participants. The training session for each condition lasted 35 minutes. After 15 minutes, participants were given a choice to continue playing the game or read a magazine.

Data were collected once weekly for three weeks. Participants’ working memory capacity for visual–spatial information was assessed with a block-tapping task. A researcher tapped 3-dimensional blocks in a specific sequence that participants were asked to replicate.

Motivation was assessed in three ways. First, the computer tracked how many grid sequences the participants attempted to solve. Second, absence time was recorded when each participant was not interacting with the mouse. Third, participants answered an exit questionnaire asking how much they liked the task and whether they would like to have it at home.

Results indicated that participants in the gaming condition spent more time playing the game, attempted to replicate more sequences, and made fewer errors. These participants also earned higher scores on the block-tapping task compared to children in the non-gaming condition. The same participants were also more likely to report liking the game and wanting a version to play at home.

 

 

Adapted from P. J. M. Prins et al., ©2011 Mary Ann Libert, Inc.

Question + Answer Choices

Based on the findings in the study, which conclusion about ADHD is most likely?

  • A.
    Immediate performance feedback reduces impulsive responding in children with ADHD.
  • B.
    Exposure to a gaming element increases impulsive responding in children with ADHD.
  • C.
    Gaming elements on working memory tasks place high demands on divided attention and inhibit focus.
  • D.
    Low levels of intrinsic motivation in children with ADHD are minimally enhanced with external reinforcers.

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is A.

  1. Children in the gaming condition received immediate feedback after making each of their responses.
  2. Children in the gaming condition attempted more sequences and made fewer errors. Therefore, they were less impulsive not more) than children in the non-gaming condition.
  3. There were no demands of divided attention in the game.
  4. External reinforcers are relevant to extrinsic (not intrinsic) motivation.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Attention; Cognition; Consciousness; Memory; and Language.

Question 216 of 230

Data ID: 832071

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:42:02 PM

Question + Answer Choices

A researcher wants to gain insight into how a social movement organization presents its beliefs to the general public in an effort to attract new members. Which methodological approach would be most appropriate for this study?

  • A.
    Observing meetings about the organization’s marketing budget
  • B.
    Conducting a content analysis of the organization’s website
  • C.
    Surveying organization members about their beliefs
  • D.
    Randomly sampling the general population about social movement organizations

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. Internal meetings about the organization's budget would not specifically indicate how the organization presents its beliefs to the general public.
  2. Conducting content analysis of online information would provide data on how the social movement organization frames issues to the broader public. Content analysis is a sociological method that is used to make inferences about a group's communication. As applied to studying a website, content analysis could help determine which beliefs the organization emphasizes to the general public.
  3. Although this option could provide some insights into the organization's beliefs, it would more precisely yield data on the organization's members and their specific beliefs. It would not be the best approach for understanding how the organization presents its beliefs to the general public for attracting new members.
  4. A random sample of the general public would only provide data on how the public perceives the organizations, but it would not provide data on how the social movement organization presents its beliefs to the general public (for attracting new members).

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Demographic Structure of Society, Demographic Shifts and Social Change.

Correct
A
B
✓

Question 217 of 230

Data ID: 832077

Captured: 6/3/2026, 4:42:02 PM

Question + Answer Choices

An immigrant teen stops participating in the ethnic customs of his family and instead identifies with the dominant culture of the new country by dressing, speaking, and acting like teens from that culture.  In this scenario, the teens of the new country’s dominant culture become which type of group for the teen?

  • A.
    Assimilated group
  • B.
    Reference group
  • C.
    Majority group
  • D.
    Peer group

Solution

Solution: The correct answer is B.

  1. An assimilated group would refer to a group that adopts the norms and values of a new culture. However, the question asks for the type of group that is represented by the dominant culture.
  2. Individuals often emulate the attitudes and behaviors of groups that they would like to join. Sociologists refer to these groups as reference groups, which are important for self-evaluation and identity formation. The scenario in the question suggests that the teen constructs an identity, through modifying behaviors (dressing, speaking, acting), to match that of teens from the dominant culture. In the context of the specific information provided in the question, the dominant culture is closest to a reference group for the teen.
  3. Although the dominant culture may reflect that of a majority group, the question does not provide information on majority and minority group dynamics in order to make a determination that the dominant culture is also the same as a majority group.
  4. A peer group is made up of individuals in a similar social position or social role. Because the immigrant teen is trying to emulate teens from the new country's culture, it is better described as a reference group (group used for evaluation) rather than a peer group.

Khan Academy Lessons

For more help on the skills, concepts, or content assessed by this question, see the Khan Academy lessons on Self-Concept, Self-Identity, and Social Identity, Formation of Identity.

Result / Timing / Tags

Correct
A
B
✓

41 secs
Difficulty Level:
